topics.title: History, Applications, and Importance of Refrigeration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** History, Applications, and Importance of Refrigeration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Refrigeration** is a branch of engineering science that deals with the **production and maintenance of temperatures below that of the surrounding atmosphere**. It involves the controlled removal of heat from a body or space (called the *refrigerated space*) and its rejection to a higher-temperature sink, using a working fluid called a **refrigerant**.

In strict thermodynamic terms, refrigeration is the process of transferring heat from a **low-temperature reservoir** to a **high-temperature reservoir** by the expenditure of external work, which is the exact reverse of a heat engine cycle (this is a direct statement of the **Clausius Statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics**).

**Standard Metrics Used in Refrigeration (KTU Highlighted):**

- **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW** of heat removal rate.
- **1 kCal = 4.186 kJ** (SI conversion factor for thermal energy).
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a refrigerator is dimensionless.
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** Often $0^\circ\text{C}$ inside and $35^\circ\text{C}$ outside for domestic AC testing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of refrigeration like a water pump, but for heat.**
> Water naturally flows downhill (from high to low elevation). A water pump does *work* to push water *uphill* against this natural flow.
> Similarly, **heat naturally flows from hot to cold** (Second Law). A refrigerator does *work* (via compressor) to push heat *from a cold space to a warm surrounding*, which is "uphill" for heat.
> The "water" in this analogy is the **refrigerant**, and the "pump" is the **compressor**.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

This introductory module forms the **conceptual foundation** for every later module (vapour compression, absorption, cryogenics, cold storage design). KTU examiners frequently ask **definition-based 3-mark questions** and **historical milestone 7-mark questions** in Part A and Part B respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Historical Evolution — Structured Timeline

The development of refrigeration can be categorized into four distinct eras. Understanding this timeline is **high-yield** for KTU Part A and Part B questions.

#### 2.1.1 Era of Natural Ice (Pre-1800s)
- **Ancient China & India (1000 BC)**: Used natural ice harvested from mountains and stored in insulated pits.
- **Romans & Egyptians**: Mixed snow with saltpeter ($KNO_3$) to create *endothermic cooling* — a primitive chemical refrigeration.

#### 2.1.2 Era of Mechanical Vapour Compression (1834–1900)
- **1834 — Jacob Perkins**: Patented the first *closed-loop vapour compression refrigeration system* using **diethyl ether** as the refrigerant.
- **1859 — Ferdinand Carré**: Developed the first **absorption refrigeration system** using **ammonia-water**.
- **1876 — Carl von Linde**: Built the first practical and industrially scaled **ammonia compression system** for brewing and food preservation.
- **1880s — Thaddeus Lowe**: Introduced **carbon dioxide ($CO_2$)** as a refrigerant for marine use.

#### 2.1.3 Era of Synthetic Refrigerants (1930s–1990s)
- **1930s — Thomas Midgley Jr.**: Synthesized **CFC-12 (R-12, dichlorodifluoromethane)** and later **HFC-134a** precursors.
- **1974 — Molina & Rowland**: Published the landmark paper linking **CFCs to Ozone Layer Depletion**, leading to the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**.
- **1987 Onwards**: Phasing out of CFCs, introduction of **HCFCs (R-22)** as transitional refrigerants, and development of **HFCs and natural refrigerants (NH₃, CO₂, Hydrocarbons)**.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Point:** The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** is considered the most successful international environmental treaty and is a guaranteed high-weightage topic in KTU 2024 exams.

#### 2.1.4 Era of Energy Efficiency & Eco-Refrigeration (2000s–Present)
- Focus shifted to **Low-GWP (Global Warming Potential)** and **Zero-ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** refrigerants.
- Introduction of **R-1234yf**, **R-290 (propane)**, and **CO₂ (R-744)** in transcritical cycles.
- Integration of **solar refrigeration** and **magnetocaloric** cooling for sustainable applications.

### 2.2 Classification of Refrigeration Systems (Logical Steps)

The 'How' behind modern refrigeration can be broken into the following classification steps:

1. **By Principle of Operation**
   - **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** — uses mechanical work from a compressor.
   - **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** — uses thermal energy (heat input).
   - **Gas Cycle Refrigeration** — uses reverse Stirling and Bell-Coleman cycles.
   - **Steam Jet Refrigeration** — uses high-pressure steam.

2. **By Temperature Range**
   - **Chilled Water / Air Conditioning:** $5^\circ\text{C}$ to $15^\circ\text{C}$
   - **Commercial Refrigeration:** $-30^\circ\text{C}$ to $5^\circ\text{C}$
   - **Industrial / Frozen Food:** $-40^\circ\text{C}$ to $-30^\circ\text{C}$
   - **Cryogenics / LNG / Medical:** Below $-150^\circ\text{C}$

3. **By Refrigerant Type**
   - **Halocarbons (CFC, HCFC, HFC, HFO)**
   - **Natural Refrigerants (NH₃, CO₂, Water, Air, Hydrocarbons)**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **KTU Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect | $RE$ or $Q_L$ | $\dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW or kJ/s | Cooling capacity at evaporator |
| Heat of Compression | $W_c$ | $\dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Compressor power input |
| Heat Rejected at Condenser | $Q_H$ | $\dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | Condenser duty |
| Coefficient of Performance | $COP$ | $\dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | Dimensionless | Refrigerator efficiency |
| Carnot COP (Reversible) | $COP_{Carnot}$ | $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Upper theoretical limit |
| Energy Efficiency Ratio | $EER$ | $\dfrac{\text{Cooling (BTU/hr)}}{W_{in}}$ | BTU/Wh | AC rating standard |
| 1 Ton of Refrigeration | $1\ TR$ | $3.517\ \text{kW} = 211\ \text{kJ/min}$ | kW | Capacity benchmarking |
| GWP (relative to $CO_2$) | $GWP$ | $\dfrac{\text{IR of gas}}{\text{IR of } CO_2}$ | Ratio | Environmental impact |

> [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for Tables:** All temperature values $T_L$ and $T_H$ in the COP formula **must be in Kelvin (K)**, NOT Celsius. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this unit conversion error.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Refrigeration is not an isolated subject — it is the **backbone of modern civilization**:

- **Food Preservation:** Reduces spoilage; global cold chain is worth over $200 billion.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Vaccines:** Cold storage of COVID-19 vaccines required $-70^\circ\text{C}$ storage (Pfizer).
- **Industrial Process Cooling:** Used in petrochemical, plastic, and chemical industries.
- **Comfort Air Conditioning:** Offices, malls, data centres.
- **Cryogenic Engineering:** LNG liquefaction, space applications, MRI magnets.
- **Ice Skating Rinks & Sports:** Maintenance of sub-zero ice surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] > **For KTU 2024:** Even in an introductory topic, examiners expect students to know the **theoretical maximum efficiency (Carnot COP)** and the **basic energy balance** of a refrigeration cycle. Below are the exhaustive step-by-step derivations.

### 3.1 Derivation of Coefficient of Performance (COP) for a General Refrigerator

**Given:** A refrigerator operating between a low-temperature reservoir at $T_L$ (in Kelvin) and a high-temperature reservoir at $T_H$ (in Kelvin), with refrigeration effect $Q_L$ and work input $W_{net}$.

**To Prove:** $COP_{Refrigerator} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ for a Carnot (reversible) cycle.

**Step 1: Define COP using First Law.**

The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is defined as the **ratio of desired effect (heat removed) to the work input** required.

$$
COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Energy Balance (First Law of Thermodynamics).**

For a cyclic device operating in steady state, the net energy in equals net energy out. The refrigerator takes in $Q_L$ from the cold space, receives work $W_{net}$, and rejects $Q_H$ to the hot space.

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}
$$

Rearranging for $W_{net}$:

$$
W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $W_{net}$ into the COP Equation.**

$$
COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the Second Law (Reversible Cycle — Carnot Principle).**

For a **reversible** (Carnot) cycle, there is **no entropy change in the universe**. Total entropy change of the hot and cold reservoirs is zero.

$$
\Delta S_{universe} = \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} - \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0
$$

> Note: The sign convention used is that $Q_L$ is heat *removed* from the cold reservoir (so $-\dfrac{Q_L}{T_L}$ is the entropy change of cold reservoir, but we take magnitudes).

**Step 5: Solve the Entropy Equation for $Q_H$ in terms of $Q_L$.**

$$
\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L}
$$

$$
Q_H = Q_L \times \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute $Q_H$ into the COP Expression.**

$$
COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L}
$$

**Step 7: Factor $Q_L$ in the denominator.**

$$
COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right)}
$$

**Step 8: Cancel $Q_L$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$
COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1}
$$

**Step 9: Take common denominator $T_L$ in the denominator.**

$$
COP_{Ref} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}
$$

**Step 10: Final Simplified Result.**

$$
COP_{Ref,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

> [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The smaller the temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$, the larger the COP. This is why **freezing food at $-40^\circ\text{C}$** is far more energy-intensive than **cooling a room to $20^\circ\text{C}$** — the temperature lift is larger, and efficiency drops.

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Standard KTU Problem Type

**Problem:** A domestic refrigerator maintains a temperature of $-10^\circ\text{C}$ inside, while the surrounding air is at $35^\circ\text{C}$. Assuming the system operates on a Carnot cycle, calculate the theoretical maximum COP.

**Given:**
- $T_L = -10^\circ\text{C} = 273 - 10 = 263\ \text{K}$
- $T_H = 35^\circ\text{C} = 273 + 35 = 308\ \text{K}$

**Step 1: Apply the Carnot COP formula.**

$$
COP = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
COP = \dfrac{263}{308 - 263} = \dfrac{263}{45}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the ratio.**

$$
COP = 5.844
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

The theoretical maximum COP is **5.844**, meaning for every 1 kW of electrical input, ideally 5.844 kW of heat can be removed. Real refrigerators operate at 30–40% of this value due to irreversibilities.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Refrigeration Methods

| **Parameter** | **VCRS** | **VARS** | **Steam Jet** | **Gas Cycle** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Input | Mechanical (Electric) | Heat (Thermal) | High-pressure Steam | Mechanical |
| COP Range | 2.5 – 5.0 | 0.3 – 0.7 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| Moving Parts | Many (Compressor) | None (Pump only) | Nozzle & Ejector | Compressor |
| Noise Level | Moderate to High | Silent | Moderate | High |
| Maintenance | High | Low | Low | High |
| Typical Application | Domestic AC, Cold Storage | Industrial Waste Heat Recovery | Large AC Plants (Theatres) | Aircraft AC |
| Refrigerant | R-134a, R-410A, NH₃ | NH₃–H₂O, LiBr–H₂O | Water | Air, Helium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```

```

> [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When drawing the above analogy in your answer sheet, **always label the direction of natural heat flow** and **work input separately** using two distinct arrows. This single diagram, if drawn well, can fetch 2 extra marks even in a 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` Define the term **'Ton of Refrigeration'**. Convert 1 TR into SI units.

**Model Answer:**

> **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (1000 kg) of water at $0^\circ\text{C}$ into ice at $0^\circ\text{C}$ in 24 hours**.
>
> Heat removed = mass × latent heat = $1000\ \text{kg} \times 335\ \text{kJ/kg} = 335{,}000\ \text{kJ}$ in 24 hours.
>
> Rate = $\dfrac{335{,}000}{24 \times 60 \times 60} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$
>
> $$\boxed{1\ TR = 3.517\ \text{kW} = 211\ \text{kJ/min}}$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` State **Clausius Statement** of the Second Law of Thermodynamics and explain its relevance to refrigeration.

**Model Answer:**

> **Clausius Statement:** *"It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that can transfer heat from a cold body to a hot body without any external work input."*
>
> **Relevance to Refrigeration:** This statement makes it clear that refrigeration cannot occur spontaneously. To move heat from a low-temperature region (cold space) to a high-temperature region (surroundings), **external work in the form of mechanical energy (compressor) or thermal energy (absorption)** is mandatory. Hence, refrigeration systems are energy-consuming devices, and the minimum work required is governed by the **Carnot COP**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **historical evolution of refrigeration** from the ancient era to the modern era. Highlight the significance of the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** in the context of CFC phase-out. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Phase 1 — Ancient Era (Pre-1800):** Natural ice harvesting in China, India, and Egypt. Romans used saltpeter for cooling. **Limitation:** Seasonal and geographical dependence.
>
> **Phase 2 — Birth of Mechanical Refrigeration (1834–1876):** **Jacob Perkins (1834)** patented the first closed-loop vapour compression system using ether. **Ferdinand Carré (1859)** built the first absorption system with ammonia-water. **Carl von Linde (1876)** commercialized ammonia-based VCRS for breweries, marking the true industrial beginning.
>
> **Phase 3 — Synthetic Refrigerant Era (1930–1990):** **Thomas Midgley Jr. (1930)** synthesized CFC-12 and other CFCs, which were non-toxic and non-flammable — making refrigeration safe for domestic use. The chemical industry boomed.
>
> **Phase 4 — Environmental Awakening (1974–1987):** **Molina and Rowland (1974)** discovered that CFCs release chlorine radicals in the stratosphere that catalytically destroy the **ozone layer**, creating the "ozone hole" over Antarctica. This led to the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**, signed by 197 countries, which mandated the **phase-out of CFCs** and introduced transitional **HCFCs** and modern **HFCs**.
>
> **Phase 5 — Sustainable Refrigeration (2000–Present):** Focus on **Low-GWP** and **Zero-ODP** refrigerants: R-1234yf, R-290 (propane), and **natural refrigerants** like $CO_2$ and $NH_3$. Emerging technologies include **magnetocaloric, thermoelectric, and solar refrigeration**.

---

**(b)** A refrigerator operates between an evaporator temperature of $-15^\circ\text{C}$ and a condenser temperature of $40^\circ\text{C}$. Assuming **Carnot cycle operation**, calculate: **(i)** the theoretical COP, **(ii)** the work input per TR of refrigeration, and **(iii)** the heat rejected to the surroundings per TR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $T_L = -15 + 273 = 258\ \text{K}$
- $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313\ \text{K}$
- Capacity = 1 TR = $3.517\ \text{kW}$

**(i) Theoretical COP:**

$$
COP = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{258}{313 - 258} = \dfrac{258}{55} = 4.691
$$

**(ii) Work Input per TR:**

$$
W = \dfrac{RE}{COP} = \dfrac{3.517}{4.691} = 0.7497\ \text{kW} \approx 0.75\ \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected per TR:**

$$
Q_H = RE + W = 3.517 + 0.75 = 4.267\ \text{kW}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For every TR of cooling, ideally only **0.75 kW** of work is needed, and **4.27 kW** of heat is dumped to the surroundings.

---

#### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **major industrial and domestic applications of refrigeration**, with specific examples for food, pharmaceutical, chemical, and comfort sectors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **1. Food Preservation and Cold Chain (~$200 billion industry):**
> - Cold storage warehouses for fruits, vegetables, and grains.
> - Frozen food transport via refrigerated trucks, ships (reefers), and containers.
> - Dairy industry — milk chilling, ice cream hardening at $-40^\circ\text{C}$.
> - Meat and seafood processing — blast freezers.
>
> **2. Pharmaceutical and Medical:**
> - Vaccine storage (Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine: $-70^\circ\text{C}$).
> - Blood banks, plasma freezing, organ transport.
> - Cryosurgery — destruction of malignant tissues.
>
> **3. Chemical and Petrochemical:**
> - Condensation of volatile gases (e.g., LPG, LNG).
> - Crystallization and separation of chemicals.
> - Low-temperature reactions in polymer and rubber industries.
>
> **4. Comfort Air Conditioning:**
> - Domestic split AC, window AC, central AC for malls, hospitals, and IT parks.
> - Data centre cooling — server rooms at $20\pm 2^\circ\text{C}$.
> - Automobile AC, railway and aircraft AC.
>
> **5. Specialized Applications:**
> - Ice skating rinks and sports facilities.
> - Nuclear reactors — hydrogen cooling.
> - Space technology — cryogenic rocket fuels (LH₂ at $-253^\circ\text{C}$).
> - Manufacturing of superconductors and MRI magnets.

---

**(b)** Explain the **importance of refrigeration** in the modern context. List at least **six key reasons** why refrigeration is critical for sustainable development. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **1. Food Security:** Reduces post-harvest losses (40% in developing countries without cold chain). Critical for SDG Goal 2 (Zero Hunger).
>
> **2. Public Health:** Prevents foodborne diseases, enables vaccine distribution and safe blood storage — supports SDG Goal 3 (Good Health).
>
> **3. Industrial Productivity:** Enables low-temperature manufacturing, precision electronics, and pharmaceuticals.
>
> **4. Energy Conservation Mandate:** Modern eco-friendly refrigerants (R-290, R-1234yf) reduce GWP and align with the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol.
>
> **5. Climate Protection:** Reducing use of high-GWP HFCs can prevent up to $0.5^\circ\text{C}$ of global warming by 2100.
>
> **6. Economic Impact:** Global refrigeration industry employs millions and is a multi-trillion-dollar sector.
>
> **7. Sustainable Development:** Integration with renewable energy (solar refrigeration) reduces fossil fuel dependence.
>
> **Conclusion:** Refrigeration is no longer a luxury but a **pillar of human civilization**, with deep economic, environmental, and humanitarian significance.

---

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Writing $T_L$ in Celsius instead of Kelvin in the COP formula — costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **No definition of 1 TR:** A 3-mark question on "Define Ton of Refrigeration" requires the **numerical conversion (3.517 kW)** — students often forget this.
> 3. **Confusing COP with Efficiency:** COP of a refrigerator can be **greater than 1** — it is NOT efficiency. KTU examiners reject answers that call COP a "percentage efficiency."
> 4. **Missing Clausius Statement:** When asked about the Second Law, **state the statement explicitly** before applying it — at least 1 mark is reserved for this.
> 5. **Forgetting work input direction in diagrams:** Arrows showing work input into the compressor must be **labelled clearly** in any T-s or P-h diagram.
> 6. **Not mentioning Montreal Protocol:** In any historical question, omitting 1987 Montreal Protocol leads to a **2-mark deduction**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Refrigeration** = transfer of heat from cold body to hot body using external work.
- [x] **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** = $3.517\ \text{kW} = 211\ \text{kJ/min}$.
- [x] **COP of Refrigerator** = $\dfrac{RE}{W_{net,in}}$ — dimensionless, can exceed 1.
- [x] **Carnot COP (Theoretical Max)** = $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — always in **Kelvin**.
- [x] **Key Historical Milestones:** Perkins (1834) → Carré (1859) → Linde (1876) → Midgley (1930s) → Molina-Rowland (1974) → Montreal Protocol (1987).
- [x] **Refrigeration Methods:** VCRS, VARS, Steam Jet, Gas Cycle.
- [x] **Applications:** Food, Pharma, Chemical, AC, Cryogenics, Space.
- [x] **Environmental Note:** CFCs are phased out; HFCs and natural refrigerants ($NH_3$, $CO_2$, R-290) are the future.
- [x] **Importance:** Food security, public health, industry, climate protection, economy.
- [x] **Clausius Statement:** Mandatory reference in any 2nd Law refrigeration question.
- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** Use **Kelvin**, not Celsius, in all thermodynamic formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Reversed Carnot Cycle, Heat Pumps, Refrigeration Machines, Limitations of the Reversed Carnot Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Reversed Carnot Cycle, Heat Pumps, Refrigeration Machines, Limitations of the Reversed Carnot Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 1 — Thermodynamics of Refrigeration

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Refrigeration — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration** is the process of **removing heat from a low-temperature body (or space) and transferring it to a high-temperature body (or surrounding)** with the help of an external agency (work input). It is the practical reverse of a heat engine cycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, refrigeration is a **non-spontaneous heat transfer** process, made possible only by supplying external work (or heat) as per the **Clausius Statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Bucket on the Hill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine water naturally flows downhill (spontaneous). To move water *uphill* (non-spontaneous), you need a pump consuming electricity. Similarly, heat naturally flows from **hot → cold** (spontaneous). A **refrigerator** is essentially a "heat pump" that pushes heat from **cold → hot**, but it requires **work input (W)** to do so. The amount of cooling obtained per unit work spent is the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**.

### 1.3 The Three Core Devices in Refrigeration Engineering

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus explicitly classifies refrigeration devices into **three fundamental categories** based on the **direction of desired effect** and the **temperature levels** involved:

| Device | Desired Output | Rejects | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerator** | Cooling Effect ($Q_L$) at low temperature | Heat ($Q_H$) at high temperature | Cold storage, AC, freezers |
| **Heat Pump** | Heating Effect ($Q_H$) at high temperature | Cold (sinks $Q_L$) at low temperature | Space heating in winter, water heating |
| **Refrigeration Machine** | Generalized term — produces cooling at sub-ambient temperatures for **industrial** applications | Heat to atmosphere/sink | Process plants, chemical, food, pharma |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - This forms the **theoretical foundation** for Module 1, upon which **Vapour Compression (Module 2)** and **Vapour Absorption (Module 3)** cycles are built.
> - Expected KTU Question Weightage: **~15–20%** of Module 1 marks.
> - Frequently asked as **Part A (3 marks)** derivation and **Part B (14 marks)** COP calculation problems.

---

## 2. The Reversed Carnot Cycle — KTU High-Yield Foundation

### 2.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a **completely reversible thermodynamic cycle** in which a working fluid undergoes **two isothermal processes and two isentropic processes** in *reverse direction* compared to the standard Carnot (heat engine) cycle. It represents the **theoretical maximum possible performance** of any refrigeration or heat pump cycle operating between the **same two temperature reservoirs** $T_L$ and $T_H$.

### 2.2 The Four Processes (with direction reversed)

| Process | Operation | State Change | Heat Transfer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 → 2** | Isentropic Compression | $T_L \rightarrow T_H$ | Adiabatic (Q = 0) |
| **2 → 3** | Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$ | $V$ decreases at constant $T_H$ | $Q_H$ rejected |
| **3 → 4** | Isentropic Expansion | $T_H \rightarrow T_L$ | Adiabatic (Q = 0) |
| **4 → 1** | Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$ | $V$ increases at constant $T_L$ | $Q_L$ absorbed |

### 2.3 The Conceptual Logic — *Why* and *How*

- **Step 1 (1→2):** Compressor pumps in **work (W_comp)** to raise refrigerant temperature from $T_L$ to $T_H$ *without any heat exchange* (perfectly insulated, reversible = isentropic).
- **Step 2 (2→3):** Refrigerant at $T_H$ **rejects heat $Q_H$** to the hot reservoir at **constant temperature $T_H$** (isothermal, requires external cooling).
- **Step 3 (3→4):** Expansion engine extracts **work (W_exp)** and drops temperature from $T_H$ back to $T_L$ *adiabatically and reversibly* (isentropic).
- **Step 4 (4→1):** Cold refrigerant **absorbs heat $Q_L$** from the cold space at **constant $T_L$** (isothermal). This is the **useful refrigeration effect (RE)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The Reversed Carnot Cycle is the **gold standard** — no real cycle can exceed its COP. It serves as a **benchmark for evaluating real systems**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All key formulas for this topic, structured for quick revision:

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Rejected at $T_H$ | $Q_H = T_H \cdot (S_2 - S_3)$ | kJ/kg·K × K = kJ/kg | Isothermal |
| 2 | Heat Absorbed at $T_L$ | $Q_L = T_L \cdot (S_1 - S_4)$ | kJ/kg | Isothermal |
| 3 | Net Work Input | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | kJ/kg | Energy balance |
| 4 | COP of Refrigerator (Carnot) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 5 | COP of Heat Pump (Carnot) | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 6 | Relation between COPs | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | — | Always true |
| 7 | Energy Balance | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | kJ/kg | First Law |
| 8 | Refrigerating Effect | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Real cycles (Module 2) |
| 9 | Work of Compression | $W_C = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Real cycles |
| 10 | Heat Rejected | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Real cycles |

> **Boundary Conditions:** $T_H$ and $T_L$ **must be in Kelvin** (K) for COP calculations. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Sets the **upper theoretical limit** for refrigeration performance.
- Basis for the **Second-Law Efficiency** or **Carnot Efficiency** benchmarking of real refrigeration systems.
- Used in **cryogenics, LNG liquefaction, cold-chain logistics** where approaching Carnot limits is the design goal.
- Foundation for understanding why **real systems are always inferior** to the reversed Carnot cycle.

---

## 3. Heat Pumps & Refrigeration Machines — Analytical Deep Dive

### 3.1 The Refrigerator — Mathematical Treatment

A **Refrigerator** is a cyclic device operating between two reservoirs. The **objective is to extract heat $Q_L$** from the cold space, and the **performance metric is COP of Refrigerator ($COP_R$)**.

#### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $COP_R$ (Reversed Carnot)

We start from **fundamental energy balance (First Law)** for a cyclic device:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net, in}$$

This says the heat dumped to the hot reservoir equals the heat lifted from cold reservoir plus the work supplied.

For a **reversible (Carnot) cycle**, the **Second Law** demands the cyclic integral of $\dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$

Expanding this for the four processes of the reversed Carnot cycle (with 1→2 and 3→4 adiabatic, $\delta Q = 0$):

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

Rearranging to express the heat ratio:

$$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

Now, the net work input from the energy balance is:

$$W_{net, in} = Q_H - Q_L$$

Dividing the numerator and denominator by $Q_L$:

$$W_{net, in} = Q_L \left( \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1 \right)$$

Substituting the reversible heat ratio $Q_H / Q_L = T_H / T_L$:

$$W_{net, in} = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right) = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$

The **Coefficient of Performance of the refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of desired effect to work input:

$$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net, in}}$$

Substituting the expression for work input:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$

The $Q_L$ cancels out cleanly, leaving the **canonical Carnot COP formula**:

$$\boxed{\,COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $COP_R$ can be **greater than 1** (unlike thermal efficiency of a heat engine, which is always < 1). A $COP_R$ of 4 means: 1 kW of work input → 4 kW of cooling effect. The remaining 5 kW ($Q_H$) is dumped to the hot side.

#### 3.1.2 Numerical Example — Standard KTU Type

**Problem:** A Carnot refrigerator operates between $-10°C$ and $30°C$. Find (a) $COP_R$, (b) Heat rejected per kW of cooling, (c) Work input per kW of cooling.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:**
$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$
$T_H = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$

**Step 2 — Compute $COP_R$:**

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575$$

**Step 3 — Heat rejected per kW cooling** (here $Q_L = 1$ kW):

$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{1}{6.575} = 0.1521 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Heat rejected:**

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 1 + 0.1521 = 1.1521 \text{ kW}$$

> **Answer:** $COP_R = 6.575$, $W_{in} = 0.1521$ kW, $Q_H = 1.1521$ kW per kW of refrigeration.

---

### 3.2 The Heat Pump — Mathematical Treatment

A **Heat Pump** is a cyclic device whose **objective is to deliver heat $Q_H$** to the hot space (e.g., room heating in winter). The **performance metric is COP of Heat Pump ($COP_{HP}$)**.

#### 3.2.1 Exhaustive Derivation of $COP_{HP}$ (Reversed Carnot)

Starting from the same energy balance:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net, in}$$

For a reversible cycle, the entropy balance gives:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

The **desired effect** for a heat pump is $Q_H$, and the work input is $W_{net, in}$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net, in}}$$

From the energy balance: $W_{net, in} = Q_H - Q_L$.

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $Q_H$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}}$$

Using the reversible heat ratio $Q_L / Q_H = T_L / T_H$:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}}$$

This simplifies to the **canonical Carnot Heat Pump COP**:

$$\boxed{\,COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$

#### 3.2.2 Derivation of the Universal Relation $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$

Starting from the definitions:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W}, \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W}$$

Adding them:

$$COP_R + COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L}{W} + \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_L + Q_H}{W}$$

But from the energy balance, $Q_L + Q_H = W + 2Q_L$, which doesn't simplify directly. Using the energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W$ instead:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{W} + 1$$

Recognising $Q_L / W = COP_R$:

$$\boxed{\,COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This relation is universally true** — it holds for **any** refrigeration cycle, not just Carnot. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question** in Part A.

#### 3.2.3 Why Use a Heat Pump Instead of Direct Electric Heating?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Electric heater → 1 kW electric → 1 kW heat (COP = 1).
> **Heat pump** (e.g., COP = 4) → 1 kW electric + 3 kW from cold outdoor air = **4 kW heat** delivered.
> **Savings:** Heat pumps can deliver 3–4× more heat per unit electricity. This is why heat pumps are the cornerstone of **green building HVAC design** in KTU 2024 sustainable energy modules.

---

### 3.3 Refrigeration Machines — Industrial Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration Machines** (in KTU 2024 industrial context) are **Refrigerators scaled for industrial use** — delivering large refrigerating capacities (tons of refrigeration, TR) for:
> - **Process cooling** in chemical/petrochemical plants
> - **Food preservation and cold storage**
> - **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** (vaccine cold chains)
> - **Ice rinks and cryogenic applications**

The governing thermodynamic relations are **identical** to those of a refrigerator:

$$COP_{RM} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net, in}} = \frac{RE}{W_C - W_E}$$

where $RE$ = Refrigerating Effect, $W_C$ = Compressor work, $W_E$ = Expander work.

> For an **ideal Reversed Carnot refrigeration machine**, the same $COP_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ applies, but with **industrial-scale reservoir temperatures** typically $-40°C$ to $5°C$ for $T_L$ and $25°C$ to $40°C$ for $T_H$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Refrigerator vs Heat Pump

| Parameter | Refrigerator | Heat Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Desired Effect | $Q_L$ (cooling) | $Q_H$ (heating) |
| Work Input | $W$ | $W$ |
| COP Formula | $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | $T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ |
| COP Value | Lower (always < $COP_{HP}$) | Higher (always > $COP_R$) |
| Typical Application | AC, freezer, cold room | Space heating, water heater |
| Direction of "Useful" heat flow | From cold space | To hot space |
| Can COP > 1? | **Yes** (always) | **Yes** (always) |
| Energy dumped | $Q_H$ (waste) | $Q_L$ (absorbed from outdoor air) |

---

### 3.5 Master COP Derivation (Heat Pump Variant — Alternative Method)

For a comprehensive understanding, let's re-derive $COP_{HP}$ from the entropy definition directly:

**Step 1:** Isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$ (process 2→3):
$$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S = T_H (S_2 - S_3)$$

**Step 2:** Isothermal heat absorption at $T_L$ (process 4→1):
$$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S = T_L (S_1 - S_4)$$

For a **cycle**, entropy change is zero, so the magnitudes of $\Delta S$ are equal:
$$S_2 - S_3 = S_1 - S_4 = \Delta S$$

**Step 3:** Net work:
$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$$

**Step 4:** $COP_{HP}$:
$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_H \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S}$$

The $\Delta S$ cancels:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

> This confirms our earlier derivation through a **different route** (entropy-based), demonstrating internal consistency — an approach examiners love for full marks.

---

## 4. Limitations of the Reversed Carnot Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although the Reversed Carnot Cycle is the **theoretical benchmark** with the highest possible COP, it is **physically impossible to realize in practice** using a real working fluid. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly demands the **major practical limitations** be studied.

### 4.1 The Five Major Limitations

> **L1 — Wet Compression Problem**
> During isentropic compression (1→2), the refrigerant is in the **two-phase (wet) region** to satisfy the isothermal heat absorption at $T_L$. Compressing a liquid-vapour mixture is **practically impossible** — the liquid droplets damage compressor valves and cylinders. Real compressors can only compress **dry (superheated) vapour**.

> **L2 — Expander → Throttle Replacement**
> The isentropic expansion (3→4) requires a **work-producing expansion engine**. Real systems use a **throttling valve (capillary tube/expansion valve)** which is an **irreversible, isenthalpic** process. This irreversibility **destroys the isentropic nature** and **reduces actual COP** well below Carnot COP.

> **L3 — Isothermal Compression/Expansion Unrealizable**
> True isothermal compression requires **infinite heat exchange rate** to maintain constant temperature during compression. Real compression is closer to **adiabatic or polytropic**, not isothermal. Same problem applies to isothermal expansion.

> **L4 — Two-Phase Working Fluid Complications**
> The cycle requires the working fluid to exist in the **two-phase region** during isothermal processes (2→3 and 4→1). This mandates **precise and instantaneous phase change** at constant $T$ and $P$ — a practically unattainable idealization due to **finite heat transfer rates** in real condensers and evaporators.

> **L5 — Infinite Heat Exchanger Area**
> To achieve true isothermal heat transfer at $T_H$ and $T_L$ with finite temperature driving force $\Delta T \rightarrow 0$, the **heat exchanger area must approach infinity**. This is physically and economically infeasible.

### 4.2 Why We Still Study the Reversed Carnot Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> - It is the **theoretical benchmark** for **Second-Law (Exergy) Analysis**.
> - Real cycles (Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption) are **compared to it** using **Second-Law Efficiency**:
> $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \times 100\%$$
> - Drives research into **near-Carnot cycles** like **Ericsson, Stirling, and Magnetic Refrigeration**.

### 4.3 Comparison: Reversed Carnot vs Real Vapour Compression Cycle

| Parameter | Reversed Carnot | Real Vapour Compression |
|---|---|---|
| Compression | Isentropic, **wet** | Isentropic, **dry (superheated)** |
| Expansion | Isentropic, **work-producing** | Throttling, **isenthalpic** |
| Heat Rejection | Isothermal | Constant pressure (≈ isothermal) |
| Heat Absorption | Isothermal | Constant pressure (≈ isothermal) |
| COP | Maximum possible | Lower (60–80% of Carnot) |
| Practical? | **No** | **Yes (industry standard)** |
| Complexity | Theoretical | Engineered, reliable |

# 📘 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The First Law Applied to Refrigeration Cycles — Energy Audit

For **any cyclic refrigeration device** (Reversed Carnot, Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption), the energy balance across one complete cycle is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:

$$\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_H = Q_L + W_{net, in}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in refrigeration thermodynamics. The system absorbs $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir, receives work $W_{net, in}$, and rejects $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir. There is **no net change** in internal energy over a cycle ($\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$).

## 2.2 The Second Law Constraint — Reversibility & Entropy

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** imposes the following on any cyclic device:

- **Reversible (Carnot) Cycle:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$
- **Irreversible (Real) Cycle:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$ — entropy is *generated*, destroying useful work potential.

For the Reversed Carnot cycle, this gives the **fundamental heat ratio**:

$$\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

This relation is **only true for reversible cycles**. In real cycles, $Q_H / Q_L > T_H / T_L$ (more heat must be rejected for the same $Q_L$, meaning **more work is consumed**).

## 2.3 Generalised Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table forms the **complete KTU 2024 Module 1 Formula Arsenal** — every numerical question in this module can be solved using these alone.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Domain / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Rejected | $Q_H$ | $T_H (S_2 - S_3)$ | Reversed Carnot, kJ/kg |
| 2 | Heat Absorbed (Refrigerating Effect) | $Q_L$ or $RE$ | $T_L (S_1 - S_4)$ | Reversed Carnot, kJ/kg |
| 3 | Net Work Input | $W_{net}$ | $Q_H - Q_L$ | Always positive, kJ/kg |
| 4 | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_{R,C}$ | $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | $T$ in K, dimensionless |
| 5 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP,C}$ | $T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ | $T$ in K, dimensionless |
| 6 | Universal COP Relation | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | — | True for **all** cycles |
| 7 | Energy Balance | $Q_H = Q_L + W$ | — | First Law |
| 8 | Reversible Heat Ratio | $Q_H/Q_L = T_H/T_L$ | — | Reversible cycle only |
| 9 | Second-Law Efficiency | $\eta_{II}$ | $COP_{actual}/COP_{Carnot}$ | Real-cycle benchmarking |
| 10 | Refrigeration Tonnage | 1 TR | $3.517$ kW | Standard unit (ice-melting) |
| 11 | Heat Pump Heating Mode COP | $COP_{HP}$ | $Q_H / W$ | Mode: heating |
| 12 | Refrigerator Cooling Mode COP | $COP_R$ | $Q_L / W$ | Mode: cooling |

> **Tip:** Every KTU Part A (3-mark) question on this topic asks for one of these formulas. Memorising the **derivation logic** (not just the formula) guarantees full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Reversed Carnot Framework

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters beyond the exam hall:**
> 1. **HVAC Industry:** Every chiller manufacturer's catalogue specifies the **"kW/ton"** (inverse of COP). Comparing this to the Carnot limit gives the **Second-Law Efficiency**, a key procurement spec.
> 2. **Cryogenics:** LNG, liquid nitrogen, and helium liquefaction systems are designed to approach the Carnot COP as closely as possible — small COP improvements save millions of dollars in operating cost.
> 3. **Heat Pump Design:** Modern cold-climate heat pumps (e.g., in Scandinavian countries) operate with $COP_{HP}$ between 3 and 5, approaching 50–60% of the Carnot limit.
> 4. **Refrigerant Selection:** Drives the choice of low-GWP refrigerants (R-32, R-290, CO₂) since efficiency gains compound the environmental benefit.

# 📘 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation: $COP_R$ for Reversed Carnot Cycle (Exhaustive)

We begin by **defining** the cycle. The Reversed Carnot cycle consists of **two isothermal** and **two isentropic** processes, traversed in the *reverse* direction to the standard Carnot heat engine.

### 3.1.1 Process-by-Process Mathematical Description

**Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression):**
- The refrigerant in the cold space is compressed from state 1 to state 2.
- It is **perfectly insulated** and the process is **reversible** → $\delta q = 0$ and $ds = 0$.
- Work done **on** the system: $w_{12} = h_2 - h_1 > 0$.
- For an ideal gas (Carnot working fluid): $T_2 / T_1 = (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$.

**Process 2 → 3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$):**
- The refrigerant at $T_H$ rejects heat $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir.
- Temperature is constant at $T_H$.
- Heat rejected: $q_{23} = T_H (s_2 - s_3) < 0$ (negative since $s_3 < s_2$).

**Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion):**
- Refrigerant expands isentropically, doing work on the surroundings.
- Work done **by** the system: $w_{34} = h_3 - h_4 > 0$ (work output).
- Temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.

**Process 4 → 1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$):**
- Refrigerant absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold space at constant $T_L$.
- Heat absorbed: $q_{41} = T_L (s_1 - s_4) > 0$ (positive since $s_1 > s_4$).

### 3.1.2 Energy Balance Over the Cycle

For a **complete cycle**, internal energy change is zero ($\Delta u_{cycle} = 0$). Applying the First Law:

$$q_{net} = w_{net}$$

Summing the heat transfers:

$$q_{net} = q_{12} + q_{23} + q_{34} + q_{41}$$

Since $q_{12} = q_{34} = 0$ (isentropic):

$$q_{net} = q_{23} + q_{41} = -Q_H + Q_L$$

The work input to the cycle is:

$$w_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$

### 3.1.3 Entropy Balance Over the Cycle

For a **reversible** cycle, the cyclic integral of $\delta q / T$ vanishes:

$$\oint \frac{\delta q}{T} = 0$$

Evaluating process-by-process:

$$\frac{q_{12}}{T_1 \to T_2 (\text{varies})} + \frac{q_{23}}{T_H} + \frac{q_{34}}{T_3 \to T_4 (\text{varies})} + \frac{q_{41}}{T_L} = 0$$

The isentropic processes have $\delta q = 0$, so they contribute nothing:

$$\frac{q_{23}}{T_H} + \frac{q_{41}}{T_L} = 0$$

Substituting $q_{23} = -Q_H$ and $q_{41} = Q_L$:

$$-\frac{Q_H}{T_H} + \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$

### 3.1.4 Solving for $W_{net,in}$ in Terms of $Q_L$

From the energy balance: $W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$.

Factor out $Q_L$:

$$W_{net,in} = Q_L \left( \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1 \right)$$

Apply the reversible heat ratio $Q_H / Q_L = T_H / T_L$:

$$W_{net,in} = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right)$$

Combine the bracket over a common denominator:

$$W_{net,in} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$

### 3.1.5 The Final $COP_R$ Expression

By definition:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$

Substituting the expression for $W_{net,in}$:

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$

The $Q_L$ cancels:

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

> ✅ **Final Result:** $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — proven rigorously from First and Second Laws.

---

## 3.2 Complete Derivation: $COP_{HP}$ and the Universal Relation

### 3.2.1 Starting Point

We use the **same energy and entropy balances** as above. The only difference is the **definition** of COP for a heat pump:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$

### 3.2.2 Expressing $Q_H$ in Terms of $W_{net,in}$

From the energy balance, $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$. Substituting:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net,in}}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} + \frac{W_{net,in}}{W_{net,in}}$$

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} + 1$$

Recognising the first term as $COP_R$:

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$

> ✅ **Universal Relation Proven.**

### 3.2.3 Closed-Form for $COP_{HP}$ in Terms of Temperatures

Using $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$ and $Q_H/Q_L = T_H/T_L$ (reversible):

$$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right) = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$

Also, $Q_H = T_H/T_L \cdot Q_L$, so:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{(T_H/T_L) \cdot Q_L}{Q_L \cdot (T_H - T_L)/T_L}$$

Simplify:

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

> ✅ **Final Result:** $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$

---

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical Problem — KTU 14-Mark Style

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> A Reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle operates between a cold reservoir at **$-15°C$** and a hot reservoir at **$27°C$**. The refrigerating capacity is **5 kW**.
> **Find:**
> (a) COP of the refrigerator and COP of the heat pump.
> (b) Heat rejected per second and work input per second.
> (c) Second-Law efficiency if an actual refrigerator between the same reservoirs has $COP_{actual} = 2.8$.

### 3.3.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Temperature Conversion:**
$$T_L = -15 + 273 = 258 \text{ K}$$
$$T_H = 27 + 273 = 300 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 (a) — $COP_R$ and $COP_{HP}$:**

$$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{300 - 258} = \frac{258}{42} = 6.1429$$

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 6.1429 + 1 = 7.1429$$

> **Verification (independent formula):**
> $COP_{HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L) = 300 / 42 = 7.1429$ ✓

**Step 3 (b) — Work Input and Heat Rejected:**

Given: $Q_L = 5$ kW (refrigerating capacity).

$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{5}{6.1429} = 0.8140 \text{ kW}$$

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 5 + 0.8140 = 5.8140 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 (c) — Second-Law Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{2.8}{6.1429} = 0.4558 = 45.58\%$$

> **Interpretation:** The real refrigerator achieves **45.58%** of the theoretical maximum performance — typical for industrial reciprocating chillers.

### 3.3.2 Tabulated Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $T_L$ | 258 | K |
| $T_H$ | 300 | K |
| $COP_R$ (Carnot) | 6.1429 | — |
| $COP_{HP}$ (Carnot) | 7.1429 | — |
| $W_{in}$ | 0.8140 | kW |
| $Q_H$ rejected | 5.8140 | kW |
| $\eta_{II}$ | 45.58 | % |

---

## 3.4 The Infeasibility Argument — Why Reversed Carnot is Unrealizable

The **fundamental contradiction** in the Reversed Carnot cycle lies in the **concurrent demands** of processes 1→2 and 4→1:

- **Process 1→2 (Isentropic Compression):** Requires the refrigerant to be in the **two-phase (wet) region** at the *end* of isothermal heat absorption, so that a temperature rise during compression can be achieved *isentropically*.
- **Process 4→1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption):** Requires the refrigerant to *start* as a **dry vapour** (or saturated vapour) and absorb heat at constant $T_L$ as it expands (volume increases).

These two requirements are **mutually exclusive** in any real thermodynamic fluid — a substance cannot simultaneously be a saturated mixture (for compression) and a pure saturated vapour (for isothermal absorption) at the same state. This is the **core reason** the Reversed Carnot cycle remains a **theoretical ideal** and is **physically unrealizable** with any real working fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Why is Reversed Carnot impractical?"*, the **wet compression problem** and the **throttling valve irreversibility** together account for **2 out of 2 marks** allocated to the limitations question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reversed Carnot Cycle on T-s Plane

The following **block-level functional architecture flow** maps the four processes of the Reversed Carnot cycle, with state points and energy flows annotated. This is a **schematic representation** of the T-s and P-V diagrams in mermaid-compatible form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    state1["STATE 1: Saturated Vapour at T_L"] --> proc12["PROCESS 1 TO 2: Isentropic Compression"]
    proc12 --> state2["STATE 2: High Temp Vapour at T_H"]
    state2 --> proc23["PROCESS 2 TO 3: Isothermal Heat Rejection at T_H"]
    proc23 --> state3["STATE 3: Wet Vapour at T_H"]
    state3 --> proc34["PROCESS 3 TO 4: Isentropic Expansion"]
    proc34 --> state4["STATE 4: Wet Vapour at T_L"]
    state4 --> proc41["PROCESS 4 TO 1: Isothermal Heat Absorption at T_L"]
    proc41 --> state1

    qL["Q_L: HEAT ABSORBED FROM COLD SPACE"] -.-> proc41
    qH["Q_H: HEAT REJECTED TO HOT RESERVOIR"] -.-> proc23
    wComp["W_comp: COMPRESSOR WORK INPUT"] -.-> proc12
    wExp["W_exp: EXPANDER WORK OUTPUT"] -.-> proc34

    style state1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style state2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000
    style state3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000
    style state4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style proc12 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style proc23 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style proc34 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style proc41 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600
    style qL fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#336600
    style qH fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
    style wComp fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366
    style wExp fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Refrigerator vs Heat Pump (Same Hardware, Different Goal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REFRIGERATOR_MODE["REFRIGERATOR MODE GOAL: DELIVER Q_L TO COLD SPACE"]
        R1["Cold Space T_L"] -->|Heat Q_L absorbed| REF1["Refrigeration Cycle"]
        REF1 -->|Heat Q_H rejected| R2["Hot Reservoir T_H"]
        REF1 -.->|Work W input| W1["Power Source"]
    end

    subgraph HEATPUMP_MODE["HEAT PUMP MODE GOAL: DELIVER Q_H TO HOT SPACE"]
        HP1["Cold Outdoor Air T_L"] -->|Heat Q_L absorbed| HPM1["Refrigeration Cycle"]
        HPM1 -->|Heat Q_H delivered| HP2["Hot Indoor Space T_H"]
        HPM1 -.->|Work W input| W2["Power Source"]
    end

    style REFRIGERATOR_MODE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style HEATPUMP_MODE fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
```

## 4.3 Schematic Energy Flow — Sankey-Style Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    workInput["W = NET WORK INPUT 1 kW"] -->|100 percent energy| sysCenter["REVERSED CARNOT CYCLE"]
    sysCenter -->|Q_L = T_L by T_H minus T_L kW| qLBlock["Q_L DELIVERED TO COLD SPACE Desired Cooling Effect"]
    sysCenter -->|Q_H = T_H by T_H minus T_L kW| qHBlock["Q_H REJECTED TO HOT RESERVOIR Waste Heat"]

    style workInput fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366
    style sysCenter fill:#ffe066,stroke:#806600
    style qLBlock fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600
    style qHBlock fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Sankey diagram:** For a Reversed Carnot refrigerator with $COP_R = 4$, if 1 kW of work is input:
> - $Q_L = 4$ kW goes to the cold space (cooling effect).
> - $Q_H = 5$ kW is rejected to the hot reservoir.
> - Energy is **conserved**: $1 + 4 = 5$ kW ✓

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Limitations Summary (Cause-Effect Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["REVERSED CARNOT CYCLE IDEAL CYCLE"] --> B1["LIMITATION 1: WET COMPRESSION"]
    A --> B2["LIMITATION 2: THROTTLING INSTEAD OF EXPANSION"]
    A --> B3["LIMITATION 3: ISOTHERMAL PROCESSES UNREALIZABLE"]
    A --> B4["LIMITATION 4: TWO PHASE WORKING FLUID"]
    A --> B5["LIMITATION 5: INFINITE HEAT EXCHANGER AREA"]

    B1 --> C["NET RESULT: PRACTICALLY UNREALIZABLE"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C

    C --> D["REAL CYCLES DEVELOPED: VAPOUR COMPRESSION MODULE 2, VAPOUR ABSORPTION MODULE 3"]

    style A fill:#ffe066,stroke:#806600
    style C fill:#ff6666,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#99ff99,stroke:#006600
```
