<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from the COP of a heat pump? Show the relation between them.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** COP of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it:
> $$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$

> **COP of Heat Pump:** Defined as the ratio of desired heating effect (heat delivered to the hot space) to the work input:
> $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$

> **Difference:** In a refrigerator, the desired output is $Q_L$ (cooling); in a heat pump, it is $Q_H$ (heating). The heat pump COP is always higher than the refrigerator COP because it delivers a *larger* useful effect for the same work input.

> **Relation:**
> $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$$
> This is proven from the First Law energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W$, hence $COP_{HP} = Q_H/W = (Q_L + W)/W = COP_R + 1$.

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **State the major limitations of the Reversed Carnot cycle for practical refrigeration.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reversed Carnot cycle, although a theoretical ideal, is **not practically realizable** due to the following five limitations:
>
> 1. **Wet Compression:** The cycle requires compression of a liquid-vapour mixture (wet vapour), which is practically impossible as liquid droplets damage compressor components.
>
> 2. **Throttling instead of Isentropic Expansion:** The isentropic expansion (3→4) requires a work-producing expansion engine. In practice, only a throttling valve is used, which is an irreversible isenthalpic process.
>
> 3. **Isothermal Processes Unrealizable:** True isothermal heat transfer requires infinite heat exchanger area; real processes deviate.
>
> 4. **Two-Phase Working Fluid:** The refrigerant must exist in the two-phase region during heat transfer processes, requiring instantaneous phase change — practically unattainable.
>
> 5. **Infinite Heat Exchanger Area:** To maintain $\Delta T \to 0$ for isothermal heat exchange, an infinitely large heat exchanger is needed.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** — Module 1, Internal Choice Option A

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **COP of a Reversed Carnot refrigerator** operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_L$ and $T_H$ ($T_H > T_L$). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Reversed Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a food preservation chamber at **$-5°C$**. The ambient temperature is **$30°C$**. If the refrigeration load is **$2.5$ kW**, calculate:
>   (i) $COP_R$ and $COP_{HP}$
>   (ii) Work input to the compressor per hour
>   (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings per hour
>   **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — B-A(a)**

**Step 1 — Define the cycle:**
The Reversed Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal and two isentropic processes in reverse direction to the standard Carnot heat engine.

**Step 2 — First Law Energy Balance:**
For a complete cycle, $\oint \delta Q = \oint \delta W$, hence:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$$

**Step 3 — Second Law (Reversibility Condition):**
For a reversible cycle:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 4 — Express Work in Terms of $Q_L$:**

$$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_L \left( \frac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1 \right) = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right) = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 5 — Definition of $COP_R$:**

$$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$

### **Model Solution — B-A(b)**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_L = -5 + 273 = 268 \text{ K}$$
$$T_H = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2(i) — Compute COPs:**

$$COP_R = \frac{268}{303 - 268} = \frac{268}{35} = 7.6571$$

$$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 7.6571 + 1 = 8.6571$$

**Step 2(ii) — Work Input per Hour:**

Refrigeration load $Q_L = 2.5$ kW = 2.5 kJ/s.

$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{2.5}{7.6571} = 0.3265 \text{ kW}$$

Per hour: $W_{in} = 0.3265 \times 3600 = 1175.3$ kJ/hr = **$1.1753$ MJ/hr**.

**Step 2(iii) — Heat Rejected per Hour:**

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 2.5 + 0.3265 = 2.8265 \text{ kW}$$

Per hour: $Q_H = 2.8265 \times 3600 = 10,175.3$ kJ/hr = **$10.175$ MJ/hr**.

**Verification:** $Q_L/W_{in} = 2.5/0.3265 = 7.657$ ✓

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** — Module 1, Internal Choice Option B

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With the help of a clear schematic, explain the working of a **Reversed Carnot heat pump** cycle. Derive the expression for the **COP of a heat pump** in terms of reservoir temperatures. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A building in a KTU campus requires **$30$ kW** of heating using a **Reversed Carnot heat pump**. The indoor temperature must be maintained at **$22°C$** while the outside air is at **$8°C$**. Calculate:
>   (i) $COP_{HP}$ and $COP_R$
>   (ii) Power consumed by the heat pump
>   (iii) Compare with direct electric heating in terms of energy saved
>   **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution — B-B(a)**

**Step 1 — Schematic Description:**
Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the functional block diagram. The heat pump absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a low-temperature source (e.g., outdoor air at $T_L$), receives work input $W$, and delivers a larger quantity of heat $Q_H = Q_L + W$ to the high-temperature indoor space at $T_H$.

**Step 2 — First Law:**
$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$$

**Step 3 — Reversible Heat Ratio:**
$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$

**Step 4 — Work in Terms of $Q_H$:**
From the energy balance: $Q_L = Q_H - W$, and from the reversible heat ratio: $Q_L = Q_H \cdot T_L / T_H$.

$$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_H - Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H} = Q_H \left( 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} \right) = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}$$

**Step 5 — COP of Heat Pump:**

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\,}$$

### **Model Solution — B-B(b)**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$T_L = 8 + 273 = 281 \text{ K}$$
$$T_H = 22 + 273 = 295 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2(i) — Compute COPs:**

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{295}{295 - 281} = \frac{295}{14} = 21.0714$$

$$COP_R = COP_{HP} - 1 = 20.0714$$

**Step 2(ii) — Power Consumed:**

Heating capacity: $Q_H = 30$ kW.

$$W_{in} = \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{30}{21.0714} = 1.4237 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2(iii) — Comparison with Electric Heating:**

| Method | Energy Input | Useful Heat Delivered |
|---|---|---|
| Direct Electric Heater | 30 kW | 30 kW (COP = 1) |
| Reversed Carnot Heat Pump | 1.4237 kW | 30 kW (COP = 21.07) |

**Energy Saved per Hour:**

$$E_{saved} = (30 - 1.4237) \text{ kW} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 28.5763 \times 3600 = 102,874.7 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

$$\text{Savings} = 28.5763 \text{ kW} \approx 28.58 \text{ kW} \cdot 95.25\% \text{ reduction in electricity consumption}$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** A Reversed Carnot heat pump can deliver the same heating as a 30 kW electric heater using only **1.42 kW of input power** — a **21-fold improvement** in energy efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Temperature Units Error (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ — using °C directly gives wrong COP values.
>
> 2. **Confusing Refrigerator vs Heat Pump COP (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often interchange the formulas. Remember:
>    - $COP_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — cooling mode
>    - $COP_{HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ — heating mode
>    - Always $COP_{HP} > COP_R$ since $T_H > T_L$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Universal Relation $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (–1 mark):** Examiners often test this independently; missing the +1 is a frequent error.
>
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions (–1 mark):** Always write "**Assuming Reversed Carnot cycle with isothermal heat transfer and isentropic compression/expansion**" at the start of derivations.
>
> 5. **Unit Mismatch in Numerical Answers (–1 mark):** Work in kW (rate) vs kJ (energy) — clearly state which is being asked.
>
> 6. **Limitations Question — Listing Only 2–3 of 5 (–2 marks):** Examiners expect **at least 4 limitations** in a 3-mark answer. Memorize all 5 from Section 4.1.
>
> 7. **Derivation Missing the Second Law Step (–3 marks):** A 7-mark derivation question without the entropy balance $\oint \delta Q/T = 0$ step is incomplete. Always show **both First Law and Second Law** explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Reversed Carnot, Heat Pumps, Refrigeration Machines, Limitations)**
>
> ✅ **Reversed Carnot Cycle** — 2 isothermal + 2 isentropic processes, traversed in reverse of heat engine.
>
> ✅ **Four Processes:**
> - 1→2: Isentropic compression ($T_L \to T_H$)
> - 2→3: Isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$
> - 3→4: Isentropic expansion ($T_H \to T_L$)
> - 4→1: Isothermal heat absorption at $T_L$
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
> - $COP_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — always use **Kelvin**
> - $COP_{HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$
> - $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (universal)
> - $Q_H = Q_L + W$ (First Law energy balance)
> - $Q_H/Q_L = T_H/T_L$ (Reversible cycle, Second Law)
>
> ✅ **Refrigerator** → wants $Q_L$ (cooling), uses $COP_R$.
>
> ✅ **Heat Pump** → wants $Q_H$ (heating), uses $COP_{HP}$.
>
> ✅ **Refrigeration Machine** = industrial-scale refrigerator.
>
> ✅ **Five Limitations:** Wet compression; Throttling vs isentropic expansion; Isothermal processes unrealizable; Two-phase fluid; Infinite heat exchanger area.
>
> ✅ **Second-Law Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot} \times 100\%$
>
> ✅ **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** = 3.517 kW (standard industrial unit).
>
> ✅ **Reversed Carnot is a theoretical benchmark** — never practically realizable but sets the upper limit of performance.
>
> ✅ **Real cycles** (Vapour Compression — Module 2) achieve **60–80%** of Carnot COP.
>
> ✅ **Heat pump advantage** over electric heating: delivers $COP_{HP}$ units of heat per unit work, where $COP_{HP} > 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Units of Refrigeration: Definition and Practical Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Units of Refrigeration: Definition and Practical Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Refrigeration and Air Conditioning**, the **"Unit of Refrigeration"** is defined as a standardized measure of the **rate of heat removal (cooling capacity)** from a space or system. Refrigeration is fundamentally a *heat-pumping* operation, and since the "useful effect" is the extraction of thermal energy, every refrigeration system must be rated by the *quantity of heat it can absorb per unit time*.

The standard, universally accepted unit is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**, supplemented by several secondary and SI units depending on regional engineering practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> A refrigeration "unit" is a *rate* quantity, NOT an energy quantity. It is expressed in terms of energy per unit time (e.g., kW, BTU/hr, kcal/hr). Students must never confuse "Ton" (rate) with "BTU" or "kJ" (total energy).

## 1.2 The Six Major Units of Refrigeration

| Sl. No. | Unit Name | Symbol | System / Origin |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Ton of Refrigeration | **TR** | American/Imperial (Practical) |
| 2 | British Thermal Unit per hour | **BTU/hr** | Imperial (Energy-rate) |
| 3 | Kilo-calorie per hour | **kcal/hr** | CGS/Metric (Energy-rate) |
| 4 | Watt / Kilo-watt | **W / kW** | SI Unit (Universal) |
| 5 | Horse-power (mechanical) | **HP** | Imperial (Mechanical) |
| 6 | Joule (for total heat quantity) | **J** | SI (Total Energy) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "What is a Unit of Refrigeration?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition (Tap-Water Analogy):**
> 
> Think of a refrigeration system as a **bucket with a hole at the bottom** that continuously drains water. The "refrigeration effect" is the *rate* at which water is being drained (liters/minute), NOT the *total water drained in a day*.
> 
> In the same way, a 1 TR air conditioner is one that "drains" **3.516 kJ of heat every second** from a room. A 2 TR AC drains *twice* as fast. The "Ton" simply tells you the *speed* of cooling, not the total heat removed over a period.

## 1.4 Primary Unit: The Ton of Refrigeration (TR)

**Definition:** One Ton of Refrigeration is the amount of refrigeration effect (heat removal rate) required to **freeze (or melt) 1 short ton (2000 lb ≈ 907.185 kg) of pure water ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**, with the water initially at 0 °C.

- **Latent heat of fusion of ice:** **144 BTU/lb** (≈ 335 kJ/kg)
- **Standard mass used:** **1 short ton = 2000 lb** (US convention)

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** This unit emerged in the 19th century when natural ice was harvested from frozen lakes and stored in insulated warehouses. The "daily ice consumption" of an ice-house became the natural benchmark for sizing mechanical refrigeration equipment, hence the name "Ton of Refrigeration" was carried forward even into modern vapor-compression systems.

## 1.5 The Six Conversion Constants (Must Memorize)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
1\ \text{TR} &= 200\ \text{BTU/min} \\
1\ \text{TR} &= 12{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr} \\
1\ \text{TR} &= 3.516\ \text{kW} \\
1\ \text{TR} &= 3516\ \text{W} \\
1\ \text{TR} &= 210\ \text{kJ/min} \\
1\ \text{TR} &= 3.024 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cal/hr}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

## 1.6 Other Secondary Units (Detailed)

### 1.6.1 British Thermal Unit (BTU) & BTU per hour
- 1 BTU = heat required to raise the temperature of **1 pound of water by 1 °F** (between 59 °F and 60 °F).
- $1\ \text{BTU} = 1055.06\ \text{J} \approx 1.055\ \text{kJ}$

### 1.6.2 Kilo-calorie (kcal) & kcal per hour
- 1 kcal = heat required to raise **1 kg of water by 1 °C**.
- $1\ \text{kcal} = 3.968\ \text{BTU} = 4186.8\ \text{J}$

### 1.6.3 Watt / Kilo-watt (SI)
- $1\ \text{kW} = 3412.14\ \text{BTU/hr} \approx 3412\ \text{BTU/hr}$
- $1\ \text{HP} = 2544.43\ \text{BTU/hr} \approx 2545\ \text{BTU/hr}$

### 1.6.4 Horse-power (HP)
- $1\ \text{HP} = 745.7\ \text{W} = 0.7457\ \text{kW}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Derivation of the Ton of Refrigeration

The derivation of the standard "Ton" originates from first principles of thermodynamics (latent heat of fusion) and unit conversion between Imperial and SI systems.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Mass of ice taken as reference:** $m = 1\ \text{short ton} = 2000\ \text{lb}$
2. **Latent heat of fusion of ice:** $h_{fg,\text{ice}} = 144\ \text{BTU/lb}$
3. **Total heat absorbed in 24 hours (melting 1 ton of ice):**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = m \times h_{fg} = 2000\ \text{lb} \times 144\ \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{lb}} = 288{,}000\ \text{BTU}
$$

4. **Rate of heat removal per hour (dividing by 24):**

$$
Q_{\text{rate}} = \frac{288{,}000\ \text{BTU}}{24\ \text{hr}} = 12{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr}
$$

5. **Converting to SI (kW):** Using $1\ \text{kW} = 3412.14\ \text{BTU/hr}$,

$$
1\ \text{TR} = \frac{12{,}000}{3412.14} = 3.5168\ \text{kW} \approx 3.516\ \text{kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** Always carry the 24-hour conversion correctly. A very common error is to forget that the "ton" is a *24-hour average* rate, leading to factors of 24 missing in the final answer.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Formula / Conversion | Numerical Value |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 TR in BTU/hr | $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr}$ | **12,000** |
| 1 TR in BTU/min | $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000 / 60$ | **200** |
| 1 TR in kW | $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000 / 3412.14$ | **3.516** |
| 1 TR in kJ/min | $1\ \text{TR} = 3.516 \times 60$ | **210.96** |
| 1 TR in kcal/hr | $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000 / 3.968$ | **3,024** |
| 1 kW in TR | $1\ \text{kW} = 1/3.516$ | **0.2843** |
| 1 HP in TR | $1\ \text{HP} = 0.7457/3.516$ | **0.212** |
| 1 kcal/hr in BTU/hr | $1\ \text{kcal/hr} = 3.968\ \text{BTU/hr}$ | **3.968** |
| 1 HP in kW | $1\ \text{HP}$ | **0.7457** |
| Latent heat of fusion of ice | $h_{fg}$ | **144 BTU/lb = 335 kJ/kg** |
| Specific heat of water (Imperial) | $c_p$ | **1 BTU/lb·°F** |
| 1 BTU in kJ | $1\ \text{BTU} \times 1.055$ | **1.055 kJ** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These conversion factors are used daily by HVAC engineers while *sizing chillers, computing cooling loads for cold storage, and selecting compressors*. The choice of unit depends purely on the *manufacturer's data-sheet conventions* — for example, American chiller manufacturers quote capacity in "Tons" whereas European manufacturers quote in "kW."

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the "Ton" — Practical Significance

- **Industrial Cold Storage:** A medium-sized cold storage for fruits may require **50–100 TR** of refrigeration capacity.
- **Food Processing:** A poultry/meat processing plant may demand **200–500 TR**.
- **District Cooling:** Large district cooling plants range from **5,000–20,000 TR**.
- **Cryogenics (LNG, medical):** Capacities often exceed **10,000 TR**.

The use of a "ton" is a *legacy industrial standard* that allows engineers worldwide to communicate capacity without ambiguity, regardless of whether the underlying power source is a vapor-compression cycle, an absorption cycle, or even an evaporative cooling system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — 1 TR = 3.516 kW (Full Imperial → SI Bridge)

We will derive, *step by step without skipping*, the exact numerical value of 1 Ton of Refrigeration in kilo-watts.

### Given Data
- Latent heat of fusion of ice: $h_{fg} = 144\ \text{BTU/lb}$
- Mass of 1 short ton: $m = 2000\ \text{lb}$
- Time period: $t = 24\ \text{hr}$
- Standard conversion: $1\ \text{BTU} = 1055.06\ \text{J}$, hence $1\ \text{BTU/hr} = 1055.06/3600\ \text{W} = 0.29307\ \text{W}$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute total heat absorbed in melting 1 ton of ice at 0 °C in 24 hr:**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot h_{fg} = 2000 \times 144
$$

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 288{,}000\ \text{BTU}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert total heat into kilo-joules (so we move from Imperial to SI):**

Using $1\ \text{BTU} = 1.05506\ \text{kJ}$,

$$
Q_{\text{total, kJ}} = 288{,}000 \times 1.05506
$$

$$
Q_{\text{total, kJ}} = 303{,}857.28\ \text{kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the *rate* of heat removal (heat per hour):**

$$
Q_{\text{rate, kJ/hr}} = \frac{303{,}857.28}{24}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{rate, kJ/hr}} = 12{,}660.72\ \text{kJ/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert kJ/hr to kW (since 1 kW = 1 kJ/s = 3600 kJ/hr):**

$$
1\ \text{TR} = \frac{12{,}660.72}{3600}\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
1\ \text{TR} = 3.5168\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{1\ \text{TR} \approx 3.516\ \text{kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "magic number" 3.516 is therefore *not arbitrary* — it is a direct consequence of converting the *latent heat of fusion of ice* from Imperial (BTU/lb) to SI (kJ/kg) units, then dividing by 24 hours and 3600 seconds.

## 3.2 Derivation — 1 TR in kcal/hr

$$
Q_{\text{rate, kJ/hr}} = 12{,}660.72\ \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Since $1\ \text{kcal} = 4.1868\ \text{kJ}$,

$$
1\ \text{TR} = \frac{12{,}660.72}{4.1868}\ \text{kcal/hr}
$$

$$
1\ \text{TR} = 3{,}024\ \text{kcal/hr}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation — 1 HP ≈ 0.212 TR

$$
1\ \text{HP} = 0.7457\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
1\ \text{TR} = 3.516\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow 1\ \text{HP} = \frac{0.7457}{3.516} = 0.2120\ \text{TR}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A small commercial cold storage removes heat at a rate of 5.0 TR. Express this capacity in (a) kW, (b) BTU/hr, and (c) kcal/min.

### Solution

**(a) In kW:**

$$
\text{Capacity} = 5.0 \times 3.516 = 17.58\ \text{kW}
$$

**(b) In BTU/hr:**

$$
\text{Capacity} = 5.0 \times 12{,}000 = 60{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr}
$$

**(c) In kcal/min:**

First, in kcal/hr:

$$
\text{Capacity} = 5.0 \times 3{,}024 = 15{,}120\ \text{kcal/hr}
$$

Then convert to kcal/min:

$$
\text{Capacity} = \frac{15{,}120}{60} = 252\ \text{kcal/min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Verification Trick:** 1 TR ≈ 50 kcal/min. So 5 TR ≈ 250 kcal/min. ✓ (Our exact answer 252 kcal/min matches within rounding.)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Practical Examples of Refrigeration Capacities

| Application / System | Typical Refrigeration Capacity | Approx. kW Equivalent |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Domestic refrigerator (single-door) | 0.10 – 0.20 TR | 0.35 – 0.70 kW |
| Domestic frost-free refrigerator | 0.25 – 0.50 TR | 0.88 – 1.76 kW |
| Window air-conditioner (1.0 TR) | 1.0 TR | 3.516 kW |
| Split air-conditioner (1.5 TR) | 1.5 TR | 5.27 kW |
| Central AC for a 2000 sq.ft home | 3 – 5 TR | 10.5 – 17.6 kW |
| Pharmaceutical cold room (small) | 5 – 10 TR | 17.5 – 35.2 kW |
| Commercial ice plant (5 ton/day) | 10 – 20 TR | 35 – 70 kW |
| Medium cold storage (fruits/vegetables) | 50 – 100 TR | 176 – 352 kW |
| Industrial meat processing plant | 200 – 500 TR | 703 – 1758 kW |
| District cooling plant (large) | 5,000 – 20,000 TR | 17,580 – 70,320 kW |
| LNG liquefaction plant | 50,000+ TR | 175,800+ kW |
| Ice skating rink (Olympic size) | 200 – 350 TR | 703 – 1,231 kW |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Context:** An "Olympic-sized ice rink" continuously extracts heat from a 30 m × 60 m slab of ice at roughly 250–350 TR, illustrating how the *same* "Ton" unit scales gracefully from a tiny domestic fridge to a multi-megawatt industrial installation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Conversion Flow Between Refrigeration Units

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the *inter-conversion flow* between the six major refrigeration units, anchored on the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** as the central reference.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Primary Anchor: 1 Ton of Refrigeration TR"] --> B["Imperial Rate: 12000 BTU per hr"]
    A --> C["SI Power: 3.516 kW"]
    A --> D["Metric Heat Rate: 3024 kcal per hr"]
    A --> E["Mechanical HP: 0.2843 TR per kW"]
    A --> F["Energy Rate: 210 kJ per min"]

    B --> G["Base Definition: 2000 lb ice melted in 24 hr"]
    G --> H["Latent Heat: 144 BTU per lb of ice"]
    H --> I["Total: 288000 BTU in 24 hr"]

    C --> J["SI Conversion: 1 kW equals 3412 BTU per hr"]
    D --> K["Metric: 1 kcal equals 3.968 BTU"]
    F --> L["Time-base: 1 min equals 60 s"]

    M["Secondary Reference: 1 Horse Power HP"] --> N["1 HP equals 745.7 W"]
    M --> O["1 HP equals 0.212 TR"]

    P["Practical Capacity Ranges"] --> Q["Domestic: 0.1 to 0.5 TR"]
    P --> R["Window and Split AC: 1 to 5 TR"]
    P --> S["Cold Storage: 50 to 100 TR"]
    P --> T["Industrial Plant: 200 to 500 TR"]
    P --> U["District Cooling: 5000 to 20000 TR"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagram:**
> - The *central anchor* is **1 TR**, from which all conversions branch out.
> - The *right side* shows practical industrial capacities to give the student a "feel" of the scale involved.
> - Reading the diagram from left to right, top to bottom, demonstrates the *chain of reasoning* behind every conversion.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Practical Examples Mapped to Capacity Ranges

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Domestic Fridge 0.2 TR"] --> B1["Residential AC 1.0 to 2.0 TR"]
    B1 --> C1["Office AC 5 to 10 TR"]
    C1 --> D1["Cold Storage 50 to 100 TR"]
    D1 --> E1["Food Plant 200 to 500 TR"]
    E1 --> F1["District Cooling 5000+ TR"]
    F1 --> G1["LNG Plant 50000+ TR"]

    A1 -.comment.-> X1["Capacity grows by 10x per stage"]
    F1 -.comment.-> Y1["Same unit TR applies at all scales"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Ton of Refrigeration". How is it related to kW and BTU/hr?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze (or melt) **1 short ton (2000 lb) of pure water ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.

Standard conversions:
- $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr}$
- $1\ \text{TR} = 3.516\ \text{kW}$

It originates from the latent heat of fusion of ice, which is **144 BTU/lb**.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any four units of refrigeration and state the SI unit.**

**Model Answer:**

Four common units of refrigeration are:
1. **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** — $1\ \text{TR} = 3.516\ \text{kW}$
2. **kilo-calorie per hour (kcal/hr)** — $1\ \text{TR} = 3024\ \text{kcal/hr}$
3. **British Thermal Unit per hour (BTU/hr)** — $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr}$
4. **Horse-power (HP)** — $1\ \text{HP} = 0.7457\ \text{kW}$

The **SI unit of refrigeration capacity is the Watt (W) or kilo-Watt (kW)**, since refrigeration capacity is a *rate of heat removal* (power).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each (or three sub-parts totaling 14, as per KTU 2024 scheme). The student must answer *one* of the two choices.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the relationship between 1 Ton of Refrigeration and kilo-watt (kW) starting from the definition of "Ton".** (7 Marks)

**(b) A small pharmaceutical cold-storage unit is rated at 8.5 TR. Convert this capacity into (i) kW, (ii) BTU/hr, (iii) kcal/hr, and (iv) HP. Comment on what this capacity is typically used for.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of 1 TR = 3.516 kW**

**Step 1:** A "Ton of Refrigeration" is defined as the heat required to melt 1 short ton (2000 lb) of ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.

**Step 2:** Total heat absorbed,

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 2000\ \text{lb} \times 144\ \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{lb}} = 288{,}000\ \text{BTU}
$$

**Step 3:** Rate of heat removal per hour,

$$
Q_{\text{rate}} = \frac{288{,}000}{24} = 12{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert BTU/hr to kW. Using $1\ \text{BTU/hr} = 0.29307\ \text{W}$,

$$
1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000 \times 0.29307\ \text{W} = 3516.84\ \text{W} = 3.5168\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{1\ \text{TR} \approx 3.516\ \text{kW}}
$$

**(b) Conversion of 8.5 TR**

**(i) In kW:**

$$
8.5 \times 3.516 = 29.886\ \text{kW} \approx 29.9\ \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) In BTU/hr:**

$$
8.5 \times 12{,}000 = 1{,}02{,}000\ \text{BTU/hr} = 1.02 \times 10^{5}\ \text{BTU/hr}
$$

**(iii) In kcal/hr:**

$$
8.5 \times 3{,}024 = 25{,}704\ \text{kcal/hr}
$$

**(iv) In HP:**

$$
8.5 \times 0.212 = 1.802\ \text{HP}
$$

**Comment:** A capacity of ~8.5 TR (≈ 30 kW) is typically used for **small pharmaceutical cold-storage rooms** storing vaccines, blood-plasma, and temperature-sensitive medicines at 2–8 °C. Such a unit would also suffice for a small restaurant's walk-in freezer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the historical origin of the unit "Ton of Refrigeration". Why is it still used in the refrigeration industry instead of the SI unit kW?** (7 Marks)

**(b) An industrial cold-storage plant has a refrigeration load of 250 kW. Express this load in (i) Tons of Refrigeration, (ii) BTU/hr, (iii) kcal/min, and (iv) HP. List any two real-world applications that fall in this capacity range.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Historical Origin & Continued Use of "Ton of Refrigeration"**

**Origin:** In the 19th century, the principal method of artificial cooling was the **harvesting of natural ice** from frozen lakes during winter. Ice was stored in insulated "ice-houses" for use throughout the year. The natural benchmark of cooling capacity was the **amount of ice consumed per day**, which became the basis of the "Ton of Refrigeration."

**Why it persists:**

1. **Practical & Intuitive:** It directly relates to a *physical quantity* (mass of ice) that field engineers can visualize, unlike the abstract SI watt.
2. **Industry Inertia:** Equipment manufacturers in the USA, Middle East, India, and SE Asia continue to rate chillers, packaged AC units, and cold rooms in TR. Replacing it globally would require re-labeling millions of data-sheets.
3. **Easy Sizing:** Engineers in the food/cold-chain industry compute cooling loads in TR using empirical thumb-rules (e.g., *W/m² of cold storage*), making TR-based sizing seamless.
4. **SI Compatibility:** Despite being non-SI, 1 TR is *exactly* and *unambiguously* defined in kW (3.516 kW), so it is fully SI-compatible.

**(b) Conversion of 250 kW**

**(i) In TR:**

$$
250\ \text{kW} \times \frac{1\ \text{TR}}{3.516\ \text{kW}} = 71.10\ \text{TR}
$$

**(ii) In BTU/hr:**

$$
250\ \text{kW} \times 3412.14\ \frac{\text{BTU/hr}}{\text{kW}} = 8{,}53{,}035\ \text{BTU/hr} \approx 8.53 \times 10^{5}\ \text{BTU/hr}
$$

**(iii) In kcal/min:**

First, kcal/hr: $250 \times 860 = 2{,}15{,}000\ \text{kcal/hr}$ (using 1 kW = 860 kcal/hr)

Then per minute: $\dfrac{2{,}15{,}000}{60} = 3{,}583.3\ \text{kcal/min}$

**(iv) In HP:**

$$
250\ \text{kW} \times \frac{1\ \text{HP}}{0.7457\ \text{kW}} = 335.25\ \text{HP}
$$

**Two real-world applications in this 70–100 TR range:**

1. **Medium-sized cold storage** for fruits, vegetables, and dairy products (≈ 100–500 metric-ton storage capacity).
2. **Process cooling in a medium dairy plant** (milk chilling to 4 °C before pasteurization).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 24-hour factor:** Many students use the *total* heat of fusion (288,000 BTU) and forget to divide by 24. This leads to an answer of 84.4 kW instead of 3.516 kW — a full-mark deduction.
> 
> 2. **Confusing "TR" with "Ton" of weight:** TR is a *cooling rate*, not a mass. Never write "1 TR = 2000 lb" — the correct statement is "1 TR = heat to melt 2000 lb of ice in 24 hr."
> 
> 3. **Wrong use of mechanical HP:** Some students use **metric HP (1 HP = 735.5 W)** instead of **mechanical HP (1 HP = 745.7 W)**. KTU board questions expect **mechanical HP = 745.7 W** unless otherwise specified.
> 
> 4. **Unit-mixing errors:** In conversion problems, always carry units through every line of the calculation. Marks are often deducted for unlabelled intermediate results.
> 
> 5. **Rounding too early:** Do not round 3.516 to 3.5 in mid-calculation. Use 3.51685 (or the exact 12,000/3412.14) until the final step, then round to 3 decimal places.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Module 1 / Topic: Units of Refrigeration**

- **Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** the *rate* of heat removal needed to freeze 1 short ton (2000 lb) of ice at 0 °C in 24 hours.
- **Latent heat of fusion of ice:** **144 BTU/lb** = **335 kJ/kg** — remember this for all derivations.
- **Master the six conversion factors** (3.516 kW, 12,000 BTU/hr, 200 BTU/min, 210 kJ/min, 3024 kcal/hr, 0.2843 TR per kW).
- **1 BTU = 1.055 kJ** ; **1 kcal = 3.968 BTU** ; **1 HP = 745.7 W** ; **1 kW = 3412 BTU/hr**.
- **Always** remember to **divide total heat by 24** when converting "1 Ton of ice in 24 hours" to a *rate*.
- The **SI unit** of refrigeration capacity is the **Watt (W)** or **kilo-Watt (kW)**.
- **TR is rate, not energy** — never substitute "TR" where a "BTU" or "kJ" is required, and vice versa.
- **Capacity ranges to memorize:**
    - Domestic fridge ≈ 0.2 TR
    - Window AC ≈ 1.0 TR
    - Split AC ≈ 1.5–2.0 TR
    - Cold storage ≈ 50–100 TR
    - Industrial plant ≈ 200–500 TR
    - District cooling ≈ 5,000+ TR
- **Practical use of "Ton":** Used in rating chiller capacities, computing HVAC cooling loads, sizing compressors, and selecting evaporators in industrial refrigeration systems.
- **Examiner's trap:** In conversion problems, *show every step with units*. Marks are awarded for method, not just the final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Refrigeration Systems: Reversed Joule Cycle, Aircraft Refrigeration Systems (Simple, Bootstrap, Regenerative, and Reduced Ambient Systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Refrigeration Systems: Reversed Joule Cycle, Aircraft Refrigeration Systems (Simple, Bootstrap, Regenerative, and Reduced Ambient Systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Reversed Joule Cycle (Bell-Coleman Cycle)

The **Reversed Joule Cycle**, also known historically as the **Bell-Coleman Cycle**, is the thermodynamic cycle obtained when a **gas (typically air)** is taken through a closed sequence of two isentropic and two isobaric processes in the reverse direction of the Joule/Brayton power cycle. It serves as the theoretical foundation for **all open-cycle air refrigeration systems**, especially those used in aircraft.

The four ideal processes are:
1. **Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression** in a compressor
2. **Isobaric heat rejection** to an external sink
3. **Isentropic expansion** in an expansion turbine (or throttling device)
4. **Isobaric heat absorption** in the refrigerated space

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box:** *A reversed Joule cycle is a theoretical gas refrigeration cycle in which a perfect gas (most commonly air) undergoes isentropic compression, isobaric cooling, isentropic expansion, and isobaric heating — traversed in the counter-clockwise direction on a P-V or T-S diagram to produce a net refrigeration effect.*

### 1.2 Aircraft Refrigeration Systems — Definition

**Aircraft refrigeration systems** are open air-cycle refrigeration systems designed to condition the cabin and avionics compartments of an aircraft at cruising altitudes (typically 9–13 km) where ambient temperatures can drop to **−50 °C** but solar radiation, electronic heat loads, and passenger metabolic heat require active cooling. Unlike vapor compression cycles, these systems use **air itself as the refrigerant** and rely on the ram-air effect from forward flight motion for heat rejection.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Lung of the Airplane"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Hold your hand a few inches from your mouth and blow slowly: the air feels warm. Now purse your lips and blow hard through a small opening — the air feels cool. What just happened?
> 
> 1. Your diaphragm **compressed** the air (got hot)
> 2. The cold room air **cooled** it
> 3. The narrow opening made it **expand rapidly** (got cold)
> 4. The cold air **cooled** your hand
> 
> An aircraft ECS does exactly this — **compress → cool → expand → cool the cabin**. The cold cabin air is then dumped overboard, and fresh ram air takes its place. Hence the term "open cycle."

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat of air at constant pressure**, $C_p = \mathbf{1.005 \; kJ/kg \cdot K}$
- **Ratio of specific heats**, $\gamma = \mathbf{1.4}$
- **Gas constant for air**, $R = \mathbf{0.287 \; kJ/kg \cdot K}$
- **Isentropic exponent**, $\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} = \mathbf{0.2857}$
- **Standard sea-level pressure**, $P_0 = \mathbf{101.325 \; kPa}$
- **Cruise altitude pressure**, ~ **23–35 kPa** depending on flight level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic carries **substantial weightage in Module 1** of PEIET412 and forms the conceptual base for industrial/commercial refrigeration. The four system variants (Simple, Bootstrap, Regenerative, Reduced Ambient) are **mandatory** for Part-B questions and frequently appear in Part-A as comparison questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Cycle Breakdown (Reversed Joule)

Let us define the four state points on the cycle:

**State 1 → State 2: Isentropic Compression**
- Air enters the **main compressor** at cabin pressure $P_1$ and temperature $T_1$.
- Compressed to higher pressure $P_2$; temperature rises to $T_2$.
- **Work input**: $W_c = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$
- **Isentropic relation**: $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$

**State 2 → State 3: Isobaric Heat Rejection**
- Hot compressed air passes through the **main cooler** (often a ram-air heat exchanger).
- Heat $q_{out}$ is rejected to ambient; temperature drops to $T_3$ at constant pressure $P_2 = P_3$.

**State 3 → State 4: Isentropic Expansion**
- Cooled, high-pressure air expands in the **expansion turbine**.
- Temperature drops dramatically to $T_4$ (the coldest point — air supplied to cabin).
- **Work output**: $W_e = C_p(T_3 - T_4)$
- **Isentropic relation**: $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$

**State 4 → State 1: Isobaric Heat Absorption**
- Cold air passes through the **cabin cooling coil**, absorbing the refrigeration load $q_{in}$.
- Temperature rises from $T_4$ back to $T_1$ at $P_4 = P_1$.
- **Refrigeration Effect (RE)**: $q_{in} = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$

### 2.2 P-V and T-S Diagram Analysis

- **P-V Diagram**: Two isentropes (steep curves 1-2 and 3-4) and two isobars (horizontal lines 2-3 at high $P$ and 4-1 at low $P$). Cycle traversed **counter-clockwise**.
- **T-S Diagram**: Isentropes appear as **vertical lines**, isobars as curves sloping upward to the right. The enclosed area represents net refrigeration effect.

### 2.3 The Four Aircraft System Variants — Overview

**(A) Simple System** — Direct configuration with one main compressor, one main cooler, and one expander. Pressure ratio limited to **3–4**.

**(B) Bootstrap System** — Adds a **secondary compressor** (driven by the expander) and a secondary cooler. Allows compound pressure ratios up to **6–8**, achieving much lower delivery temperatures.

**(C) Regenerative System** — Adds a **regenerative heat exchanger (recuperator)** between cabin return air and expander exhaust air. Increases RE without adding work.

**(D) Reduced Ambient System** — Places a **pre-cooling expander** *before* the main compressor, lowering compressor inlet temperature and thereby reducing $W_c$. Ideal for hot, ground-level conditions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All temperatures in **Kelvin**. $C_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. $\gamma$ = 1.4. Exponent $\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$ = **0.2857** for air.

| Parameter | Simple System | Bootstrap System | Regenerative System | Reduced Ambient System |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Compressor Work** $W_c$ | $C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ | $C_p(T_2' - T_7)$ |
| **Expander Work** $W_e$ | $C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | $C_p(T_5 - T_6)$ | $C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ | $C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ |
| **Net Work** $W_{net}$ | $W_c - W_e$ | $W_c - W_e$ | $W_c - W_e$ | $W_c - W_e$ |
| **Refrigeration Effect** $RE$ | $C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ | $C_p(T_1 - T_6)$ | $C_p(T_4' - T_4)$ | $C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ |
| **COP** | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$ | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_6}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_5 - T_6)}$ | $\dfrac{T_4' - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$ | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2' - T_7) - (T_3 - T_4)}$ |
| **Pressure Ratio $r_p$** | $P_2 / P_1$ | Compound: main $\times$ secondary | $P_2 / P_1$ | Modified with pre-expansion |
| **Typical $r_p$ Range** | 3 – 4 | 6 – 8 | 4 – 6 | 3 – 5 |

**Universal Relations:**

$$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} \qquad ; \qquad \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{\,0.2857}$$

**Useful Numerical Values of $r_p^{0.2857}$:**

- $r_p = 2$: **1.219**
- $r_p = 3$: **1.369**
- $r_p = 4$: **1.486**
- $r_p = 5$: **1.584**
- $r_p = 8$: **1.811**

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation Industry**: All modern commercial jets (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) use a **combination of Bootstrap + Regenerative systems** in their Environmental Control Systems (ECS).
- **Defense Aircraft**: Military jets in tropical climates use **Reduced Ambient systems** for ground cooling.
- **Industrial Applications**: Gas pipeline cooling, turbine inlet air chilling, and dry-ice manufacturing are based on reversed Joule cycle principles.
- **Environmental Edge**: Air as refrigerant has **ODP = 0** and **GWP = 0**, making it the most eco-friendly working fluid — especially relevant under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of COP for Reversed Joule Cycle

Apply **SFEE (Steady Flow Energy Equation)** to each component, neglecting KE and PE changes.

**Step 1 — Compressor Work (Process 1→2):**
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$$

**Step 2 — Heat Rejected in Cooler (Process 2→3):**
$$q_{rej} = h_2 - h_3 = C_p(T_2 - T_3)$$

**Step 3 — Expander Work (Process 3→4):**
$$w_e = h_3 - h_4 = C_p(T_3 - T_4)$$

**Step 4 — Heat Absorbed in Cabin (Process 4→1):**
$$q_{abs} = h_1 - h_4 = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$$

**Step 5 — Net Work of Cycle:**
$$\begin{aligned}
w_{net} &= w_c - w_e \\
&= C_p(T_2 - T_1) - C_p(T_3 - T_4)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Coefficient of Performance (Definition):**
$$\begin{aligned}
COP &= \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{q_{abs}}{w_{net}} \\
&= \frac{C_p(T_1 - T_4)}{C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]}
\end{aligned}$$

After cancellation of $C_p$:
$$\boxed{\;COP_{RJC} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Isentropic Relations:**

For isentropic processes 1-2 and 3-4 with $P_2 = P_3$ and $P_1 = P_4$:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \quad ; \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

Since $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4} = r_p$, we obtain the elegant result:
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{\,0.2857}$$

This can be rearranged to give:
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} \quad ; \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r_p^{0.2857}$$

### 3.2 Detailed Analysis — Simple Aircraft Refrigeration System

**Process Sequence:** Cabin return air → Main Compressor → Main Cooler (ram air) → Expansion Turbine → Cabin Cooling Coil → (back to state 1)

**Key Equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{compressor} &= C_p(T_2 - T_1) \\
W_{expander} &= C_p(T_3 - T_4) \\
W_{net} &= C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] \\
RE &= C_p(T_1 - T_4) \\
COP_{simple} &= \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Limitation:** Pressure ratio is bounded by cabin temperature. To achieve low $T_4$ (deep cooling), the air entering the cabin must be very cold, requiring either high $r_p$ or very low $T_3$, both of which are physically limited.

### 3.3 Detailed Analysis — Bootstrap System

**Process Sequence:** Cabin → Main Comp → Main Cooler → **Secondary Comp** (driven by expander) → Secondary Cooler → Expander → Cabin

**Shaft-Energy Balance on Expander–Secondary Compressor Coupling:**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{secondary,comp} &= W_{expander} \\
C_p(T_4 - T_3) &= C_p(T_5 - T_6)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $T_4 - T_3 = T_5 - T_6$

**System Equations:**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{main,c} &= C_p(T_2 - T_1) \\
W_{secondary,c} &= C_p(T_4 - T_3) = C_p(T_5 - T_6) \\
W_{expander} &= C_p(T_5 - T_6) \\
W_{net} &= C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_5 - T_6)] \\
RE &= C_p(T_1 - T_6)
\end{aligned}$$

**Why it works:** The expander does **no external work** — its energy drives the secondary compressor. This means the cycle achieves an **effective compound pressure ratio** at the cost of only the main compressor's work.

### 3.4 Detailed Analysis — Regenerative System

**Process Sequence:** Cabin → Regenerator (hot side) → Main Comp → Main Cooler → Expander → Regenerator (cold side) → Cabin

Let $T_1$ = cabin temperature, $T_4$ = expander exit temperature. After regeneration:
- Cabin return air cools from $T_1 \to T_1'$ in regenerator (hot side)
- Expander exit air warms from $T_4 \to T_4'$ in regenerator (cold side)

**Regenerator Effectiveness:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_4' - T_4}{T_1 - T_4}$$

**Refrigeration Effect (now using $T_4'$, not $T_4$):**
$$RE_{regen} = C_p(T_4' - T_4)$$

**Net Work:**
$$W_{net} = C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]$$

Note that $T_1$ here is now the **post-regenerator** temperature, slightly lower than cabin temperature, which **reduces compressor work**.

**COP:**
$$COP_{regen} = \frac{T_4' - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}$$

### 3.5 Detailed Analysis — Reduced Ambient System

**Process Sequence:** Cabin → **Pre-expander** → Main Comp → Main Cooler → Main Expander → Cabin

The pre-expander drops the temperature from $T_1$ to $T_7$ *before* the compressor. This is the key innovation.

**System Equations:**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{pre\text{-}expander} &= C_p(T_1 - T_7) \quad \text{[work recovered, may drive auxiliaries]} \\
W_{main,comp} &= C_p(T_2' - T_7) \quad \text{[note: starts from } T_7, \text{ not } T_1] \\
W_{main,exp} &= C_p(T_3 - T_4) \\
W_{net} &= C_p[(T_2' - T_7) - (T_3 - T_4)] \\
RE &= C_p(T_1 - T_4)
\end{aligned}$$

**Crucial Observation:** Since $T_7 < T_1$, the value of $T_2'$ is significantly less than $T_2$ would be in a simple system. This is what reduces compressor work.

**COP:**
$$COP_{RA} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2' - T_7) - (T_3 - T_4)}$$

### 3.6 Illustrative Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement:** A simple aircraft refrigeration system operates between cabin pressure 1.0 bar and maximum pressure 4.0 bar. The cabin air temperature is $27°C$ and ram air temperature is $17°C$. Take $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. Calculate:
(i) Temperatures $T_2$, $T_3$, $T_4$
(ii) Net work per kg of air
(iii) Refrigeration effect
(iv) COP of the system

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 27 + 273 = 300 \; K$
- $T_4 = T_{ram} = 17 + 273 = 290 \; K$
- $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = 4 / 1 = 4$
- $r_p^{0.2857} = 4^{0.2857} = 1.486$

**Step 1: Compute $T_2$**
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 300 \times 1.486 = 445.8 \; K$$

**Step 2: Compute $T_3$**
$$T_3 = T_4 \cdot r_p^{0.2857} = 290 \times 1.486 = 430.9 \; K$$

**Step 3: Net Work**
$$\begin{aligned}
W_{net} &= C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] \\
&= 1.005 \times [(445.8 - 300) - (430.9 - 290)] \\
&= 1.005 \times [145.8 - 140.9] \\
&= 1.005 \times 4.9 = 4.92 \; kJ/kg
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Refrigeration Effect**
$$RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (300 - 290) = 10.05 \; kJ/kg$$

**Step 5: COP**
$$COP = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{10.05}{4.92} = 2.04$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $T_2$ | 445.8 K (172.8 °C) |
| $T_3$ | 430.9 K (157.9 °C) |
| $T_4$ | 290.0 K (17.0 °C) |
| $W_{net}$ | 4.92 kJ/kg |
| $RE$ | 10.05 kJ/kg |
| $COP$ | **2.04** |
