## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are typically **definitional, formula-based, or short derivations**. Model answers should be concise (3–4 lines) but technically accurate.

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. Show that $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat removed from the cold space) to the work input to the compressor:

$$
COP_R = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** From the 1st law applied to the cyclic system:

$$
q_H = RE + W_{comp}
$$

For a heat pump:

$$
COP_{HP} = \dfrac{q_H}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{RE + W_{comp}}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{RE}{W_{comp}} + 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\,COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1\,}
$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the effect of subcooling on the COP of a vapor compression refrigeration system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Subcooling refers to cooling the liquid refrigerant below its saturation temperature at the condenser pressure. **(1 mark)**

- The enthalpy of liquid at the expansion valve inlet decreases from $h_3$ to $h_3' = h_3 - c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sc}$.
- Since $h_4 = h_3$ (throttling), the new $h_4' = h_3'$ is also lower.
- The refrigeration effect $RE = h_1 - h_4$ **increases** because $h_4$ decreases. **(1 mark)**
- The compressor work $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ is **unchanged** because the evaporator side is unaffected. **(1 mark)**

Therefore, subcooling **increases the COP** of the refrigerator:

$$
COP_{R,subcooled} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_3'}{h_2 - h_1} > \dfrac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} = COP_{R,simple}
$$

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions for Module 2 typically carry **internal choice**. Below are **TWO fully solved alternative questions** for the same slot.

---

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q.A (a) [7 marks]** Draw the simple vapor compression refrigeration cycle on **T-s and P-h diagrams**. Label all four state points and the four processes clearly.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**T-s Diagram (3.5 marks):** The T-s diagram of the simple cycle has temperature $T$ on the y-axis and entropy $s$ on the x-axis. The dome-shaped saturation curve separates the subcooled liquid (left), two-phase mixture (inside dome), and superheated vapor (right) regions.

- **State 1:** Saturated vapor at the evaporator pressure $P_L$ — located on the **right boundary (saturated vapor line)** of the dome.
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor at the condenser pressure $P_H$ — located to the **right of the dome**, in the superheat region.
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at $P_H$ — located on the **left boundary (saturated liquid line)** of the dome.
- **State 4:** Low-quality two-phase mixture at $P_L$ — located **inside the dome**, close to the saturated liquid line.

**Processes on T-s diagram:**
- **1 → 2 (Compression):** Vertical straight line (isentropic, $s_1 = s_2$).
- **2 → 3 (Heat Rejection):** A curve starting in the superheat region, then a horizontal line inside the dome (constant-temperature condensation at $T_{cond}$).
- **3 → 4 (Throttling):** Vertical line dropping from saturated liquid line to inside the dome (isenthalpic, but **vertical** is only approximate on T-s; **horizontal** on P-h).
- **4 → 1 (Heat Absorption):** Horizontal line inside the dome (constant-temperature evaporation at $T_{evap}$).

**P-h Diagram (3.5 marks):** The P-h diagram has pressure $P$ (log scale) on the y-axis and enthalpy $h$ on the x-axis. The dome is slightly tilted, opening to the right.

- **State 1:** On the saturated vapor curve at $P_L$.
- **State 2:** In the superheat region at $P_H$.
- **State 3:** On the saturated liquid curve at $P_H$.
- **State 4:** Inside the dome at $P_L$.

**Processes on P-h diagram:**
- **1 → 2 (Compression):** Curve going up and slightly right in the superheat region.
- **2 → 3 (Heat Rejection):** Curve going from superheat region into the dome, then horizontal line inside the dome.
- **3 → 4 (Throttling):** **Perfectly horizontal line** (constant $h = h_3 = h_4$).
- **4 → 1 (Heat Absorption):** Horizontal line inside the dome (constant $P_L$, increasing $h$).

The **area under curve 4 → 1** on the T-s diagram represents the **refrigeration effect in kJ/kg per unit entropy**; on the P-h diagram, the **horizontal distance $h_1 - h_4$** directly equals the refrigeration effect in kJ/kg.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 marks]** A simple VCRS uses R-134a as refrigerant. The refrigerant operates between evaporator temperature $-10$ °C and condenser temperature $40$ °C. The refrigerant is **subcooled by 5 °C** below the condenser saturation temperature before entering the expansion valve. From the property tables:

- At $-10$ °C (sat. vapor): $h_1 = 392$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 1.733$ kJ/(kg·K)
- At $40$ °C (sat. liquid): $h_3 = 256$ kJ/kg
- At $40$ °C, $s_g = 0.900$ kJ/(kg·K) → For isentropic compression, $s_2 = s_1 = 1.733$ kJ/(kg·K)
- At $40$ °C and $s = 1.733$: $h_2 = 430$ kJ/kg (superheated)
- $c_{p,l} = 1.5$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Calculate: (i) COP of the simple cycle without subcooling, (ii) COP with subcooling, (iii) Percentage improvement in COP.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) COP without subcooling (2.5 marks):**

Throttling: $h_4 = h_3 = 256$ kJ/kg

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392 - 256 = 136 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 = 430 - 392 = 38 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
COP_{R,simple} = \dfrac{136}{38} = 3.579
$$

**(ii) COP with subcooling (3 marks):**

$$
h_3' = h_3 - c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sc} = 256 - 1.5 \times 5 = 256 - 7.5 = 248.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_4' = h_3' = 248.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
RE_{new} = h_1 - h_4' = 392 - 248.5 = 143.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{comp,new} = 38 \text{ kJ/kg (unchanged)}
$$

$$
COP_{R,subcooled} = \dfrac{143.5}{38} = 3.776
$$

**(iii) Percentage improvement (1.5 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{3.776 - 3.579}{3.579} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.197}{3.579} \times 100 = 5.50\%
$$

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B (a) [7 marks]** What is meant by **superheating of the refrigerant**? Explain how superheating affects the COP of a simple VCRS. Why is **useful superheating** preferred over **useless superheating**?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Superheating is the process of raising the temperature of the refrigerant vapor **above its saturation temperature** at a given pressure. It occurs when the refrigerant is **fully evaporated** in the evaporator but continues to absorb heat from the surroundings (the load) before leaving.

**Types of Superheating (2 marks):**

1. **Useless Superheating (External):** The vapor is superheated by **absorbing heat from the cold space** (e.g., air being cooled in the evaporator). This is "useless" because the same heat has already been delivered as a useful refrigeration effect; any further heat absorption is a **reduction in net useful cooling**.

2. **Useful Superheating (Internal):** The vapor is superheated by **absorbing heat from a source other than the cold space**, typically through a **Liquid-Suction Heat Exchanger (LSHX)** where heat is taken from the warm liquid leaving the condenser.

**Effect on COP (2.5 marks):**

- **Useless superheating:** Both $h_1$ and $h_2$ increase. While $RE$ increases slightly, $W_{comp}$ increases more. **COP decreases**. The additional heat absorbed from the cold space is a loss, not a benefit.
- **Useful superheating (LSHX):** The vapor entering the compressor is superheated (so $h_1$ increases), but the liquid entering the expansion valve is **simultaneously subcooled** (so $h_4$ decreases). The increase in $RE$ is $2\Delta h$ while the increase in $W_{comp}$ is only $\approx \Delta h$. **COP definitely increases**.

**Preference (1 mark):** Useful superheating is preferred because it improves COP without any penalty on the cold space. It is "free" useful refrigeration since the heat is taken from the liquid that was about to be throttled anyway.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **working of a Liquid-Suction Heat Exchanger (LSHX)**. Show its effect on the **P-h diagram** of a simple VCRS. The enthalpy values for a cycle with and without LSHX are given below:

| State | Without LSHX (kJ/kg) | With LSHX (kJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| $h_1$ (evap exit) | 240 | 248 |
| $h_2$ (comp exit) | 264 | 270 |
| $h_3$ (cond exit) | 116 | 108 |
| $h_4$ (evap inlet) | 116 | 108 |

**Calculate: (i) RE, W, and COP without LSHX, (ii) RE, W, and COP with LSHX, (iii) % improvement in COP.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Working of LSHX (2 marks):** A Liquid-Suction Heat Exchanger is a counter-flow heat exchanger installed between the **condenser outlet** and the **expansion valve inlet** (liquid side) and between the **evaporator outlet** and the **compressor inlet** (vapor side). The warm high-pressure liquid transfers part of its sensible heat to the cool low-pressure vapor. As a result, the **liquid is subcooled** before throttling, and the **vapor is superheated** before compression. This improves the refrigeration effect and the COP of the system.

**Effect on P-h diagram (1 mark):** On the P-h diagram, point 3 shifts left (to $h_3' < h_3$) due to subcooling, and point 1 shifts right (to $h_1' > h_1$) due to useful superheating. The cycle becomes "wider" on the enthalpy axis, indicating higher refrigeration effect.

---

**(i) Without LSHX (1.5 marks):**

$$
RE_1 = h_1 - h_4 = 240 - 116 = 124 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_1 = h_2 - h_1 = 264 - 240 = 24 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
COP_{R,1} = \dfrac{124}{24} = 5.167
$$

---

**(ii) With LSHX (1.5 marks):**

$$
RE_2 = h_1' - h_4' = 248 - 108 = 140 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_2 = h_2' - h_1' = 270 - 248 = 22 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
COP_{R,2} = \dfrac{140}{22} = 6.364
$$

---

**(iii) % Improvement in COP (1 mark):**

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{6.364 - 5.167}{5.167} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.197}{5.167} \times 100 = 23.16\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $h_4 = h_3$ in the simple cycle.** Many students mistakenly look up a separate value for $h_4$ from the table. Always remember: in the simple cycle, throttling is **isenthalpic**, so $h_4 = h_3$.
> 2. **Confusing state numbering.** KTU expects a **standard convention**: 1 = compressor inlet, 2 = compressor outlet, 3 = condenser outlet, 4 = evaporator inlet. Using reversed numbering will lead to negative values of RE and zero marks.
> 3. **Units mismatch.** $c_p$ is in **kJ/(kg·K)**, and $\Delta T$ is in **K** (or °C — the difference is the same). Mixing °C and K inconsistently is a frequent error.
> 4. **Assuming superheating always improves COP.** Without LSHX, **useless superheating** actually *decreases* COP. Always specify which type of superheating the question is asking about.
> 5. **Skipping the energy balance check.** After solving, verify $q_H = RE + W_{comp}$. This catches arithmetic errors and earns "method" marks even if the final number is off.
> 6. **Drawing the P-h dome incorrectly.** The dome is **tilted** (saturated vapor line slopes to the right with increasing pressure). A symmetric or vertical dome will lose diagram marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Topic: Simple VCRS & COP Improvement**

- ⭐ **Four state points** of the simple VCRS: 1 (comp inlet), 2 (comp outlet), 3 (cond outlet), 4 (evap inlet).
- ⭐ **Four processes:** 1→2 isentropic compression, 2→3 constant-P heat rejection, 3→4 isenthalpic throttling, 4→1 constant-P heat absorption.
- ⭐ **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $RE = h_1 - h_4$
  - $W = h_2 - h_1$
  - $q_H = h_2 - h_3$
  - $COP_R = (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$
  - $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$
  - $h_3 = h_4$ (throttling)
  - $s_1 = s_2$ (ideal compression)
- ⭐ **T-s diagram:** Throttling is approximately **vertical** (not exact — entropy can increase slightly).
- ⭐ **P-h diagram:** Throttling is **perfectly horizontal** (true isenthalpic process). The horizontal distance $h_1 - h_4$ equals RE.
- ⭐ **Subcooling effect:** Increases RE, leaves $W$ unchanged → **COP improves** by $c_{p,l} \cdot \Delta T_{sc} / W$.
- ⭐ **Useless superheating:** Slightly increases RE, increases $W$ more → **COP decreases**.
- ⭐ **Useful superheating (LSHX):** Doubly beneficial — subcools liquid + superheats vapor → **COP improves significantly** ($RE$ increase = $2\Delta h$).
- ⭐ **LSHX** is a **counter-flow heat exchanger** between the condenser-evaporator line (warm liquid) and the evaporator-compressor line (cool vapor).
- ⭐ **Standard refrigerants:** R-12 (older, being phased out — ozone depletion), R-134a (current, automotive & domestic), R-410A, R-32, R-404A, R-507A, R-744 (CO₂), R-717 (ammonia — industrial).
- ⭐ **Typical KTU exam marks distribution:** Part A 2 × 3 = 6 marks; Part B 1 × 14 = 14 marks (with internal choice); Total 20 marks per module.
- ⭐ **Valuation hack:** Always write the **1st law energy balance** $q_H = RE + W$ as a verification step — it earns a "method" mark even if numerical errors occur.
- ⭐ **The COP of a refrigerator is always greater than 1** (unlike an engine's efficiency, which is less than 1). This is a frequently asked conceptual question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-pressure Systems: Multi-compression and Multi-evaporator Systems, Inter-cooling, Flash Intercooling, Flash Gas Removal, Cascade Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Multi-pressure Systems: Multi-compression and Multi-evaporator Systems, Inter-cooling, Flash Intercooling, Flash Gas Removal, Cascade Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Multi-Pressure Refrigeration System? A **multi-pressure refrigeration system** is a vapor-compression refrigeration (VCRS) configuration that operates at more than one evaporator pressure and/or more than one condenser pressure. They are used when the temperature difference between the refrigerated space and the condenser ambient is so large that a single-stage cycle becomes thermodynamically inefficient or mechanically impractical. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Multi-pressure systems are employed in industrial refrigeration to achieve low temperatures (below −20 °C) or to serve multiple loads at different temperature levels using a common compressor/condenser network. > [!NOTE] > **Why can't we just use a single-stage system for everything?** > A single-stage system suffers from: > 1. **Excessive discharge temperature** — At very low evaporating temperatures, the pressure ratio becomes huge, causing the compressor to overheat. > 2. **Low volumetric efficiency** — High pressure ratios drop η_vol to impractical values (< 50%). > 3. **Poor COP** — Compression work becomes disproportionately large. > 4. **Lubrication failure** — Very high discharge temperatures coke the lubricating oil. > > Multi-pressure systems solve all of these problems by **splitting the pressure lift** across multiple stages. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** > > Imagine climbing a 5000 m mountain. You wouldn't sprint from base to summit in one go — you'd stage your climb at base camps (camp 1 at 2000 m, camp 2 at 3500 m), rest, acclimatize, then continue. Each camp corresponds to a **pressure level** in the refrigeration system. The **intercooler** is the rest stop where you breathe thinner air (lower temperature/pressure) before the next push. **Flash gas removal** is like dumping your heavy backpack at a camp so you climb lighter — only the liquid (essential cargo) moves up. --- ### 1.3 Classification of Multi-Pressure Systems | # | System Type | Main Use | |---|-------------|----------| | 1 | **Multi-Stage (Multi-Compression)** with Intercooling | Very low temperature single-load applications | | 2 | **Flash Intercooling** (Flash Chamber) | Economiser between two compression stages | | 3 | **Flash Gas Removal (FGR)** | Improves intercooler effectiveness | | 4 | **Multi-Evaporator System** | Serves multiple loads at different temperatures | | 5 | **Cascade System** | Very low temperature (−40 °C to −80 °C) with two independent loops | --- ### 1.4 Standard Refrigerants Used (Industrial Context) - **Ammonia (R-717)** — Most common industrial refrigerant; **ODP = 0**, **GWP = 0**. - **R-502** — Once common for low-temp, now phased out (high GWP). - **CO₂ (R-744)** — Cascade low-stage and trans-critical use. - **R-404A / R-507** — Modern HFC replacements for low-temp. > [!NOTE] > Industrial refrigeration (as per KTU) is dominantly **ammonia-based** for capacities above 100 kW. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Multi-Stage Compression with Intercooling **Operating Principle:** - The total pressure ratio is split into two (or more) smaller ratios. - Between stages, the vapor is **cooled at constant intermediate pressure** back toward the suction temperature (ideally). - This reduces: - Compressor work - Discharge temperature - Specific volume entering the next stage **Two-Stage Compression System Components:** 1. Low-stage (L.P.) compressor 2. High-stage (H.P.) compressor 3. **Intercooler** (between discharge of L.P. and suction of H.P.) 4. Condenser, expansion valve(s), evaporator **Why Intercool?** - Reduces specific volume at H.P. compressor suction → smaller cylinder volume. - Reduces H.P. compressor discharge temperature. - Reduces compression work (the area under the P-v diagram shrinks). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Perfect (Ideal) Intercooling:** Vapor leaves the intercooler at the **same temperature as the saturated vapor at the L.P. compressor suction**. This is the most thermodynamically efficient case. --- ### 2.2 Flash Intercooling (with Flash Chamber / Economiser) **Concept:** A **flash chamber** is an intermediate pressure vessel placed between the condenser and the evaporator. Subcooled (or saturated) liquid from the condenser is throttled to the **intermediate pressure** ($P_i$). Some of it **flashes** into vapor; the remaining (colder) saturated liquid is sent to the evaporator, while the flash vapor is sent to the **H.P. compressor suction** (mixed with the L.P. compressor discharge) or to an **economiser inlet of the H.P. compressor**. **Key Idea:** - The flash gas does **not** pass through any heat exchanger — it is generated by throttling. - This **flash gas** is at a lower enthalpy than the H.P. compressor discharge, so it **cools** the discharge vapor (internal intercooling). - The evaporator receives **colder liquid** → more refrigeration effect per kg. **The Flash Intercooling Cycle (5-port):** 1. Condenser exit (state 1) — saturated liquid at $P_c$ 2. Throttle valve 1 → flash chamber (state 2) at $P_i$ 3. Saturated liquid exits flash chamber (state 3) → throttle valve 2 → evaporator (state 4) at $P_e$ 4. Saturated vapor from evaporator (state 5) → L.P. compressor → state 6 5. Saturated vapor from flash chamber (state 7) mixes with L.P. discharge at $P_i$ → state 8 → H.P. compressor → state 9 → condenser --- ### 2.3 Flash Gas Removal (FGR) **Definition:** A flash gas removal system is a modification of the flash intercooling system where the **flash gas is NOT mixed** with the L.P. compressor discharge. Instead, it is **ducted directly** to the H.P. compressor suction (after possible further cooling) or even to a **separate, smaller H.P. compressor** (separate compression of flash gas). **Advantage over Flash Intercooling:** - The L.P. compressor is not asked to handle the flash gas → **L.P. compressor work decreases**. - The H.P. compressor can be sized for the combined mass flow (main + flash), so the savings depend on the design. **Key thermodynamic effect:** Mass flowing through the L.P. compressor is **reduced** to just the refrigerant that actually vaporized in the evaporator. --- ### 2.4 Multi-Evaporator Systems **Concept:** Multiple evaporators, each at a **different pressure/temperature**, are served by a **single compressor-condenser assembly**. Common applications: - Supermarket refrigeration (chillers + freezers) - Food processing (cold storage at multiple temperatures) **Two main arrangements:** **(a) Individual Expansion Valves + Pressure Reducing Valves (PRV):** - Highest pressure evaporator directly feeds compressor. - Lower pressure evaporators feed back through a **pressure-reducing valve** to match the compressor suction pressure. - Used when one evaporator is at much higher pressure than the others. **(b) Individual Compressors (Compound System):** - Each evaporator has its own compressor. - Discharges of all compressors go to a common condenser. - Used when capacities and pressures differ significantly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Refrigeration effect** in a multi-evaporator system is the **sum of refrigeration effects** of each evaporator: > > $$RE_{total} = \sum \dot{m}_i (h_{fg})_i$$ --- ### 2.5 Cascade Refrigeration System **Concept:** A **cascade system** uses **two independent refrigeration cycles** (low-stage and high-stage) connected by a **cascade heat exchanger** (also called the **intercooler** of the cascade). The condenser of the low-stage cycle is cooled by the evaporator of the high-stage cycle. **Why Cascade?** - When the temperature difference is so large (e.g., −80 °C evaporator + 35 °C condenser) that a single refrigerant cannot efficiently span the range. - Low-stage uses refrigerant optimized for low temperatures (e.g., R-23, R-508B, CO₂). - High-stage uses refrigerant optimized for moderate temperatures (e.g., R-717, R-404A, R-134a). **Range of Application:** - LNG liquefaction - Pharmaceutical freeze-drying - Cryogenic storage - Drying processes --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\text{PR}_{total} = \frac{P_c}{P_e}$ | Overall pressure ratio (single stage) | dimensionless | | 2 | $\text{PR}_{stage} = \sqrt{\frac{P_c}{P_e}}$ | Optimum intermediate pressure (equal pressure ratio) for two-stage | dimensionless | | 3 | $P_i = \sqrt{P_e \cdot P_c}$ | Optimum intermediate pressure (geometric mean) | kPa | | 4 | $W_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | Compressor work (per stage) | kW | | 5 | $RE = \dot{m} (h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration Effect | kW | | 6 | $\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{COP}_{2-stage} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_6 - h_5) + (h_8 - h_7)}$ | COP for two-stage with intercooling | dimensionless | | 8 | $x = \frac{h_f - h_3}{h_{fg}}$ | Quality after flash (in flash chamber) | dimensionless | | 9 | $\dot{m}_{flash} = \dot{m}_{total} \cdot (1 - x)$ | Mass flow to evaporator after flash | kg/s | | 10 | $\dot{m}_{flash-vapor} = \dot{m}_{total} \cdot x$ | Flash vapor mass flow to H.P. compressor | kg/s | | 11 | $\text{COP}_{cascade} = \text{COP}_{low} \times \text{COP}_{high}$ | Cascade COP (product, not sum) | dimensionless | | 12 | $\text{RE}_{cascade} = \dot{m}_{low} (h_{L3} - h_{L4})$ | Cascade refrigeration effect | kW | > [!NOTE] > **Critical boundary condition for optimum $P_i$:** Equal pressure ratio per stage minimizes total compression work (for isentropic compression of an ideal gas). This is the **fundamental KTU derivation step** in most exam problems. --- ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | System Used | Reason | |--------|-------------|--------| | Cold Storage Warehouses (India) | Two-stage ammonia with intercooling | Suction temp ≈ −35 °C | | LNG Plants | Cascade (R-23 / R-404A or CO₂/NH₃) | Reaches −160 °C | | Ice Rinks | Two-stage ammonia | High COP at low temp | | Pharma Lyophilization | Cascade | Precise low-temperature control | | Dairy / Breweries | Multi-evaporator ammonia | Different process temperatures | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Intermediate Pressure for Two-Stage Compression **Statement:** The intermediate pressure that minimizes the total compression work is the **geometric mean** of evaporator and condenser pressures. **Setup:** - L.P. compressor: suction at $P_e$, discharge at $P_i$ - H.P. compressor: suction at $P_i$, discharge at $P_c$ - Same refrigerant mass flow $\dot{m}$ through both stages (simple two-stage without flash chamber). - Assume **ideal gas** behavior, isentropic compression, perfect intercooling (so suction of H.P. = suction of L.P., both at $T_e$). **Step 1 — Work of L.P. compressor:** $$ W_{LP} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_e \left[ \left(\frac{P_i}{P_e}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$ **Step 2 — Work of H.P. compressor (suction cooled to $T_e$):** $$ W_{HP} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_e \left[ \left(\frac{P_c}{P_i}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$ **Step 3 — Total compression work:** $$ W_{tot} = W_{LP} + W_{HP} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_e \left[ \left(\frac{P_i}{P_e}\right)^{n} + \left(\frac{P_c}{P_i}\right)^{n} - 2 \right] $$ where $n = \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}$. **Step 4 — Minimize w.r.t. $P_i$.** Take $\frac{dW_{tot}}{dP_i} = 0$: $$ \frac{d}{dP_i} \left[ \left(\frac{P_i}{P_e}\right)^{n} + \left(\frac{P_c}{P_i}\right)^{n} \right] = 0 $$ $$ \frac{n}{P_e} \left(\frac{P_i}{P_e}\right)^{n-1} - \frac{n \cdot P_c^n}{P_i^{n+1}} = 0 $$ **Step 5 — Algebraic simplification:** $$ \frac{1}{P_e} \cdot \left(\frac{P_i}{P_e}\right)^{n-1} = \frac{P_c^n}{P_i^{n+1}} $$ $$ \frac{P_i^{n-1}}{P_e^n} = \frac{P_c^n}{P_i^{n+1}} $$ **Step 6 — Cross-multiplying:** $$ P_i^{n-1} \cdot P_i^{n+1} = P_c^n \cdot P_e^n $$ $$ P_i^{2n} = (P_c \cdot P_e)^n $$ **Step 7 — Solving for $P_i$:** $$ P_i^2 = P_e \cdot P_c $$ $$ \boxed{P_i = \sqrt{P_e \cdot P_c}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical meaning:** The two pressure ratios are equal at the optimum: > $$\frac{P_i}{P_e} = \frac{P_c}{P_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PR}_{LP} = \text{PR}_{HP}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: COP of Two-Stage System with Intercooling (No Flash Chamber) **Step 1 — Mass balance:** Same $\dot{m}$ through both compressors and the evaporator. **Step 2 — Refrigeration Effect (per kg of refrigerant):** $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 $$ where: - $h_1$ = enthalpy of vapor leaving evaporator (saturated vapor at $P_e$) - $h_4$ = enthalpy of liquid leaving condenser (saturated liquid at $P_c$) **Step 3 — Total compressor work (using isentropic relations and P-h tables):** $$ w_{comp} = (h_2 - h_1) + (h_6 - h_5) $$ where: - $h_2$ = L.P. compressor discharge (superheated vapor at $P_i$) - $h_5$ = H.P. compressor suction (after intercooling, ideally = $h_1$) - $h_6$ = H.P. compressor discharge (superheated vapor at $P_c$) **Step 4 — COP:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2 - h_1) + (h_6 - h_5)} $$ **Step 5 — If perfect intercooling** ($h_5 = h_1$): $$ \text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2 - h_1) + (h_6 - h_1)} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Mass Flow Analysis in a Flash Intercooling System **Setup:** - Mass flow from condenser: $\dot{m}_1$ - State 3: saturated liquid at intermediate pressure $P_i$ (after flash) - State 4: throttled to evaporator pressure $P_e$ **Step 1 — Energy balance on flash chamber (steady, adiabatic, no work):** Mass entering: $\dot{m}_1$ at $h_2$ Mass leaving: liquid $\dot{m}_3$ at $h_3$ + vapor $\dot{m}_{fg}$ at $h_7$ $$ \dot{m}_1 h_2 = \dot{m}_3 h_3 + \dot{m}_{fg} h_7 $$ **Step 2 — Mass balance:** $$ \dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_3 + \dot{m}_{fg} $$ **Step 3 — Enthalpy of mixture leaving the flash chamber** (assuming the entering liquid from condenser is saturated at $P_c$, then throttled to $P_i$ — the throttle is isenthalpic, so $h_2 = h_{f@P_c}$ entering the flash chamber at $P_i$): The quality $x$ of the mixture is: $$ x = \frac{h_2 - h_{f@P_i}}{h_{fg@P_i}} $$ **Step 4 — Fraction of flash vapor:** $$ \frac{\dot{m}_{fg}}{\dot{m}_1} = x = \frac{h_2 - h_{f@P_i}}{h_{fg@P_i}} $$ **Step 5 — Mass flow to evaporator:** $$ \dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 (1 - x) $$ **Step 6 — Refrigeration Effect:** $$ RE = \dot{m}_3 (h_1 - h_4) $$ Note: $\dot{m}_3$ is **less** than $\dot{m}_1$ (some mass is lost to flash), but the liquid entering the evaporator is **colder** (saturated liquid at $P_i$ instead of $P_c$), so the **specific refrigeration effect** $(h_1 - h_4)$ is **larger**. The net RE is typically **greater** than the single-stage case. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: COP of a Cascade Refrigeration System **Setup:** - Low-stage cycle: refrigerant A, evaporator at $T_{e,L}$, condenser at $T_{c,L}$ (= cascade HX hot side) - High-stage cycle: refrigerant B, evaporator at $T_{e,H}$ (= cascade HX cold side), condenser at $T_{c,H}$ - **Heat balance on cascade heat exchanger:** Heat rejected by low-stage condenser = Heat absorbed by high-stage evaporator $$ \dot{m}_{L} (h_{L,2} - h_{L,3}) = \dot{m}_{H} (h_{H,1} - h_{H,4}) $$ **Step 1 — RE of cascade (per kg of low-stage refrigerant):** $$ RE_{cascade} = h_{L,1} - h_{L,4} $$ **Step 2 — Total work input (sum of both compressors):** $$ W_{tot} = \dot{m}_{L}(h_{L,2} - h_{L,1}) + \dot{m}_{H}(h_{H,2} - h_{H,1}) $$ **Step 3 — COP:** $$ \text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{RE_{cascade}}{W_{tot}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{L}(h_{L,1} - h_{L,4})}{\dot{m}_{L}(h_{L,2} - h_{L,1}) + \dot{m}_{H}(h_{H,2} - h_{H,1})} $$ **Step 4 — Substituting COP definitions for each loop** and the HX balance: $$ \text{COP}_{cascade} = \text{COP}_{low} \times \text{COP}_{high} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multiplication Rule:** The COP of a cascade system is the **product** of the individual cycle COPs. This is a key KTU exam point — students often (incorrectly) try to add them. **Proof sketch:** $$ \text{COP}_{low} = \frac{Q_{L,evap}}{W_{low}} = \frac{\dot{m}_L (h_{L,1} - h_{L,4})}{\dot{m}_L (h_{L,2} - h_{L,1})} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{high} = \frac{Q_{H,evap}}{W_{high}} = \frac{\dot{m}_H (h_{H,1} - h_{H,4})}{\dot{m}_H (h_{H,2} - h_{H,1})} $$ Since the cascade HX equates $Q_{L,cond} = Q_{H,evap}$, the overall COP multiplies. --- ### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A two-stage ammonia refrigeration system has: - Evaporator pressure: 2 bar (sat. temp ≈ −18.86 °C, $h_g = 1437$ kJ/kg) - Condenser pressure: 12 bar (sat. temp ≈ 30.6 °C, $h_f = 323$ kJ/kg) - Isentropic efficiency of each compressor: 85% - Mass flow: 0.1 kg/s Find: (a) Optimum intermediate pressure. (b) Refrigeration Effect. (c) COP. (d) Compare COP with a single-stage system having the same conditions. **Solution:** **Part (a):** $$ P_i = \sqrt{P_e \cdot P_c} = \sqrt{2 \times 12} = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.899 \text{ bar} $$ **Part (b):** At $P_i = 4.9$ bar, $T_{sat} \approx 4.5$ °C, $h_g = 1465$ kJ/kg, $h_f = 196$ kJ/kg. For **two-stage with intercooling** (intercooler brings H.P. suction back to saturated vapor at $P_e$): $$ RE = h_{g@P_e} - h_{f@P_c} = 1437 - 323 = 1114 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Part (c):** Compressor work (assuming isentropic then corrected for efficiency): L.P. compressor: $h_2$ (from tables at $P_i = 4.9$ bar, $s = s_g@P_e$). For ideal: $w_{LP,s} = h_2 - 1437$. Typical value: ~190 kJ/kg. H.P. compressor: $h_6$ (at $P_c = 12$ bar, $s = s_g@P_e$, after perfect intercooling). Typical: ~215 kJ/kg. $$ w_{comp} = 190 + 215 = 405 \text{ kJ/kg (isentropic)} $$ With $\eta_{isen} = 0.85$, actual: $$ w_{actual} = \frac{405}{0.85} \approx 476 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{2-stage} = \frac{1114}{476} \approx 2.34 $$ **Part (d):** Single-stage: pressure ratio = 6. With same conditions, single-stage compressor work ≈ 480 kJ/kg, $h_4 = 323$ kJ/kg, RE = 1114 kJ/kg. $$ \text{COP}_{1-stage} = \frac{1114}{480} \approx 2.32 $$ > [!NOTE] > In this case the COP improvement is **modest** because the pressure ratio (6) is not extreme. At PR = 20 (industrial low-temp), the improvement is **~30–40%**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Two-Stage Compression with Intercooling + Flash Chamber (System Architecture) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Condenser
High Pressure Pc] -->|State 1 sat liquid| nodeB[Expansion Valve 1] nodeB -->|State 2 sat mixture| nodeC[Flash Chamber
Intermediate Pi] nodeC -->|State 7 sat vapor| nodeD[Mixing Chamber
Pi] nodeC -->|State 3 sat liquid| nodeE[Expansion Valve 2] nodeE -->|State 4 mixture| nodeF[Evaporator
Low Pressure Pe] nodeF -->|State 5 sat vapor| nodeG[LP Compressor] nodeG -->|State 6 superheated| nodeD nodeD -->|State 8 mixed vapor| nodeH[HP Compressor] nodeH -->|State 9 superheated| nodeA nodeI[Intercooler option
between LP and HP] -.-> nodeD ``` --- ### 4.2 Flash Intercooling Cycle — State Point Map ```mermaid graph TB s1[1: Cond exit sat liquid Pc] -->|Throttle 1| s2[2: Flash chamber inlet Pi] s2 -->|Phase split| s3[3: Saturated liquid Pi] s2 -->|Phase split| s7[7: Saturated vapor Pi] s3 -->|Throttle 2| s4[4: Evaporator inlet Pe] s4 -->|Evaporation| s5[5: Evaporator exit sat vapor Pe] s5 -->|LP Compression| s6[6: LP discharge Pi] s7 -->|Mix with state 6| s8[8: HP compressor inlet Pi] s6 -->|Mix| s8 s8 -->|HP Compression| s9[9: HP discharge Pc] s9 -->|Condensation| s1 ``` --- ### 4.3 Cascade Refrigeration System — Block Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph LowStage[LOW STAGE CYCLE Refrigerant A e.g. R-23] L1[LP Compressor] --> L2[Condenser of Low Stage] L2 --> L3[Expansion Valve Low] L3 --> L4[Evaporator Low Stage] L4 --> L1 end subgraph CascadeHX[CASCADE HEAT EXCHANGER] HX1[Hot Side Low Stage Cond] --- HX2[Cold Side High Stage Evap] end L2 -. Heat Rejected .-> HX1 HX2 -. Heat Absorbed .-> L4 subgraph HighStage[HIGH STAGE CYCLE Refrigerant B e.g. NH3] H1[HP Compressor] --> H2[Condenser High Stage] H2 --> H3[Expansion Valve High] H3 --> HX2 HX2 --> H1 end L1 -.-> L2 ``` --- ### 4.4 Multi-Evaporator System with Pressure Reducing Valves ```mermaid graph TB C[Common Compressor] --> CD[Common Condenser] CD --> EV1[Expansion Valve Evap 1 Hi P] CD --> EV2[Expansion Valve Evap 2 Mid P] CD --> EV3[Expansion Valve Evap 3 Low P] EV1 --> E1[Evaporator 1 High Temp] EV2 --> E2[Evaporator 2 Mid Temp] EV3 --> E3[Evaporator 3 Low Temp] E1 --> MJ[Common Suction Manifold] E2 --> PRV1[Pressure Reducing Valve] PRV1 --> MJ E3 --> PRV2[Pressure Reducing Valve] PRV2 --> MJ MJ --> C ``` --- ### 4.5 Flash Gas Removal System — Functional Flow ```mermaid graph LR CD[Condenser] -->|Sat liq Pc| TV1[Throttle Valve 1] TV1 -->|Mixture Pi| FC[Flash Chamber] FC -->|Sat vapor Pi| SEP[Flash Gas Ducted Separately] FC -->|Sat liquid Pi| TV2[Throttle Valve 2] TV2 -->|Mixture Pe| EV[Evaporator] EV -->|Sat vapor Pe| LPC[LP Compressor] LPC -->|Superh Pi| MIX[Mixing Point with Flash Gas] SEP --> MIX MIX -->|Combined| HPC[HP Compressor] HPC --> CD ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. What is a multi-pressure refrigeration system? Why is it preferred over single-stage systems at very low evaporator temperatures?** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** A multi-pressure refrigeration system is a vapor compression system that operates at more than one pressure level — using either multiple compressors (multi-compression) or multiple evaporators (multi-evaporator) or both (cascade). It is preferred over single-stage systems at low evaporator temperatures because: 1. **Lower discharge temperature** — splitting the pressure ratio prevents the compressor from exceeding safe discharge limits. 2. **Higher volumetric efficiency** — smaller pressure ratio per stage. 3. **Better COP** — reduced compression work and better heat rejection. 4. **Lubrication stability** — avoids oil coking at high discharge temperatures. > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate between flash intercooling and flash gas removal (FGR) systems.** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Flash Intercooling | Flash Gas Removal | |--------|-------------------|-------------------| | Flash vapor path | Mixed with LP compressor discharge | Routed **separately** to HP compressor suction | | LP compressor | Handles evaporator vapor **only** | Handles evaporator vapor **only** | | HP compressor | Handles **combined** mass (LP discharge + flash vapor) | Handles **combined** mass (LP discharge + flash vapor from separate line) | | Mixing point | Inside the system piping | May be at HP compressor inlet only | | Advantage | Simpler piping | Avoids mixing losses; cleaner thermodynamic path | --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Draw a neat schematic of a two-stage vapor compression refrigeration system with a flash chamber (intercooler). Label all the components and state points. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A two-stage ammonia refrigeration system operates between evaporator temperature −20 °C and condenser temperature 40 °C. The refrigerant is subcooled by 5 °C at the condenser exit. The system uses a flash chamber at the optimum intermediate pressure. Determine: - (i) Optimum intermediate pressure - (ii) Refrigeration effect per kg - (iii) Work of compression per kg (assume isentropic compression with perfect intercooling) - (iv) COP of the system Use the following approximate data for ammonia: - At −20 °C: $P_e = 1.901$ bar, $h_f = 174$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 1418$ kJ/kg, $s_g = 5.616$ kJ/kg·K - At 40 °C: $P_c = 15.554$ bar, $h_f = 371$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 1474$ kJ/kg - At $P_i$ (intermediate): $h_{fg} = 1056$ kJ/kg **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A:** **Part (a) — Schematic (7 Marks):** The schematic is the two-stage VCRS with flash chamber. Students should draw: 1. Condenser at $P_c$ 2. Expansion valve 1 (high-side) → Flash chamber at $P_i$ 3. Saturated liquid from flash chamber → Expansion valve 2 → Evaporator at $P_e$ 4. LP compressor (suction $P_e$, discharge $P_i$) 5. Saturated vapor from flash chamber mixes with LP compressor discharge 6. HP compressor (suction $P_i$, discharge $P_c$) 7. Intercooler optional between LP and HP compressor (in the interstage line) 8. All 9 state points labeled: 1 (cond exit), 2 (after throttle 1), 3 (saturated liquid from flash), 4 (after throttle 2), 5 (evaporator exit), 6 (LP discharge), 7 (flash vapor), 8 (mixed HP inlet), 9 (HP discharge) **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** - Neatness and labeling: 2 marks - Correct component identification: 3 marks - State point numbering and flow direction: 2 marks --- **Part (b) — Calculations (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Optimum Intermediate Pressure:** $$ P_i = \sqrt{P_e \cdot P_c} = \sqrt{1.901 \times 15.554} = \sqrt{29.57} \approx 5.438 \text{ bar} $$ **Step 2 — Subcooled liquid enthalpy at condenser exit (state 1):** Subcooled by 5 °C → $T_1 = 40 - 5 = 35$ °C. Approximate as $h_1 \approx h_{f@40°C} - c_p \cdot \Delta T = 371 - 4.5 \times 5 = 371 - 22.5 = 348.5$ kJ/kg. **Step 3 — Quality after throttling to flash chamber:** Throttling is isenthalpic: $h_2 = h_1 = 348.5$ kJ/kg. $$ x_2 = \frac{h_2 - h_{f@P_i}}{h_{fg@P_i}} = \frac{348.5 - h_{f@P_i}}{1056} $$ At $P_i \approx 5.44$ bar, $T_{sat} \approx 10$ °C, $h_f \approx 246$ kJ/kg (interpolating from ammonia tables). $$ x_2 = \frac{348.5 - 246}{1056} = \frac{102.5}{1056} \approx 0.0971 $$ So ~9.71% of the mass flashes to vapor; 90.29% continues as saturated liquid. **Step 4 — Refrigeration Effect per kg of total mass flow:** Mass to evaporator: $\dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 (1 - x_2) = \dot{m}_1 \times 0.9029$. $$ RE = (1 - x_2)(h_{g@P_e} - h_{f@P_i}) = 0.9029 \times (1418 - 246) $$ $$ RE = 0.9029 \times 1172 = 1058.2 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — LP Compressor Work (isentropic):** Suction: $P_e = 1.901$ bar, $h_1^{LP} = h_{g@P_e} = 1418$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 5.616$ kJ/kg·K. Discharge: $P_i = 5.44$ bar, $s_2 = s_1 = 5.616$ kJ/kg·K. From ammonia tables, at $P_i = 5.44$ bar with $s = 5.616$ → superheated, $h_2^{LP} \approx 1585$ kJ/kg. $$ w_{LP} = 1585 - 1418 = 167 \text{ kJ/kg (per kg through LP compressor)} $$ Per kg of total mass: $w_{LP}' = w_{LP} \times 1 = 167$ kJ/kg (all total mass passes through LP compressor if it's the only LP path; for FGR it'd be less — note this is the standard assumption). **Step 6 — HP Compressor Work (isentropic, perfect intercooling):** Suction: state 8 = mixture of LP discharge (state 6) and flash vapor (state 7) at $P_i$. For perfect intercooling, $h_8 = h_{g@P_e} = 1418$ kJ/kg (cooler brings it back to suction state). Discharge: $P_c = 15.55$ bar, $s_8 = s_{g@P_e} = 5.616$ kJ/kg·K. From ammonia tables, at 15.55 bar with $s = 5.616$ → $h_9 \approx 1665$ kJ/kg. $$ w_{HP} = 1665 - 1418 = 247 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Per kg of total mass: $w_{HP}' = 247$ kJ/kg. **Step 7 — COP:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{RE}{w_{LP}' + w_{HP}'} = \frac{1058.2}{167 + 247} = \frac{1058.2}{414} \approx 2.56 $$ > **Final Answer (b):** $P_i \approx 5.44$ bar, RE ≈ 1058 kJ/kg, $W \approx 414$ kJ/kg, COP ≈ 2.56 --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **cascade refrigeration system**. Mention the typical refrigerants used for the low-stage and high-stage cycles. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A cascade refrigeration system uses R-12 in the high stage and R-13 in the low stage. The evaporator of the low stage is at −70 °C and the condenser of the high stage is at 30 °C. The cascade heat exchanger temperature is −10 °C on the high-stage side. Determine: - (i) Refrigeration effect of the low stage (per kg of R-13) - (ii) Mass flow ratio of R-12 to R-13 - (iii) COP of the cascade system Given (saturated values): - R-13 at −70 °C: $h_f = 60$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 270$ kJ/kg - R-13 at −10 °C (cond): $h_f = 95$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 290$ kJ/kg - R-12 at −10 °C (evap): $h_f = 30$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 180$ kJ/kg - R-12 at 30 °C (cond): $h_f = 65$ kJ/kg, $h_g = 200$ kJ/kg - Assume isentropic compression: $h_{2,LP} = 305$ kJ/kg, $h_{2,HP} = 210$ kJ/kg **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B:** **Part (a) — Diagram and Explanation (7 Marks):** A cascade system consists of two independent VCRS cycles: 1. **Low-temperature (low-stage) cycle** — produces the required low temperature. Condenser of this cycle is cooled by the evaporator of the high-stage cycle through a **cascade heat exchanger**. 2. **High-temperature (high-stage) cycle** — rejects heat to the ambient. Its evaporator absorbs heat from the low-stage condenser. **Typical Refrigerants:** - **Low stage** (very low T): R-13, R-23, R-508B, **R-744 (CO₂)** - **High stage** (moderate T): R-12, **R-717 (NH₃)**, R-134a, R-404A **Schematic:** Two separate loops, with a common cascade heat exchanger in between. Each loop has its own compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator. --- **Part (b) — Calculations (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Refrigeration Effect of Low Stage (R-13):** $$ RE_{low} = h_{g,@-70°C} - h_{f,@-70°C} = 270 - 60 = 210 \text{ kJ/kg of R-13} $$ **Step 2 — Heat rejected by low-stage condenser (per kg of R-13):** $$ Q_{L,cond} = h_{g,@-10°C} - h_{f,@-10°C} = 290 - 95 = 195 \text{ kJ/kg of R-13} $$ **Step 3 — Refrigeration Effect of High Stage (R-12):** $$ RE_{high} = h_{g,@-10°C} - h_{f,@-10°C} = 180 - 30 = 150 \text{ kJ/kg of R-12} $$ **Step 4 — Cascade HX Energy Balance:** $$ \dot{m}_{R13} \cdot Q_{L,cond} = \dot{m}_{R12} \cdot RE_{high} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{R13} \times 195 = \dot{m}_{R12} \times 150 $$ $$ \frac{\dot{m}_{R12}}{\dot{m}_{R13}} = \frac{195}{150} = 1.30 $$ **Step 5 — Compressor Work:** Low stage (R-13): $w_{LP} = h_{2,LP} - h_{g,@-70°C} = 305 - 270 = 35$ kJ/kg High stage (R-12): $w_{HP} = h_{2,HP} - h_{g,@-10°C} = 210 - 180 = 30$ kJ/kg **Step 6 — COP of Cascade:** Per kg of R-13: $$ W_{tot} = \dot{m}_{R13} \cdot 35 + \dot{m}_{R12} \cdot 30 = \dot{m}_{R13} (35 + 1.30 \times 30) = \dot{m}_{R13} (35 + 39) = 74 \, \dot{m}_{R13} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{\dot{m}_{R13} \times 210}{74 \, \dot{m}_{R13}} = \frac{210}{74} \approx 2.84 $$ **Verification using product rule:** $$ \text{COP}_{low} = \frac{210}{35} = 6.0 $$ $$ \text{COP}_{high} = \frac{150}{30} = 5.0 $$ $$ \text{COP}_{cascade} = 6.0 \times 5.0 / (\text{ratio factor}) \quad \text{— note the product rule applies only with same mass flow assumption; here mass flows differ, so we use direct method.} $$ > **Final Answer (b):** RE_low = 210 kJ/kg, mass flow ratio = 1.30, COP_cascade ≈ 2.84 --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Pressure ratio confusion:** Many students write $P_i = (P_e + P_c)/2$ (arithmetic mean) instead of $\sqrt{P_e P_c}$ (geometric mean). **Always use geometric mean for isentropic compression of ideal gas.** > 2. **Subcooling in flash chamber:** State 2 of the flash chamber is **after throttling**, so it equals $h_1$ (subcooled liquid enthalpy from condenser exit), NOT $h_{f@P_c}$. Then find $x$ from $h_2$ at $P_i$. > 3. **Mass flow direction in FGR:** In FGR, the flash gas is **routed to HP compressor** — it does **not** go to the LP compressor. This reduces LP compressor work but increases HP compressor mass flow. > 4. **Cascade COP — Addition vs Multiplication:** The cascade COP is **not** simply additive. Use direct energy balance (per kg of low-stage refrigerant) to avoid mistakes. > 5. **Intercooling "perfect" condition:** A student must explicitly state that **$T$ at H.P. compressor suction = $T$ at L.P. compressor suction**. This is the ideal intercooling assumption that gives maximum COP improvement. > 6. **State numbering:** Mixing chamber between flash vapor and LP discharge → state 8. Forgetting to label this is a common **−1 mark** mistake in KTU valuation. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Fire Revision — KTU Module 2: Multi-Pressure Systems** - [x] **Optimum intermediate pressure** for two-stage compression: $P_i = \sqrt{P_e \cdot P_c}$ — derived from minimization of total compression work. - [x] **Pressure ratios are equal** at optimum: $PR_{LP} = PR_{HP} = \sqrt{P_c/P_e}$. - [x] **Perfect intercooling** assumes H.P. compressor suction temperature = L.P. compressor suction temperature. - [x] **Flash intercooling** uses the enthalpy of throttling to produce cold liquid + cold vapor at $P_i$. - [x] **Flash gas quality** $x = (h_{2} - h_{f@P_i})/h_{fg@P_i}$ where $h_2 = h_1$ (from condenser exit, subcooled). - [x] **FGR** routes flash vapor **separately** to HP compressor — keeps LP compressor mass flow minimal. - [x] **Multi-evaporator** systems use either individual expansion valves + PRVs or individual compressors per evaporator. - [x] **Cascade COP = product rule** only when both cycles have the same per-kg COP definition; in general use direct energy balance. - [x] **Cascade heat exchanger** is the key coupling — heat rejected by low-stage condenser = heat absorbed by high-stage evaporator. - [x] **Refrigerant selection rule:** Low-stage refrigerant should have **low NBP** (boiling point) → R-23, R-13, R-744. High-stage refrigerant optimized for moderate temperatures → NH₃, R-134a. - [x] **Discharge temperature limit** in single-stage: typically $T_{disch} \le 150$ °C for NH₃ to prevent oil breakdown. - [x] **Pressure ratio rule of thumb:** When $P_c/P_e > 8$, two-stage is recommended for NH₃ systems. - [x] **Net refrigeration effect** in a flash system is **larger per kg of liquid to evaporator** because the liquid is colder, even though mass is reduced by the flash fraction. - [x] **Multi-evaporator RE total** = $\sum \dot{m}_i (h_{fg})_i$ — sum, not weighted average. - [x] **Industrial applications** of cascade: LNG (−162 °C), freeze-drying, pharmaceutical cold chains, cryobiology.

topics.title: Refrigerants: Properties, Eco-friendly Refrigerants, Mixed Refrigerants, Selection Criteria for Different Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Refrigerants: Properties, Eco-friendly Refrigerants, Mixed Refrigerants, Selection Criteria for Different Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigerant (KTU 2024 Definition):** A refrigerant is a working fluid used in a refrigeration cycle that undergoes a reversible phase-change process (evaporation and condensation) at appropriate temperature and pressure levels, thereby absorbing heat from a low-temperature region (evaporator) and rejecting it to a high-temperature region (condenser).

Mathematically, the heat absorbed in the evaporator per unit mass is the **latent heat of vaporization**, $h_{fg}$, evaluated at the evaporator saturation pressure $P_{evap}$:

$$q_L = h_{fg}(P_{evap})$$

And the heat rejected in the condenser is:

$$q_H = h_{fg}(P_{cond}) + c_p (T_{cond} - T_{evap})$$

where $c_p$ is the specific heat of the superheated vapor.

## 1.2 Classification of Refrigerants

### 1.2.1 Primary Refrigerants
These fluids undergo a phase change in the refrigeration cycle (evaporate at low temperature, condense at high temperature). Examples: R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-717 (Ammonia), R-744 (CO₂), R-290 (Propane).

### 1.2.2 Secondary Refrigerants
These are heat-transfer fluids that do **not** undergo phase change. They transport thermal energy from one location to another. Examples: Brine, water, glycol mixtures, sodium chloride solutions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Primary refrigerants = Phase-change fluids. Secondary refrigerants = Sensible heat carriers (no phase change).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Perspective)

Think of a refrigerant as the **"blood" of the refrigeration system**, just as blood circulates oxygen and nutrients throughout a human body:

- **Evaporator = Lungs:** The refrigerant "inhales" heat from the space to be cooled (just as lungs absorb oxygen).
- **Compressor = Heart:** It pumps the refrigerant through the system at high pressure (just as the heart pumps blood).
- **Condenser = Skin/Kidneys:** The refrigerant "exhales" heat to the surroundings (just as sweat releases body heat).
- **Expansion Valve = Arterioles:** A pressure drop occurs here, enabling the cycle to repeat.

The **latent heat of vaporization** of the refrigerant is analogous to the "oxygen-carrying capacity" of blood. A refrigerant with higher $h_{fg}$ can transport more heat per kg of fluid circulated, which is why it is a key selection parameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants (Standard Metrics in Bold):**
> - **1 ton of refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW = 211 kJ/min** (heat extraction rate)
> - **COP (Coefficient of Performance) = Desired Effect / Work Input**
> - **1 hp = 0.7457 kW** (for compressor power calculations)
> - **STP: T = 273.15 K, P = 101.325 kPa**

## 1.4 Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant — Overview

An ideal refrigerant must satisfy thermodynamic, chemical, physical, safety, and environmental criteria simultaneously. A detailed analysis is presented in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Refrigerant Properties

### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Properties

- **Low Boiling Point and Low Freezing Point:** The refrigerant should evaporate at low temperature (typically $-15°C$ to $-40°C$ for commercial applications) and should not freeze at evaporator temperatures.
- **High Latent Heat of Vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** Higher $h_{fg}$ means more heat absorbed per kg circulated, allowing smaller compressor displacement.
- **Low Specific Volume of Vapor:** Lower specific volume at compressor inlet reduces compressor work.
- **Moderate Operating Pressures:** Evaporator pressure should be above atmospheric (to prevent air ingress) but not too high; condenser pressure should be low to avoid heavy piping and high compressor discharge temperatures.
- **High Critical Temperature ($T_c$):** To allow condensation using readily available cooling media (air or water at ambient).
- **High Coefficient of Performance (COP):** Directly tied to thermodynamic efficiency.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Properties

- **Chemical Stability:** Must not decompose under operating temperatures and pressures.
- **Non-Corrosive:** Should not corrode metals (copper, steel, aluminum) used in the system.
- **Non-Reactive with Lubricants:** Must be miscible with the compressor lubricating oil to ensure proper lubrication and oil return.
- **No Toxicity:** Safe for use in occupied spaces.

### 2.1.3 Physical Properties

- **Low Viscosity:** Reduces pressure drop in the system.
- **High Thermal Conductivity:** Enhances heat transfer in evaporator and condenser.
- **Low Surface Tension:** Promotes better wetting of heat transfer surfaces.
- **Non-Flammable / Low Flammability:** Preferred for commercial and residential use.

### 2.1.4 Safety Properties

- **ASHRAE 34 Safety Classification:** Refrigerants are classified by **two capital letters**:
  - **Lowercase letter** = toxicity: `A` (low), `B` (high)
  - **Uppercase letter** = flammability: `1` (no flame propagation), `2L` (mild), `2` (low), `3` (high)

| Class | Toxicity | Flammability | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A1** | Low | No flame propagation | R-134a, R-410A, R-404A |
| **A2L** | Low | Mild flammability | R-32, R-1234yf |
| **A2** | Low | Low flammability | R-152a, R-744 |
| **A3** | Low | High flammability | R-290 (Propane), R-600a (Isobutane) |
| **B1** | High | No flame propagation | R-123 |
| **B2L** | High | Mild flammability | R-717 (Ammonia) |

### 2.1.5 Environmental Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** Relative measure of a substance's ability to deplete stratospheric ozone, with R-11 (CFC-11) defined as **ODP = 1.0** (reference).
>
> **GWP (Global Warming Potential):** Relative measure of a substance's contribution to global warming, with **CO₂ defined as GWP = 1.0** over a 100-year horizon (IPCC AR5 values).

| Refrigerant | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | Atmospheric Lifetime (yr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| R-12 (CFC) | 0.82 | 10,900 | 100 |
| R-22 (HCFC) | 0.055 | 1,810 | 12 |
| R-134a (HFC) | 0 | 1,430 | 14 |
| R-410A | 0 | 2,088 | 15–29 |
| R-404A | 0 | 3,922 | 16.6 |
| R-32 | 0 | 675 | 5.6 |
| R-744 (CO₂) | 0 | 1 | Variable |
| R-717 (NH₃) | 0 | 0 | Days |
| R-290 (Propane) | 0 | ~3 | Weeks |

## 2.2 Refrigerant Nomenclature (ASHRAE Standard 34)

The numbering convention follows the form: **R-XYZ** or **R-XYZW**

$$X = (n_C - 1) \text{ for saturated HCFCs/HFCs with one H replaced}$$

**General rule:**

$$\text{Number} = (n_C - 1) \times 100 + (n_H + 1) + \text{letter for isomers}$$

Where:
- $n_C$ = number of carbon atoms
- $n_H$ = number of hydrogen atoms
- The **last digit** indicates the isomer (symmetry/unsaturation)

**Examples:**
- R-12: $n_C=1, n_H=0, n_F=2$ → Difluorodichloromethane (CCl₂F₂)
- R-22: $n_C=1, n_H=1, n_F=1, n_Cl=1$ → Chlorodifluoromethane (CHClF₂)
- R-134a: $n_C=2, n_H=2, n_F=4$ → Tetrafluoroethane, isomer "a"
- R-744: Atomic mass of CO₂ = 44
- R-717: Molecular weight of NH₃ = 17

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyclical numbering (inorganic compounds):** The number after the decimal = 90 + molecular weight (e.g., R-744 = 90 + 44 = 134 → 7**44** indicates mass and classification).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $COP_{R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator), Carnot | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | COP (Heat Pump), Carnot | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $COP_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$ | Actual COP | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}}$ | Second-Law Efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat of vaporization | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $Q_L = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg,evap}$ | Cooling capacity | kW |
| 7 | $Q_H = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{fg,cond} + c_p \Delta T_{sup})$ | Heat rejection in condenser | kW |
| 8 | $W_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work | kW |
| 9 | $v_{suction} = v_g @ T_{evap}$ | Specific volume at compressor inlet | m³/kg |
| 10 | $V_c = \dot{m} \cdot v_{suction} / \eta_{vol}$ | Compressor displacement volume | m³/s |
| 11 | $\text{TEWI} = \text{Direct Emissions} + \text{Indirect Emissions}$ | Total Equivalent Warming Impact | kg CO₂-eq |
| 12 | $\text{Direct Emissions} = m_{ref} \cdot GWP \cdot \dfrac{1}{L_{life}}$ | Annualized refrigerant release | kg CO₂-eq |
| 13 | $\text{Indirect Emissions} = W_{annual} \cdot EF_{grid}$ | Annual energy-related emissions | kg CO₂-eq |
| 14 | $P_{ideal} = P_{surr} \cdot (P_{disch}/P_{surr})^{k/(k-1)}$ | Isentropic pressure ratio | kPa |
| 15 | $HRC = \dfrac{T_{cond} + 273}{T_{evap} + 273}$ | Heat Rejection Coefficient (Carnot analog) | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Anchor:** For any VCRS cycle, always remember: **COP = Refrigeration Effect / Compressor Work = $Q_L / W_c$**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In **industrial refrigeration**, refrigerant selection directly determines:
1. **Capital cost** of compressors (lower $v_{suction}$ → smaller compressor)
2. **Operating cost** of the plant (higher COP → lower electricity bills)
3. **Safety compliance** (ASME, ASHRAE 15, ATEX for ammonia)
4. **Regulatory compliance** (Montreal Protocol, Kigali Amendment, F-Gas Regulation)
5. **Maintenance cost** (oil return, leak frequency, chemical stability)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Refrigeration Effect and Compressor Work from First Law

### Step 1: State Definitions of the Ideal Vapour Compression Cycle
- **State 1:** Saturated vapor at evaporator outlet
- **State 2:** Superheated vapor at compressor outlet
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser outlet
- **State 4:** Liquid-vapor mixture at evaporator inlet

### Step 2: Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the Evaporator
Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming adiabatic operation:

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4$$

Since throttling (4→1 would actually be throttling 3→4) and State 4 is after throttling from State 3:

$$h_3 = h_4$$

Therefore, the **Refrigeration Effect** is:

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$$

### Step 3: SFEE for the Compressor
Assuming adiabatic, steady-state, no significant KE/PE change:

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

For an isentropic compression (ideal):

$$s_1 = s_2 \implies P_2/P_1 = (T_2/T_1)^{k/(k-1)}$$

### Step 4: SFEE for the Condenser
$$q_H = h_2 - h_3$$

### Step 5: SFEE for the Expansion Valve
The expansion process is isenthalpic (throttling):

$$h_3 = h_4$$

### Step 6: Derive the Coefficient of Performance

$$COP_R = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$$

### Step 7: Derive the Carnot COP for Comparison
The Carnot refrigerator operates between $T_L$ (evaporator) and $T_H$ (condenser) on the absolute temperature scale:

$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_{evap,K}}{T_{cond,K} - T_{evap,K}}$$

### Step 8: Second-Law Efficiency (Exergy-Based Comparison)
$$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \times 100\%$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Problem:** A refrigeration system using R-134a operates between an evaporator temperature of $-10°C$ and a condenser temperature of $40°C$. The refrigerant is dry-saturated at the compressor inlet and subcooled by $5°C$ at the condenser outlet. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = 0.05$ kg/s. Determine:
1. Refrigeration effect (kJ/kg)
2. Compressor work (kJ/kg)
3. COP of the system
4. Compressor discharge temperature

**R-134a Property Data:**

| State | Property | Value |
|---|---|---|
| State 1 ($-10°C$, sat. vapor) | $h_1$ | 244.51 kJ/kg |
| State 1 | $s_1$ | 0.9370 kJ/kg·K |
| State 3 (saturated liquid at $40°C$) | $h_3$ | 256.41 kJ/kg |
| State 3 ($40°C$, subcooled by 5°C) | $h_3'$ | 256.41 − 1.45 × 5 = 249.16 kJ/kg |
| $P_2$ (saturation at $40°C$) | — | 1.0166 MPa |

### Solution:

**Step A — Throttling (3 → 4):**
$$h_3 = h_4 = 249.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step B — Refrigeration Effect:**
$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 244.51 - 249.16 = -4.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative refrigeration effect!** This indicates the subcooling was insufficient. In practice, subcooling of about 5–8°C is needed; let us re-evaluate with no subcooling for a valid scenario.

**Re-evaluated (saturated liquid exit from condenser):**
- $h_3 = 256.41$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at $40°C$)
- $h_4 = h_3 = 256.41$ kJ/kg
- $RE = 244.51 - 256.41 = -11.9$ kJ/kg

This is still negative because the enthalpies in the reference table happen to give this result for these conditions. In real KTU problems, the property tables are carefully chosen so that the answer is positive. Let us use proper R-134a values from a standard table:

**Corrected R-134a values (R-134a saturated tables):**
- $h_1$ at $-10°C$ (sat. vapor) = **392.31 kJ/kg**, $s_1$ = **1.7335 kJ/kg·K**
- $h_3$ at $40°C$ (sat. liquid) = **256.41 kJ/kg**
- $h_g$ at $40°C$ = **419.43 kJ/kg**
- $s_g$ at $40°C$ = **1.7111 kJ/kg·K**

**Step A — Subcooled liquid enthalpy at $35°C$:** Approximating with saturated liquid at $35°C$:

$$h_3 \approx h_f(35°C) = 246.69 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step B — Throttling (3 → 4):**
$$h_3 = h_4 = 246.69 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step C — Refrigeration Effect:**
$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392.31 - 246.69 = 145.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step D — Cooling Capacity:**
$$Q_L = \dot{m} \times RE = 0.05 \times 145.62 = 7.281 \text{ kW}$$

**Step E — Isentropic Compression (1 → 2):** $s_2 = s_1 = 1.7335$ kJ/kg·K at $P_2 = 1.0166$ MPa. From superheated tables:

At $P = 1.0$ MPa and $s = 1.7335$ kJ/kg·K, interpolating between $40°C$ ($s = 1.7111$) and $50°C$ ($s = 1.7467$):

$$T_2 = 40 + \frac{1.7335 - 1.7111}{1.7467 - 1.7111} \times 10 = 40 + 6.27 = 46.27°C$$

$$h_2 = 419.43 + \frac{1.7335 - 1.7111}{1.7467 - 1.7111} \times (430.10 - 419.43) = 419.43 + 6.27 = 425.70 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step F — Compressor Work:**
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 425.70 - 392.31 = 33.39 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step G — Compressor Power:**
$$W_c = \dot{m} \times w_c = 0.05 \times 33.39 = 1.670 \text{ kW}$$

**Step H — COP:**
$$COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{145.62}{33.39} = 4.36$$

**Step I — Carnot COP for Comparison:**
$$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50} = 5.263$$

**Step J — Second-Law Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{II} = \frac{4.36}{5.263} \times 100 = 82.84\%$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Refrigerant Selection Decision Tree

| Decision Criterion | Weight | R-12 | R-22 | R-134a | R-410A | R-717 | R-744 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ODP (lower better) | 25% | ✗ (0.82) | △ (0.055) | ✓ (0) | ✓ (0) | ✓ (0) | ✓ (0) |
| GWP (lower better) | 25% | ✗ (10900) | ✗ (1810) | ✗ (1430) | ✗ (2088) | ✓ (0) | △ (1) |
| COP (higher better) | 20% | High | High | Med | High | Very High | Med |
| Toxicity (lower better) | 10% | Low | Low | Low | Low | High | Low |
| Flammability (lower better) | 10% | None | None | None | None | Mild | None |
| Cost | 10% | Low | Med | Med | Med | Low | High (P) |

**Notation:** ✓ = Good, △ = Moderate, ✗ = Poor, (P) = Pressure penalty
