# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the term 'Refrigerant' and list any four desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant.**

**Model Answer:**
A refrigerant is a working substance that circulates through a refrigeration cycle, absorbing heat at low temperature by evaporating and rejecting heat at high temperature by condensing. It is the "blood" of the refrigeration system.

Four desirable properties:
1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure to enable evaporation at low temperatures.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** for higher refrigeration effect per kg.
3. **Zero ODP and low GWP** to comply with environmental regulations.
4. **Non-toxic, non-flammable** to ensure safe operation in occupied spaces (ASHRAE Class A1).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Explain the difference between Azeotropic and Zeotropic refrigerant blends with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Azeotropic Blend | Zeotropic Blend |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature Glide | Zero (negligible < 0.5°C) | Significant (3 to 10°C) |
| Composition on Leakage | Constant | Changes (fractionation) |
| Behaviour | Like pure refrigerant | Like mixture |
| Phase Change | At constant temperature | Over a temperature range |
| Examples | R-502, R-507, R-410A (near) | R-404A, R-407C, R-409A |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the desirable **thermodynamic, chemical, physical, safety, and environmental properties** of an ideal refrigerant. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A refrigeration system uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator temperature is $-15°C$ and the condenser temperature is $35°C$. The refrigerant is **dry saturated** at the compressor inlet and **subcooled by 5°C** at the condenser exit. The mass flow rate of refrigerant is **0.08 kg/s**. Calculate: (i) Refrigeration effect, (ii) Compressor work, (iii) COP of the system, (iv) Compressor discharge temperature. Take the isentropic compression assumption. (7 Marks)

**Property Data for R-134a:**

| State | Property | Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 ($-15°C$, sat. vapor) | $h_1$ | 389.20 kJ/kg, $s_1$ = 1.7270 kJ/kg·K |
| Sat. liquid at $35°C$ | $h_f$ | 249.69 kJ/kg, $h_g$ | 417.00 kJ/kg |
| 3 (subcooled 5°C) | $h_3$ | 241.04 kJ/kg |
| 2 (superheated, 1.0 MPa) | at 40°C, $h$ = 419.43 kJ/kg, $s$ = 1.7111 kJ/kg·K |
| | at 50°C, $h$ = 430.10 kJ/kg, $s$ = 1.7467 kJ/kg·K |

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Properties:**
*(Answer covered in Section 2.1. Provide a structured response with 5 sub-headings: Thermodynamic, Chemical, Physical, Safety, Environmental. Award 1.4 marks per category.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Step 1 — Identify state enthalpies:**
- $h_1 = 389.20$ kJ/kg (dry sat. vapor at $-15°C$)
- $h_3 = 241.04$ kJ/kg (subcooled by 5°C below $35°C$ saturation)

**Step 2 — Throttling (3→4):**
$$h_3 = h_4 = 241.04 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Refrigeration Effect:**
$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 389.20 - 241.04 = 148.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Cooling Capacity:**
$$Q_L = \dot{m} \times RE = 0.08 \times 148.16 = 11.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compressor outlet state (2):** Saturation pressure at $35°C$ is $P_2 \approx 0.888$ MPa ≈ 1.0 MPa (using given superheated table). At $P_2 = 1.0$ MPa, $s_2 = s_1 = 1.7270$ kJ/kg·K. Interpolating:

$$T_2 = 40 + \frac{1.7270 - 1.7111}{1.7467 - 1.7111} \times 10 = 40 + 4.46 = 44.46°C$$

$$h_2 = 419.43 + \frac{1.7270 - 1.7111}{1.7467 - 1.7111} \times (430.10 - 419.43) = 419.43 + 4.77 = 424.20 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Compressor Work:**
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 424.20 - 389.20 = 35.00 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 7 — Compressor Power:**
$$W_c = 0.08 \times 35.00 = 2.80 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — COP:**
$$COP = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{148.16}{35.00} = 4.23$$

**Step 9 — Heat Rejected:**
$$Q_H = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3) = 0.08 \times (424.20 - 241.04) = 14.65 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 10 — Verification (Energy Balance):**
$$Q_H - Q_L = 14.65 - 11.85 = 2.80 \text{ kW} = W_c \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** Define **ODP** and **GWP**. Explain the **Montreal Protocol** and **Kigali Amendment** in the context of refrigerant selection. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Compare the following refrigerant pairs based on the given criteria and recommend the most suitable for the application: (i) R-12 vs R-134a for domestic refrigerator, (ii) R-22 vs R-410A for residential air conditioner, (iii) R-134a vs R-744 for automotive AC, (iv) R-717 vs R-22 for cold storage warehouse. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Protocols:**

**ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** A relative index of a refrigerant's ability to destroy stratospheric ozone, with CFC-11 (R-11) defined as the reference with ODP = 1.0.

**GWP (Global Warming Potential):** A relative measure of a refrigerant's contribution to global warming over a 100-year horizon, with CO₂ as reference (GWP = 1.0).

**Montreal Protocol (1987):** International treaty signed to phase out **CFCs and later HCFCs** due to their ODP. India's commitments: complete phaseout of CFCs (1996 for developing countries was 2010), phaseout of HCFCs by 2030 (under HPMP stage I, II, III).

**Kigali Amendment (2016):** Amendment to Montreal Protocol to phase down **high-GWP HFCs** under the "F-gas" framework. India committed to:
- Baseline freeze in 2024–2026
- 10% reduction by 2029
- 80% reduction by 2045
- 85% reduction by 2047

**Part (b) — Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Pair | Comparison | Recommendation | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) R-12 vs R-134a for domestic refrigerator | R-12: ODP=0.82, GWP=10900, A1; R-134a: ODP=0, GWP=1430, A1 | **R-134a** | Zero ODP, lower GWP, same safety class, similar capacity. R-12 is fully banned. |
| (ii) R-22 vs R-410A for residential AC | R-22: ODP=0.055, GWP=1810, A1; R-410A: ODP=0, GWP=2088, A1 | **R-410A** (or R-32) | R-22 phased out; R-410A is drop-in replacement with better capacity, though R-32 is even better with GWP=675. |
| (iii) R-134a vs R-744 for automotive AC | R-134a: GWP=1430; R-744 (CO₂): GWP=1 | **R-744 (CO₂)** | Lower GWP, natural refrigerant, used in EU under MAC Directive (2006/40/EC). Requires high-pressure system. |
| (iv) R-717 vs R-22 for cold storage | R-717 (NH₃): GWP=0, toxic (B2L), very high COP; R-22: GWP=1810, banned | **R-717 (Ammonia)** | Zero GWP, highest COP, low operating cost. Toxicity managed with proper engineering. R-22 phased out. |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** in Carnot COP calculation. This is a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Using saturated liquid enthalpy** without accounting for subcooling — the cooling effect $h_1 - h_3$ changes by 1.45 kJ/kg per °C of subcooling for R-134a.
> 3. **Mixing up R-22 and R-410A** in tables — R-22 is HCFC (ODP>0), R-410A is HFC blend (ODP=0).
> 4. **Not stating ASHRAE safety class** (A1, A2L, A3, B1, B2L) when describing a refrigerant — the KTU 2024 marking scheme awards 1 mark for safety classification.
> 5. **Confusing azeotropic and zeotropic blends** — R-404A is zeotropic (not azeotropic); R-410A is classified as near-azeotropic.
> 6. **Neglecting to verify energy balance** $Q_H = Q_L + W_c$ — a quick check that catches sign errors.
> 7. **Mixing specific heat in kJ/kg·K and J/kg·K** — common in subcooling calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-Density Summary — Refrigerants**

- 🔹 **Refrigerant = Working fluid of VCRS / VARS**, undergoes phase change in the cycle.
- 🔹 **Primary refrigerant** = phase-change fluid (e.g., R-134a, R-717, R-744).
- 🔹 **Secondary refrigerant** = sensible heat carrier (e.g., brine, glycol, water).
- 🔹 **Five property categories** of ideal refrigerants: Thermodynamic, Chemical, Physical, Safety, Environmental.
- 🔹 **ASHRAE 34 Safety Class** format: A=low toxicity, B=high toxicity; 1=non-flammable, 2L=mild, 2=low, 3=high flammability.
- 🔹 **ODP** reference is **R-11 (CFC-11) = 1.0**; ODP is zero for all HFCs, HFOs, and natural refrigerants.
- 🔹 **GWP** reference is **CO₂ = 1.0**; GWP of R-12 = 10,900 (worst); R-744 (CO₂) and R-717 (NH₃) have negligible GWP.
- 🔹 **Refrigerant number rule:** $R_{n_1 n_2 n_3} = (n_C - 1) \times 100 + (n_H + 1) + \text{isomer digit}$; for inorganics use $90 + MW$ → e.g., R-744, R-717, R-718 (water), R-729 (air).
- 🔹 **Azeotropic blend** = behaves as pure fluid, zero temperature glide, no fractionation. Examples: R-502, R-507.
- 🔹 **Zeotropic blend** = shows temperature glide (3–10°C), undergoes fractionation on leakage. Examples: R-404A, R-407C, R-409A.
- 🔹 **Near-azeotropic** = small glide < 1°C, e.g., R-410A.
- 🔹 **COP formula** (Vapour Compression): $COP_R = (h_1 - h_4) / (h_2 - h_1)$.
- 🔹 **Carnot COP** = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — always use **Kelvin**.
- 🔹 **Subcooling** increases refrigeration effect linearly: $\Delta RE = c_{p,liq} \times \Delta T_{sub}$.
- 🔹 **Superheating** at compressor inlet requires care: useful for preventing liquid slugging, but increases compressor work.
- 🔹 **Refrigerant capacity per kg** ∝ latent heat $h_{fg}$, so high $h_{fg}$ = smaller system.
- 🔹 **Compressor power** ∝ specific volume $v_g$ at suction, so low $v_g$ = lower power.
- 🔹 **Natural refrigerants** (CO₂, NH₃, hydrocarbons) are eco-friendly but have safety or pressure challenges.
- 🔹 **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out CFCs; **Kigali Amendment (2016)** is phasing down high-GWP HFCs.
- 🔹 **R-22 phaseout** in developing countries including India: complete ban by **2030** (for new equipment) and **2040** (for servicing).
- 🔹 **Domestic refrigerator trend:** R-12 → R-134a → R-600a (isobutane, A3, GWP=3).
- 🔹 **AC trend:** R-22 → R-410A → R-32 (GWP=675) → R-290 (propane, A3) → R-1234yf (HFO).
- 🔹 **Industrial cold storage trend:** R-22 → R-717 (ammonia) → cascade with R-744.
- 🔹 **TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact)** is the modern metric for comparing refrigerants including energy emissions.
- 🔹 **GWP-weighted emissions** = mass leaked (kg/yr) × GWP × (1/lifetime) — used in F-gas regulations.
- 🔹 **Mass flow rate of refrigerant**: $\dot{m} = Q_L / RE = (Cooling\ Capacity\ in\ kW) / (Refrigeration\ Effect\ in\ kJ/kg)$.
- 🔹 **Energy balance check:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_c$ — must always balance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ammonia-water System, Lithium Bromide-water Systems, Electrolux Systems, Comparison with Vapour Compression Systems and Drawbacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ammonia-water System, Lithium Bromide-water Systems, Electrolux Systems, Comparison with Vapour Compression Systems and Drawbacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Absorption Refrigeration — Formal Definition

**Absorption Refrigeration** is a thermally-driven refrigeration process in which the mechanical work input of the conventional vapour compression cycle is replaced by a **thermal energy input** and a small amount of pumping work. The cycle achieves cooling by exploiting the affinity of a **refrigerant-absorbent pair**, where the refrigerant is alternately absorbed into and desorbed (boiled out) from a secondary fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PEIET412 focuses on the three principal absorption systems — the **Ammonia-Water (NH₃–H₂O)** system, the **Lithium Bromide-Water (LiBr–H₂O)** system, and the **Electrolux (NH₃–H₂O–H₂) three-fluid** system. Comparison with Vapour Compression and the inherent drawbacks of absorption technology are also examinable.

### 1.2 Working Pair Terminology

- **Refrigerant:** The fluid that actually produces the cooling effect by evaporating at low temperature and pressure.
- **Absorbent (or Absorber fluid):** The secondary fluid that readily dissolves the refrigerant at low pressure and releases it upon heating.
- **Weak Solution:** Liquid mixture in the absorber with a **low concentration of refrigerant** (mostly absorbent).
- **Strong Solution:** Liquid mixture in the generator with a **high concentration of refrigerant** (rich mixture leaving absorber).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and Water" Model

Imagine a **dry sponge** (the absorbent) and a **cup of water** (the refrigerant). When you place the sponge on the water, it **absorbs** the water naturally (no pump needed) — this is the **absorber**. Now, if you **heat** the wet sponge over a flame, the water **boils out** as vapour — this is the **generator**. The vapour is then **condensed** (cooling the steam back to liquid), **expanded** (pressure drop), and allowed to **evaporate** (absorbing heat from the cold space). The dry sponge is then ready to absorb more refrigerant. The only "mechanical" work needed is to lift the now-dry sponge back to the water tank — analogous to the **solution pump** in an absorption system.

> [!NOTE]
> The compressor in a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) is replaced by an **absorber + pump + generator + rectifier** assembly. This is the central engineering substitution in absorption technology.

### 1.4 Why Three Different Systems?

Different working pairs are optimized for different temperature ranges and applications. The KTU syllabus expects students to recognize that:

- **NH₃–H₂O:** High-pressure pair, produces refrigeration **below 0 °C** (used for industrial cold storage, ice plants).
- **LiBr–H₂O:** Low-pressure pair, produces refrigeration **above 0 °C** (used for air-conditioning only).
- **Electrolux (3-fluid):** Domestic refrigerator technology, hermetically sealed, **no moving parts**.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics in Absorption Refrigeration

- **COP (Coefficient of Performance):** Ratio of refrigeration effect to total energy input (heat + work).
- **Crystallization Temperature:** The temperature below which the absorbent precipitates out of solution, damaging equipment.
- **Heat Input Ratio (HIR):** Ratio of generator heat to refrigeration effect.
- **Generator Temperature (T_g):** **Typically 80–200 °C** depending on the working pair.
- **Standard COP range for NH₃–H₂O systems:** **0.4 – 0.7** (single-effect).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Simple Ammonia-Water Absorption System — Step-by-Step Logic

**Components:** Absorber, Solution Pump, Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator, and **Rectifier**.

**Cycle of Operations (14 stages of state change):**

1. **State 1 — Saturated ammonia vapour** leaves the evaporator at low pressure P_e and temperature T_e.
2. **State 2 — Vapour enters the absorber**, gets dissolved in weak solution, forming **strong (rich) solution**.
3. **State 3 — Strong solution is pumped** by the solution pump from low pressure P_a to high pressure P_g. Work input = W_pump.
4. **State 4 — High-pressure strong solution enters the generator**, where external heat Q_g is supplied.
5. **State 5 — Ammonia vapour (with some water vapour)** boils off and rises to the **rectifier**.
6. **State 6 — Rectified pure ammonia vapour** leaves the rectifier at high pressure P_g.
7. **State 7 — Ammonia vapour condenses** in the condenser by rejecting heat Q_c to the surroundings.
8. **State 8 — High-pressure liquid ammonia** leaves the condenser.
9. **State 9 — Liquid ammonia is throttled** through the expansion valve to low pressure P_e.
10. **State 10 — Low-pressure, low-temperature liquid ammonia** enters the evaporator.
11. **State 11 — Liquid ammonia evaporates**, absorbing heat Q_e from the cold space.
12. **State 12 — Weak (lean) solution** leaves the generator and passes through a heat exchanger.
13. **State 13 — Cooled weak solution** enters the absorber to repeat the cycle.
14. **State 14 — Heat rejection Q_a** occurs in the absorber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **rectifier** in an NH₃–H₂O system is critical because ammonia and water have a **finite volatility difference** — water vapour tends to co-distil with ammonia and must be removed by reflux condensation, otherwise it would freeze in the evaporator.

### 2.2 The Simple Lithium Bromide-Water System

- **Refrigerant:** Water (H₂O) — evaporates at low pressure and temperature.
- **Absorbent:** Lithium Bromide (LiBr) — a hygroscopic salt.
- **Operating Pressure:** **Sub-atmospheric** (typically 6–10 mmHg in the evaporator).
- **Rectifier:** **Not required** because LiBr is non-volatile; only water vapour rises.
- **COP:** **0.6 – 0.8** (slightly higher than NH₃–H₂O).
- **Limitation:** Crystallization occurs if solution temperature drops below **about 25–30 °C** at the absorber outlet. Hot-water, steam, or direct-fire generators are used.

**Modifications to improve COP:**
- **Double-effect LiBr–H₂O chiller:** Uses a second generator at higher pressure, achieving **COP ≈ 1.2**.
- **Triple-effect:** Theoretically possible, rarely commercial.

### 2.3 The Electrolux Three-Fluid Absorption System

Invented by Baltzar von Platen and Carl Munters (1925), commercialized by AB Arctic (later Electrolux).

**Working Fluids:**
- **Ammonia (NH₃):** Refrigerant
- **Water (H₂O):** Absorbent
- **Hydrogen (H₂):** Inert auxiliary gas that enables diffusion-based mass transfer

**Key Innovation — No Moving Parts:**
- **No solution pump** (replaced by bubble pump / thermosiphon effect in the generator).
- **No expansion valve** (replaced by hydrogen gas buffer at low total pressure).
- **Only moving part:** A small flame under the generator.

**Mechanism of the "Bubble Pump":**
When the generator is heated, the strong ammonia-water solution boils. The **ammonia-rich vapour bubbles rise** through a vertical pipe, carrying liquid with them by **momentum transfer** — this lifts the liquid without any mechanical pump.

> [!NOTE]
> The hydrogen gas in the evaporator side acts as a **partial-pressure barrier**: total pressure is uniform throughout the system, but the **partial pressure of ammonia** in the evaporator is very low, allowing ammonia to evaporate at the cold temperature even though the system is at high total pressure.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Symbol | Expression | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{COP}_{\text{abs}}$ | $\dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_{\text{pump}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of absorption system | dimensionless |
| $Q_e$ | $\dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration effect (evaporator load) | kW |
| $Q_g$ | $\dot{m}_g (h_4 - h_3) + \dot{m}_r (h_6 - h_5)$ | Generator heat input | kW |
| $Q_c$ | $\dot{m}_r (h_6 - h_5)$ | Condenser heat rejection | kW |
| $Q_a$ | $\dot{m}_g (h_2 - h_1) + \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_8)$ | Absorber heat rejection | kW |
| $\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}}$ | $\dfrac{T_e (T_g - T_a)}{T_g (T_a - T_e)}$ | Maximum (reversible) COP of absorption cycle | dimensionless |
| $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$ | $\dfrac{T_e}{T_a - T_e}$ | COP of ideal Carnot refrigerator | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{\text{II}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}}}$ | Second-law efficiency | dimensionless |
| $\text{PR}$ | $\dfrac{P_{\text{cond}}}{P_{\text{evap}}}$ | Pressure ratio (for compressor work comparison) | dimensionless |
| $\dot{m}_g / \dot{m}_r$ | $f$ | Solution-to-refrigerant mass flow ratio (circulation ratio) | dimensionless |
| $\Delta x$ | $x_1 - x_2$ | Concentration difference driving the cycle | kg NH₃/kg solution |

> [!WARNING]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in tables. **Never use `|x|` directly** as it breaks the markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Ammonia-Water:** Large cold storage warehouses, ice rinks, food processing (Beverages, dairy, fish freezing).
- **Lithium Bromide-Water:** Commercial building air-conditioning, hospitals, hotels, district cooling.
- **Electrolux:** Domestic refrigerators, mobile cooling units, off-grid refrigeration powered by solar or waste heat.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of COP of a Simple Ammonia-Water Absorption System

**Given:**
- Refrigerant mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_r$ (kg/s)
- Strong-solution mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_g$ (kg/s)
- Weak-solution mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_w$ (kg/s)
- Mass balance in absorber: $\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_w$
- Enthalpy at each state as per cycle diagram

**Step 1: Energy balance on the Evaporator**

The refrigeration effect is the heat absorbed by the refrigerant as it evaporates from state 9 to state 1.

$$Q_e = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_9)$$

**Step 2: Energy balance on the Absorber**

Heat rejected by the absorber equals the enthalpy gained by the solution plus the enthalpy gained by the absorbed refrigerant.

$$Q_a = \dot{m}_g h_2 - (\dot{m}_r h_1 + \dot{m}_w h_{13})$$

After simplification using mass balance:

$$Q_a = \dot{m}_g (h_2 - h_{13}) - \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_{13})$$

**Step 3: Energy balance on the Solution Pump**

$$W_{\text{pump}} = \dot{m}_g (h_3 - h_2)$$

Since the pump work is small compared to $Q_g$, it is often neglected in simplified analysis.

**Step 4: Energy balance on the Generator**

Heat input at the generator boils off refrigerant from the strong solution.

$$Q_g = \dot{m}_r h_6 + \dot{m}_w h_4 - \dot{m}_g h_3$$

Substituting $\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_w$:

$$Q_g = \dot{m}_r (h_6 - h_4) + \dot{m}_g (h_4 - h_3)$$

**Step 5: Energy balance on the Condenser**

$$Q_c = \dot{m}_r (h_6 - h_5)$$

**Step 6: Final COP Expression**

By the definition of COP:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{abs}} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_{\text{pump}}}$$

Substituting the expressions derived above:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{abs}} = \frac{\dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_9)}{\dot{m}_r (h_6 - h_4) + \dot{m}_g (h_4 - h_3) + \dot{m}_g (h_3 - h_2)}$$

When pump work is neglected ($h_3 \approx h_2$):

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{abs}} = \frac{h_1 - h_9}{(h_6 - h_4) + (\dot{m}_g / \dot{m}_r)(h_4 - h_3)}}$$

This is the **KTU board examiner's expected form** for full-mark answers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum (Reversible) COP

For a reversible absorption cycle operating between three temperature reservoirs (T_e, T_a, T_g), the maximum COP is obtained by combining two Carnot cycles.

**Step 1:** Consider a Carnot refrigerator between T_e and T_a.

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,R}} = \frac{T_e}{T_a - T_e}$$

**Step 2:** Consider a Carnot heat pump between T_g and T_a (driving the generator-absorber loop).

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,HP}} = \frac{T_g}{T_g - T_a}$$

**Step 3:** The total work consumed by the reversible cycle is the net of the two Carnot machines.

$$W_{\text{net}} = Q_e \left(\frac{T_a - T_e}{T_e}\right) - Q_g \left(\frac{T_g - T_a}{T_g}\right) \cdot \text{(factor)}$$

**Step 4:** Applying energy balance, the reversible COP becomes:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_e \cdot (T_g - T_a)}{T_g \cdot (T_a - T_e)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{max,abs}} = \left(\frac{T_e}{T_a - T_e}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{T_g - T_a}{T_g}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** The **product form** makes the physical meaning clear:
- First factor: Carnot refrigeration COP (cold space).
- Second factor: Thermal efficiency of heat-driven pump (generator–absorber).
- The actual COP is always less than this maximum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU typical):** If T_e = 263 K (−10 °C), T_a = 308 K (35 °C), T_g = 388 K (115 °C):
> - $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = (263/45) \times (80/388) = 5.844 \times 0.2062 = 1.205$
> - Actual COP ≈ 0.5 → Second-law efficiency ≈ 41 %.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Working Pairs

| Property | NH₃–H₂O System | LiBr–H₂O System | Electrolux (3-fluid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | Ammonia (NH₃) |
| Absorbent | Water (H₂O) | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) | Water (H₂O) + Hydrogen (H₂) |
| Operating Pressure | High (10–20 bar) | Sub-atmospheric (0.01 bar) | Moderate (15–20 bar) |
| Evaporator Temperature | **Below 0 °C** possible | **Above 0 °C only** | **Below 0 °C** possible |
| Toxicity | Ammonia is **toxic** above 25 ppm | LiBr is **non-toxic** | Ammonia sealed — risk on leak |
| Crystallization Risk | Low | **High (≈ 30 °C limit)** | Low |
| Rectifier Required | **Yes** | No | Internal |
| Moving Parts | Pump + valves | Pump + valves | **None** |
| Typical COP | 0.4 – 0.7 | 0.6 – 0.8 (single), 1.0 – 1.3 (double) | 0.2 – 0.4 |
| Primary Application | Industrial cold storage | Air-conditioning | Domestic refrigeration |
| Heat Source | Steam, hot water, gas | Steam, hot water, gas, solar | LPG, kerosene, electricity |

---

### 3.4 Comparison: Absorption vs Vapour Compression

| Parameter | Vapour Compression (VCRS) | Absorption System |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Input | Mechanical (compressor) | **Thermal + small mechanical** |
| Prime Mover | Electric motor | Heat source (steam, flame, solar) |
| COP | **2.5 – 4.5** (typical) | 0.4 – 1.3 |
| Moving Parts | Compressor, expansion valve | Pump, expansion valve (or none) |
| Noise Level | High | **Very low** (almost silent) |
| Maintenance | Regular (compressor wear) | **Very low** (no compressor) |
| Capital Cost | Lower | **Higher** (heat exchangers large) |
| Operating Cost | High (electricity) | Low (waste heat usable) |
| Refrigerant Charge | Small | **Large** (solution) |
| Suitability for Solar | Poor | **Excellent** |
| Toxicity of Refrigerant | HFC/HCFC — environmental concern | NH₃ is natural; LiBr is salt |
| Capacity Range | 0.5 kW – 10 MW | **10 kW – 5000 TR** (industrial) |

---

### 3.5 Drawbacks of Absorption Refrigeration Systems

1. **Low COP:** Always less than the corresponding VCRS — requires more heat input per kW of cooling.
2. **Large Heat Exchanger Area:** The solution circulation rate is **5–10 times** the refrigerant flow rate → bulky absorber and generator.
3. **Crystallization (LiBr–H₂O):** Solution freezes in tubes if not carefully controlled, causing permanent damage.
4. **Bulky Equipment:** Heat exchangers 2–3 times larger than VCRS condensers and evaporators.
5. **High Initial Cost:** Capital investment is higher than VCRS of equivalent capacity.
6. **Slow Response to Load Changes:** Thermal inertia of the generator causes delayed cooling.
7. **Pumping Power Penalty:** Solution pump consumes additional electricity (10–15 % of total input).
8. **Rectifier Required (NH₃–H₂O):** Adds complexity, cost, and irreversibilities.
9. **Ammonia Toxicity:** Even small leaks of NH₃ can be hazardous, requiring careful sealing and detection systems.
10. **Sub-atmospheric Leakage (LiBr–H₂O):** Air ingress into sub-atmospheric system degrades performance and causes corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Simple Ammonia-Water Absorption Refrigeration System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1([Evaporator]) --Saturated NH3 vapor, State 1--> A1([Absorber])
    A1 --Rich solution, State 2--> P1([Solution Pump])
    P1 --High pressure rich sol, State 3--> HX1([Heat Exchanger])
    HX1 --Preheated rich sol, State 3a--> G1([Generator])
    G1 --NH3 + water vapor, State 5--> R1([Rectifier])
    R1 --Pure NH3 vapor, State 6--> C1([Condenser])
    C1 --Liquid NH3, State 7--> V1([Expansion Valve])
    V1 --Low P liquid NH3, State 8--> E1
    G1 --Weak solution, State 4--> HX1
    HX1 --Cooled weak solution, State 13--> A1
    C1 -.Heat Qc.-> ENV1((Cooling Tower))
    A1 -.Heat Qa.-> ENV1
    G1 -.Heat Qg.-> SRC1((Steam/Heater))
    E1 -.Refrigeration Qe.-> CLD1((Cold Space))
```

### 4.2 Simple Lithium Bromide-Water Absorption Chiller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EV1([Evaporator]) --Water vapor, low P--> AB1([Absorber])
    AB1 --LiBr weak solution enters--> AB1
    AB1 --Strong LiBr sol, State 2--> SP1([Solution Pump])
    SP1 --High P strong sol--> SH1([Shell and Tube HX])
    SH1 --Preheated strong sol--> GN1([Generator])
    GN1 --Pure water vapor, State 5--> CD1([Condenser])
    CD1 --Liquid water, State 6--> XV1([Expansion Valve])
    XV1 --Low P cold water--> EV1
    GN1 --LiBr weak sol, State 4--> SH1
    SH1 --Cooled weak sol--> AB1
    CD1 -.Qc.-> CW1((Cooling Water))
    AB1 -.Qa.-> CW1
    GN1 -.Qg.-> ST1((Steam Source))
    EV1 -.Qe.-> CH1((Chilled Water to Building))
```

### 4.3 Electrolux Three-Fluid Absorption Refrigerator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GN2([Generator]) --NH3 rich vapor rises--> BP1([Bubble Pump Riser])
    BP1 --NH3 + H2O vapor--> RT2([Rectifier Analyzer])
    RT2 --Pure NH3 vapor top, State 1--> CD2([Condenser Coil])
    CD2 --Liquid NH3, State 2--> EV2([Evaporator Cold Space with H2 gas])
    EV2 --NH3 evaporates, absorbs heat--> EV2
    EV2 --NH3 + H2 mixture, State 3--> AB2([Absorber])
    AB2 --NH3 absorbed in water--> AB2
    AB2 --Weak solution returns, State 5--> GN2
    AB2 --H2 gas returns, State 4--> EV2
    GN2 -.Qg.-> FL1((LPG or Kerosene Flame))
    CD2 -.Qc.-> RO1((Room Air))
    EV2 -.Qe.-> FR1((Food Compartment))
```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture: Absorption vs Vapour Compression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VCRS["Vapour Compression"]
        direction LR
        V1([Evaporator]) --> V2([Compressor Work Wc]) --> V3([Condenser Qc]) --> V4([Expansion Valve]) --> V1
    end

    subgraph ABSSYS["Absorption System"]
        direction LR
        A1A([Evaporator]) --> A2A([Absorber Qa]) --> A3A([Solution Pump Wp]) --> A4A([Generator Qg]) --> A5A([Condenser Qc]) --> A6A([Expansion Valve]) --> A1A
    end

    VCRS -.Uses.- EM1((Electricity))
    ABSSYS -.Uses.- HS1((Heat Source: Steam, Solar, Waste Heat))
```

### 4.5 Enthalpy–Concentration (h–x) Diagram for NH₃–H₂O (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HX([h-x Diagram for NH3-H2O])
    HX --> N1[State 3: Strong solution, high P, x = 0.4]
    HX --> N2[State 4: Weak solution, high P, x = 0.2]
    HX --> N3[State 6: Saturated NH3 vapor, high P]
    HX --> N4[State 8: Liquid NH3 after expansion, low P]
    HX --> N5[State 2: Rich solution after absorber, low P]
    N1 --Heat Qg, vertical to N3--> N3
    N3 --Condenser Qc, vertical to N4--> N4
    N4 --Evaporator, vertical to N2--> N2
    N2 --Pump, vertical to N1--> N1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the h–x diagram, the **vertical line from State 3 to State 6** represents the generator heat input where pure ammonia vapour leaves the solution. The **slope of the tie lines** between liquid and vapour phases determines the rectifier design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]**
*List any three advantages of an absorption refrigeration system over a vapour compression refrigeration system.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **No compressor required** → Less mechanical wear, lower noise (suitable for libraries, hospitals, and domestic use).
2. **Operates on heat input** → Can utilize waste heat, solar energy, geothermal energy, or process steam → lower running cost where heat is free or cheap.
3. **Fewer moving parts** → Lower maintenance cost and longer equipment life.
4. (Bonus) **Environmentally friendly** working fluids (NH₃, H₂O, LiBr) → no CFCs or HCFCs.

*Each advantage: 1 mark. Total: 3 marks.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
*What is the function of a rectifier in an ammonia-water absorption system?*

**Model Answer:**

In an NH₃–H₂O system, ammonia and water both vaporize in the generator. The **rectifier** (also called analyzer) is a partial condenser at the top of the generator. Its function is to:
1. **Condense and reflux the water vapour** back into the generator (using a reflux condenser coil cooled by water or ammonia).
2. **Permit only pure ammonia vapour** to leave towards the condenser.

This ensures that **water does not enter the evaporator**, where it would freeze and block the tubes. The rectifier thus provides pure ammonia to the refrigeration cycle, protecting the evaporator and improving cycle efficiency.

*Function: 2 marks. Significance: 1 mark. Total: 3 marks.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions provide **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Both questions carry equal marks (14) and have two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the working of a simple ammonia-water absorption refrigeration system. Derive an expression for its COP and discuss the function of each component. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Working Principle and Schematic — 7 Marks

**Components and their functions:**

1. **Evaporator (Q_e):** Low-pressure, low-temperature liquid ammonia evaporates by absorbing heat from the cold space.
2. **Absorber (Q_a):** Ammonia vapour is dissolved in weak (lean) ammonia-water solution to form strong (rich) solution. Heat is rejected to cooling water.
3. **Solution Pump (W_p):** Lifts the strong solution from low absorber pressure to high generator pressure.
4. **Generator (Q_g):** External heat (steam/gas) drives ammonia out of the strong solution.
5. **Rectifier:** Removes water vapour from ammonia vapour using reflux condensation — delivers pure NH₃ to condenser.
6. **Condenser (Q_c):** High-pressure ammonia vapour rejects heat to cooling medium and condenses.
7. **Expansion Valve:** Throttles high-pressure liquid ammonia to evaporator pressure.

*(Diagram: 3 marks, Explanation: 4 marks)*

#### Part (b): COP Derivation — 7 Marks

Energy balances on the four key components:

**Evaporator:**
$$Q_e = \dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)$$

**Absorber:**
$$Q_a = \dot{m}_g h_2 - \dot{m}_r h_1 - \dot{m}_w h_{13}$$

**Generator:**
$$Q_g = \dot{m}_r h_6 + \dot{m}_w h_4 - \dot{m}_g h_3$$

**Condenser:**
$$Q_c = \dot{m}_r (h_6 - h_5)$$

**Solution Pump:**
$$W_p = \dot{m}_g (h_3 - h_2)$$

**Definition:**
$$\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p} = \frac{\dot{m}_r (h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}_r(h_6 - h_4) + \dot{m}_g(h_4 - h_3) + \dot{m}_g(h_3 - h_2)}}$$

For **maximum (reversible) COP**:
$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \frac{T_e (T_g - T_a)}{T_g (T_a - T_e)}}$$

*(Energy balance: 4 marks, Final expression: 2 marks, Max COP: 1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — Alternative Choice]

**Explain the construction and working of the Electrolux three-fluid absorption refrigeration system. Compare absorption systems with vapour compression systems and list five drawbacks of absorption refrigeration. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a): Electrolux System Description — 7 Marks

**Working Fluids:**
- **Ammonia (NH₃):** Refrigerant
- **Water (H₂O):** Absorbent
- **Hydrogen (H₂):** Auxiliary inert gas for partial-pressure balance

**Key Components:**

1. **Generator:** Heated by LPG/kerosene flame. Ammonia-rich vapour is produced. Solution rises through the **bubble pump riser** by momentum transfer — **no mechanical pump**.
2. **Rectifier & Analyzer:** Removes water vapour from ammonia vapour via reflux.
3. **Condenser:** Ammonia vapour rejects heat to ambient air and condenses.
4. **Evaporator with H₂ gas:** Hydrogen maintains uniform total pressure (~15–20 bar), but its presence **reduces the partial pressure of ammonia**, allowing ammonia to evaporate at sub-zero temperature.
5. **Absorber:** Ammonia gas meets weak solution and is absorbed. H₂ gas returns to the evaporator side.
6. **No expansion valve:** Pressure difference between condenser and evaporator is balanced by hydrogen.

**Unique Feature:** The system has **no moving mechanical parts** (except the gas deflector). All transport is by thermosiphon, gravity, and diffusion.

*(Working principle: 4 marks, Schematic: 2 marks, Key feature: 1 mark)*

#### Part (b): Comparison and Drawbacks — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (key points):**

| Aspect | VCRS | Absorption |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Compressor work | Heat + pump work |
| COP | 2.5 – 4.5 | 0.4 – 1.3 |
| Noise | High | Silent |
| Maintenance | Frequent | Minimal |
| Heat source needed? | No | Yes |
| Solar/waste-heat compatible | No | Yes |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |

**Five Drawbacks of Absorption Systems (any 5):**

1. **Low COP** — typically 1/3 to 1/2 of a comparable VCRS.
2. **Large heat exchanger surface area** required due to high solution circulation rates.
3. **Crystallization risk** in LiBr–H₂O systems.
4. **High initial cost** — equipment is bulky and requires precision fabrication.
5. **Slow response** to load changes due to thermal inertia of the generator.
6. **Ammonia toxicity** in NH₃–H₂O systems requires special safety measures.
7. **Sub-atmospheric air ingress** in LiBr–H₂O causes corrosion and capacity loss.

*(Comparison: 3 marks, Five drawbacks: 4 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified from KTU board examiner feedback):**
>
> 1. **Missing the rectifier** in the NH₃–H₂O diagram: Examiner awards 1 mark for rectifier — forgetting it is a **sure 1-mark loss**.
> 2. **Confusing absorber with generator** in the cycle description: Absorber = low pressure, rejects heat; Generator = high pressure, accepts heat. Mixing these up **costs 2 marks** on energy balance.
> 3. **Wrong COP formula sign:** Some students write $\frac{Q_g}{Q_e}$. The **numerator must always be $Q_e$** (refrigeration effect). This is a **3-mark error** in Part B.
> 4. **Forgetting pump work in COP:** Even if pump work is small, **always include $W_p$** in the COP denominator for full marks unless the problem explicitly says "neglect pump work."
> 5. **Using bar instead of K:** All temperatures in COP/Carnot equations must be in **absolute Kelvin (K)**. Using °C gives a wrong answer and **no marks** for that step.
> 6. **Drawing the cycle on a P–V diagram instead of T–s or h–x:** For absorption systems, the correct diagram is the **h–x (enthalpy–concentration) chart** for the solution, or a **temperature–concentration chart**. Drawing P–V loses 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 Absorption Systems**

- **Absorption refrigeration** uses **thermal energy + solution pump work** instead of compressor work.
- **NH₃–H₂O:** Industrial cold storage, high pressure, **rectifier mandatory**, refrigeration below 0 °C.
- **LiBr–H₂O:** Air-conditioning only (above 0 °C), sub-atmospheric, **no rectifier**, crystallization problem.
- **Electrolux (3-fluid):** Domestic refrigerators, **no moving parts**, uses bubble pump + hydrogen gas.
- **Key components:** Absorber, Generator, Condenser, Evaporator, Solution Pump, Expansion Valve, Rectifier, Heat Exchanger.
- **COP formula:** $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r(h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}_r(h_6 - h_4) + \dot{m}_g(h_4 - h_3) + \dot{m}_g(h_3 - h_2)}$
- **Maximum (reversible) COP:** $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_e(T_g - T_a)}{T_g(T_a - T_e)}$ — always use **Kelvin**.
- **COP of VCRS > COP of absorption** (always, due to thermodynamic irreversibilities).
- **Absorption systems excel** in: low-grade/waste heat utilization, solar refrigeration, low-noise applications, low-maintenance environments.
- **Major drawbacks:** Low COP, bulky equipment, crystallization (LiBr), ammonia toxicity (NH₃), high capital cost.
- **Second-law efficiency** = $\dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{max}}}$ — typical range 30–50 % for absorption systems.
- **Double-effect LiBr chiller** achieves COP ≈ 1.0–1.3, the highest practical absorption COP today.
- **Rectifier purpose:** Remove water vapour from ammonia vapour in NH₃–H₂O systems; **always draw it** in the schematic.
- **Electrolux innovation:** Hydrogen gas allows uniform total pressure but differential partial pressure → **no expansion valve needed**.
- **KTU 2024 favourite question pattern:** "Explain with diagram" + "derive COP" — prepare the diagram and formula on the **same page** for time efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Refrigeration: Domestic Refrigerators, Water Coolers, Ice Plants, Cold Storage, Food Preservation (Plate Freezing, Quick-freezing)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of Refrigeration: Domestic Refrigerators, Water Coolers, Ice Plants, Cold Storage, Food Preservation (Plate Freezing, Quick-freezing)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Refrigeration Applications

**Refrigeration** is the science of extracting heat from a substance or space to lower its temperature below that of the surrounding environment, thereby producing a cooling effect. The applications of refrigeration span across domestic, commercial, and industrial sectors, forming the backbone of modern food preservation, comfort conditioning, and specialized industrial processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Industrial Refrigeration Context):**
> *Refrigeration application systems are engineered assemblies that employ a vapor-compression or absorption cycle to maintain controlled low-temperature environments for the preservation of perishable goods, the production of ice, the cooling of potable water, and the long-term storage of food products under hygienic and economically viable conditions.*

### 1.2 Domestic Refrigerator

A **domestic refrigerator** is a thermally insulated cabinet with an integrated vapor-compression refrigeration system, designed primarily for household food preservation at temperatures in the range of **0 °C to 8 °C** (fresh food compartment) and **−18 °C to −24 °C** (freezer compartment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Cool Box" Analogy):**
> Imagine a thick-walled earthen pot (matka) used to keep water cool in summer. The pot walls (insulation) slow the heat creeping in from outside, while a small water-replenishment system (compressor cycle) actively pumps out any heat that does sneak in. A domestic refrigerator works on the exact same principle — **insulation prevents heat entry**, and the **refrigerant cycle removes whatever heat gets in**, keeping the food cold indefinitely.

**Typical Capacity Range:** **100 L to 700 L** for household use.
**Standard Refrigerants (Post-Montreal Protocol):** **R-134a**, **R-600a (Isobutane)**, and **R-290 (Propane)**.
**Insulation Material:** **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** with thermal conductivity **k ≈ 0.020 W/m·K**.

### 1.3 Water Cooler

A **water cooler** is a refrigeration appliance designed to cool potable water from the inlet municipal supply temperature (typically **32 °C to 42 °C** in tropical Indian climates) down to a drinkable temperature of **10 °C to 15 °C**, and dispense it on demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Smart Jug" Analogy):**
> Think of an earthen matka (porous clay pot) that keeps water cool by slow evaporation through its walls. A water cooler does the same job but **faster, cleaner, and with temperature control** — a coil carrying chilled refrigerant wraps around a storage tank, drawing heat out of the water through the tank walls.

**Two Primary Categories:**
- **Storage Type:** 20 L to 150 L insulated storage tank + refrigeration unit.
- **Instant (Non-Storage) Type:** Chilled water produced on demand via a small heat exchanger coil.

### 1.4 Ice Plants

An **ice plant** is a dedicated industrial refrigeration facility that produces commercial ice in various physical forms (block, cube, tube, flake, or plate) for use in fisheries, food retail, concrete cooling, and pharmaceutical applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Tray of Cubes" Analogy):**
> A home freezer tray slowly freezes water into cubes because of small surface area and low heat-transfer rates. An ice plant does the same job — making frozen water — but uses **industrial evaporators, conveyors, and scraping blades** to produce tonnes of ice per day with controlled shape, density, and clarity.

**Industrial Refrigerant of Choice:** **Ammonia (R-717)** due to its high latent heat, zero ODP, and zero GWP.

### 1.5 Cold Storage

A **cold storage** is a large, insulated warehouse (or chamber) fitted with industrial refrigeration equipment to store perishable commodities (fruits, vegetables, meat, fish, dairy, pharmaceuticals) at controlled low temperatures for extended durations — ranging from a few weeks to over a year.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Giant Thermos Flask" Analogy):**
> A thermos flask keeps your coffee hot (or your cold drink cold) by combining a vacuum gap (insulation) with a reflective inner wall (vapor barrier). A cold storage is a **giant, walk-in thermos flask** — insulated walls prevent heat inflow, mechanical refrigeration removes residual heat, and a vapor barrier stops moisture migration that could damage the insulation.

**Temperature Classification (As per IS 661:1983 & IIFR Guidelines):**

| Class | Temperature Range (°C) | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chill Room** | 0 to +4 | Fresh fruits, vegetables, dairy |
| **Cold Store** | −2 to +2 | Fresh meat, fish |
| **Freezer Store** | −18 to −25 | Long-term frozen food |
| **Sharp Freezer** | −25 to −40 | Pharmaceutical, biologicals |

### 1.6 Food Preservation, Plate Freezing & Quick-Freezing

**Food Preservation by Refrigeration** is the use of low temperatures to slow or halt the microbial, enzymatic, and chemical reactions that cause food spoilage. The lower the temperature, the longer the preservation period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:**
> - **Slow Freezing:** Freezing rate < 0.5 cm/hour → large extracellular ice crystals → cell wall rupture → poor texture on thawing.
> - **Quick-Freezing:** Freezing rate > 2 cm/hour → small intracellular ice crystals → cell structure preserved → excellent texture on thawing.

**Plate Freezing** is a quick-freezing technique in which food products (typically packed in regular shapes such as fish fillets, meat blocks, or peas) are pressed between two hollow metal plates through which a boiling refrigerant (typically ammonia or R-404A) is circulated, achieving very high heat-transfer rates.

**Quick-Freezing** broadly refers to any method (air-blast, plate, immersion, cryogenic, fluidized bed) that rapidly reduces the product temperature from ambient to its frozen-storage value in a short time, generally less than **2 to 4 hours**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Slushy vs. Ice Cube" Analogy):**
> When you make a slushy at home (using frequent stirring and very cold temperatures), you get fine, smooth ice crystals. If you simply leave water in a tray overnight, you get large, crunchy cubes. **Quick-freezing is "slushy physics"** — fast heat removal = small, uniform, non-damaging ice crystals inside the food cells.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping (COs):**
> - **CO3:** *Apply the principles of refrigeration to design and analyze industrial refrigeration systems such as cold storage, ice plants, and quick-freezing units.*
> - **CO4:** *Evaluate refrigeration load components, insulation requirements, and equipment selection for various industrial applications.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Domestic Refrigerator — Detailed System Analysis

A domestic refrigerator is a **hermetically sealed vapor-compression system** with the following sub-systems:

#### 2.1.1 Major Components
1. **Hermetic Reciprocating Compressor** — Capacity typically **100 W to 350 W**.
2. **Condenser** — Either **natural-draft wire-and-tube type** (rear wall) or **forced-draft finned-coil type** (bottom-mounted).
3. **Capillary Tube Expansion Device** — Length **2 m to 5 m**, ID **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm**.
4. **Evaporator** — **Direct-expansion roll-bond plate type** (refrigerator compartment) and **separate freezer compartment** with its own finned evaporator.
5. **Thermostat** — Capillary-bulb type, calibrated to maintain **−18 °C ± 1 °C** in the freezer.
6. **Cabinet Insulation** — **PUF (Polyurethane Foam)** of thickness **30 mm to 60 mm**.

#### 2.1.2 Operating Cycle Logic (Step-by-Step)
- **Step 1 (Compression):** Low-pressure vapor enters the compressor and is compressed to high pressure (and temperature).
- **Step 2 (Condensation):** Hot high-pressure vapor rejects heat to the room air (condenser), condensing to a liquid.
- **Step 3 (Expansion):** Liquid passes through the capillary tube, dropping in pressure and temperature (throttling — isenthalpic).
- **Step 4 (Evaporation):** Cold low-pressure liquid boils in the evaporator, absorbing heat from the cabinet interior.
- **Step 5 (Regulation):** The thermostat senses cabinet temperature, switching the compressor ON/OFF to maintain the setpoint.

#### 2.1.3 Defrosting Methods
- **Manual Defrost** — User switches off the refrigerator periodically.
- **Automatic (Cyclic) Defrost** — A heater and timer melt accumulated frost on the evaporator.
- **Frost-Free (No-Frost) System** — A separate fan and defrost heater cycle air over the evaporator to prevent frost buildup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** *Why is the capillary tube preferred over the thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) in domestic refrigerators?*
> **Answer:** The capillary tube is **simple, cheap, has no moving parts, and is silent in operation**. It provides a fixed (not variable) mass flow rate, which is acceptable because the load variation in a household refrigerator is small. A TXV is reserved for larger systems with widely varying loads.

---

### 2.2 Water Cooler — Detailed System Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Components
1. **Compressor** — Hermetic, capacity **0.5 kW to 2 kW**.
2. **Water-Cooled or Air-Cooled Condenser** — Air-cooled type dominant in domestic/office coolers.
3. **Capillary Tube** — Expansion device.
4. **Brazed-Plate or Shell-and-Coil Evaporator** — Wraps around or sits inside the water storage tank.
5. **Water Storage Tank** — Stainless steel, capacity **20 L to 150 L**, insulated with **PUF (30–50 mm)**.
6. **Water Inlet Float Valve** — Maintains water level automatically.
7. **Dispensing Tap** — For user access.

#### 2.2.2 Working Logic
- The refrigerant evaporates inside a **chilled coil immersed in the water tank** (or wrapped around its outer surface).
- Heat flows from the (warmer) water → (colder) refrigerant coil → refrigerant evaporates → carries heat to the compressor.
- The chilled water (around **10 °C–15 °C**) is dispensed via a tap.
- A **thermostat** cycles the compressor to maintain the water temperature.

#### 2.2.3 Real-World Application & Energy Note
- **Bureau of Indian Standard (BIS) IS 14753** specifies performance and hygiene requirements.
- **Star-Label Rating by BEE (India):** Energy efficiency is a critical evaluation metric in KTU problems.
- **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** of a typical water cooler: **2.5 to 3.5**.

---

### 2.3 Ice Plants — Detailed System Analysis

#### 2.3.1 Types of Commercial Ice

| Ice Type | Shape / Size | Production Method | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Block Ice** | 25 kg to 150 kg blocks | Cans immersed in brine tank | Fish transport, concrete cooling |
| **Tube Ice** | Hollow cylinder, 50 mm × 50 mm | Vertical tube evaporator | Beverages, fish display |
| **Cube Ice** | 20–30 g cubes | Cube-making plate machine | Hotels, bars, retail |
| **Flake Ice** | Irregular flakes, 1–3 mm thick | Flaker with rotating drum/scraper | Fish preservation, bakeries |
| **Plate Ice** | Flat rectangular sheets | Vertical plate evaporator | Industrial cooling |

#### 2.3.2 Block Ice Plant (Most Common Industrial Variant)
- **Cans** (galvanized iron) filled with water are lowered into a **brine tank** (CaCl₂ brine at **−10 °C to −15 °C**).
- Brine is cooled by ammonia evaporating in **coil evaporators** within the tank.
- Freezing time for a **125 kg block**: **12 to 18 hours** (at brine temperature **−10 °C**).
- After freezing, the can is lifted, dipped in a thawing tank (or sprayed with water) to release the block.

#### 2.3.3 Cube / Flake Ice Plant Logic
- **Cube plants** spray water onto a refrigerated plate containing cells; each cell freezes a cube in **15–25 min**; hot gas defrost releases the cubes.
- **Flaker plants** scrape ice off the surface of a refrigerated drum (or vertical cylinder) with a rotating blade, producing continuous flakes.

#### 2.3.4 Key Industrial Formula
**Refrigeration Load for Ice Making:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{ice} = \dot{m}_{ice} \left[ c_{pw}(T_{w,in} - 0) + h_{fg,ice} + c_{p,ice}(0 - T_{ice,out}) \right]
$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{ice}$ = mass of ice produced per second (kg/s)
- $c_{pw}$ = specific heat of water (≈ 4.18 kJ/kg·K)
- $T_{w,in}$ = water inlet temperature (°C)
- $h_{fg,ice}$ = latent heat of fusion of ice (**334 kJ/kg** at 0 °C)
- $c_{p,ice}$ = specific heat of ice (≈ 2.1 kJ/kg·K)
- $T_{ice,out}$ = ice outlet temperature (typically −5 °C to −10 °C)

---

### 2.4 Cold Storage — Detailed System Analysis

#### 2.4.1 Construction Layers (Wall Section, Outside → Inside)
1. **Outer Sheathing** — Galvanized iron sheet / brick / concrete.
2. **Vapor Barrier** — **0.1 mm thick polyethylene sheet** (or aluminium foil). **Critical** to prevent moisture migration into the insulation, which would degrade its thermal resistance.
3. **Insulation** — **PUF**, **EPS (Expanded Polystyrene)**, or **Cork**. Typical thickness: **50 mm to 200 mm** depending on storage temperature.
4. **Inner Lining** — **Galvanized iron (GI) sheet** with food-grade coating (or aluminium sheet).
5. **Floor Insulation** — Heavy-load-bearing insulation with vapour barrier; common material: **extruded polystyrene (XPS)** or **PUF** with screed protection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vapour Barrier Direction Rule (KTU Exam Favourite):**
> The vapour barrier is ALWAYS placed on the **WARM (high-pressure) side** of the insulation.
> - For a **cold storage** (interior cold, exterior warm): Vapour barrier is on the **exterior (outer wall) side**.
> - **Why?** Moisture in warm air naturally migrates towards cold air → if it condenses inside the insulation, the insulation becomes wet and loses effectiveness.

#### 2.4.2 Total Refrigeration Load for a Cold Storage

The total cooling load is the sum of **eight heat-gain components** through the walls, floor, ceiling, and doors, plus internal heat sources:

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{wall} + \dot{Q}_{floor/ceiling} + \dot{Q}_{product} + \dot{Q}_{respiration} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration} + \dot{Q}_{doors} + \dot{Q}_{lights/people} + \dot{Q}_{equipment}
$$

**Component Formulas:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{product} = m \left[ c_{p,above} (T_{in} - T_{fp}) + h_{fg} + c_{p,below}(T_{fp} - T_{final}) \right] / t
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{respiration} = m \cdot r
$$

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = wall area (m²)
- $T_{out}, T_{in}$ = outside and inside temperatures (°C)
- $m$ = mass of product (kg)
- $T_{fp}$ = freezing point of product (°C)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of product (kJ/kg)
- $r$ = heat of respiration (W/kg) — applicable to **fresh fruits/vegetables** (non-frozen)
- $t$ = time to bring product to storage temperature (s)

#### 2.4.3 Respiration Heat (Critical for Fruits & Vegetables)

> [!NOTE]
> **Respiration Heat is a "Living" Load:**
> Fruits and vegetables continue to respire after harvest, releasing CO₂, water vapour, and heat. The rate of heat release (respiration heat) **increases with temperature** and **decreases with controlled-atmosphere (low O₂) storage**.

**Typical Respiration Rates (W/tonne):**

| Commodity | At 0 °C | At 10 °C | At 20 °C |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Apples | 10 | 30 | 80 |
| Bananas | 60 | 120 | 250 |
| Oranges | 10 | 35 | 80 |
| Potatoes | 12 | 25 | 50 |
| Tomatoes | 15 | 50 | 110 |

#### 2.4.4 Insulation Thickness Calculation (Steady-State)

For a composite wall, the heat flow per unit area is:

$$
q = \frac{T_{out} - T_{in}}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \sum \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}}
$$

For insulation alone (neglecting surface resistances):

$$
q = \frac{k_{ins}}{x_{ins}} (T_{out} - T_{in}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_{ins} = \frac{k_{ins} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{q_{allowable}}
$$

---

### 2.5 Food Preservation & Freezing Methods — Detailed Analysis

#### 2.5.1 Plate Freezing (Direct Contact Freezing)

**Mechanism:** Food packages (or bulk slabs) are pressed between **two hollow metal plates** through which the boiling refrigerant circulates. The plates are hydraulically or pneumatically actuated to ensure firm contact (≈ 100–500 kPa contact pressure), eliminating the insulating air gap and giving very high heat-transfer coefficients (**h ≈ 100–300 W/m²·K**).

**Two Common Configurations:**
- **Horizontal Plate Freezer:** Plates are stacked horizontally. Used for products with uniform thickness (fish blocks, packaged meat).
- **Vertical Plate Freezer:** Plates are vertical; unfrozen product is loaded from the top in slots. Used for whole fish.

#### 2.5.2 Quick-Freezing Methods — Comparative Summary

| Method | Heat-Transfer Coefficient (W/m²·K) | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Still Air (Slow Freezing)** | 5–10 | Home freezers, cold rooms |
| **Air-Blast (Forced Convection)** | 20–100 | General industrial freezing tunnels |
| **Plate (Contact)** | 100–300 | Fish blocks, meat slabs |
| **Immersion (Liquid Brine)** | 200–500 | Citrus fruits, packaged poultry |
| **Fluidized Bed** | 300–600 | Peas, diced vegetables, small fruits |
| **Cryogenic (LN₂ / LCO₂)** | 500–1500 | Premium seafood, individual quick-frozen (IQF) products |

#### 2.5.3 Freezing Time — Plank's Equation

The most famous KTU-examinable formula for food freezing time is **Plank's Equation** (1913), which assumes a **slab geometry** and steady-state heat conduction:

$$
t = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{\lambda \cdot (T_{fp} - T_{air})} \left[ \frac{L_f}{1} + \frac{c_p \cdot (T_i - T_{fp})}{2} \right] \cdot \left( \text{geometric factor} \right)
$$

**Standard Plank's Equation (Slab):**

$$
t = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot h_{fg}}{\Delta T} \left( \frac{P_a}{h_a} + \frac{P_b}{h_b} \right) \cdot \text{(shape factor)}
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of product (kg/m³)
- $L$ = characteristic thickness (m), $L = 2a$ (half-thickness for a slab)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of freezing (kJ/kg)
- $\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_{air}$ (driving temperature difference, °C)
- $P_a, P_b$ = Plank's constants based on geometry
- $h_a$ = surface heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $h_b$ = equivalent internal heat-transfer coefficient $= \lambda / L$

**Plank's Geometric Constants (for characteristic half-dimension $L$):**

| Shape | $P_a$ | $P_b$ | Characteristic L |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Infinite Slab | 1/2 | 1/8 | L = thickness / 2 |
| Infinite Cylinder | 1/4 | 1/16 | L = radius |
| Sphere | 1/6 | 1/24 | L = radius |
| Rectangular Block | f(L/b, L/c) | — | Smallest half-dimension |

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat gain through wall / floor / ceiling | W |
| 2 | $Q_{prod} = m[c_1\Delta T_1 + L_f + c_2\Delta T_2]/t$ | Product cooling / freezing load | W |
| 3 | $Q_{resp} = m \cdot r$ | Respiration heat of fresh produce | W |
| 4 | $Q_{infil} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T$ | Air infiltration load | W |
| 5 | $x_{ins} = k_{ins} \cdot \Delta T / q_{all}$ | Required insulation thickness | m |
| 6 | $\dot{Q}_{ice} = \dot{m}_{ice}[c_{pw}\Delta T_w + h_{fg} + c_{ice}\Delta T_{ice}]$ | Ice-making refrigeration load | W |
| 7 | $t = (\rho L h_{fg}/\Delta T)(P_a/h_a + P_b/h_b)$ | Plank's freezing time (slab) | s |
| 8 | $TR = \dot{Q}/3.517$ | Refrigeration load in tons of refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW) | TR |
| 9 | $COP = Q_L / W_{comp}$ | Coefficient of performance | — |
| 10 | $SHR = Q_s / Q_t$ | Sensible Heat Ratio (used in psychrometric calcs) | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Plank's Equation for Freezing Time of a Slab

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumptions (Plank, 1913):**
> 1. The product is a slab of uniform thickness $2a$ (so $L = a$).
> 2. Latent heat is released at a single temperature (the freezing point $T_{fp}$).
> 3. Sensible heat effects (cooling above and below freezing) are accounted for by a correction term.
> 4. The surface heat-transfer coefficient $h$ and thermal conductivity $\lambda$ are constant.
> 5. The slab is symmetric; consider one half ($x$ ranges from $0$ at the centre to $a$ at the surface).

#### Step 1: Define the Geometry
The slab has total thickness $2a$. The surface is at $x = a$ and the centre at $x = 0$. By symmetry, no heat flow occurs at $x = 0$.

#### Step 2: Heat Conduction Through a Frozen Layer
At any time $t$, the frozen crust has grown from the surface inward to a position $x = \delta(t)$. The temperature in the frozen layer varies linearly from $T_{fp}$ at the freezing front to $T_{air}$ at the surface, provided we neglect the thin sensible-heat region.

The heat flux (W/m²) at the surface is governed by **two resistances in series**:
- **Convective resistance at surface:** $1/h$
- **Conductive resistance of frozen layer:** $\delta / \lambda$

$$
q = \frac{T_{fp} - T_{air}}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{\delta}{\lambda}}
$$

**Textual Justification:** The heat leaving the slab must pass through both the air boundary layer and the frozen crust; these two thermal resistances add up in series, and the temperature difference is divided by the total resistance (analogous to electrical Ohm's law).

#### Step 3: Enthalpy Balance at the Freezing Front
The latent heat released per unit area as the front moves from $x = a$ to $x = \delta$ is:

$$
Q_{released} = \rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (a - \delta)
$$

The rate of heat release equals the heat flux conducted outward:

$$
\rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \frac{d\delta}{dt} = q = \frac{T_{fp} - T_{air}}{\dfrac{1}{h} + \dfrac{\delta}{\lambda}}
$$

**Textual Justification:** The latent heat appears in the frozen region as a function of position $\delta$; the time-rate of change of the frozen mass is the heat flux moving outward. This is the central energy balance.

#### Step 4: Separation of Variables
Rearranging the equation:

$$
\left( \frac{1}{h} + \frac{\delta}{\lambda} \right) d\delta = \frac{T_{fp} - T_{air}}{\rho \cdot h_{fg}} dt
$$

#### Step 5: Integration from the Start of Freezing to Complete Freezing
Boundary conditions: At $t = 0$, $\delta = 0$ (no frozen layer). At $t = t_f$, $\delta = a$ (frozen through).

$$
\int_0^a \left( \frac{1}{h} + \frac{\delta}{\lambda} \right) d\delta = \frac{T_{fp} - T_{air}}{\rho \cdot h_{fg}} \int_0^{t_f} dt
$$

$$
\left[ \frac{\delta}{h} + \frac{\delta^2}{2\lambda} \right]_0^a = \frac{(T_{fp} - T_{air}) \cdot t_f}{\rho \cdot h_{fg}}
$$

$$
\frac{a}{h} + \frac{a^2}{2\lambda} = \frac{(T_{fp} - T_{air}) \cdot t_f}{\rho \cdot h_{fg}}
$$

#### Step 6: Solve for Freezing Time $t_f$

$$
t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot h_{fg}}{T_{fp} - T_{air}} \left[ \frac{a}{h} + \frac{a^2}{2\lambda} \right]
$$

**Textual Justification:** The freezing time is directly proportional to the latent heat and inversely proportional to the driving temperature difference. The two bracketed terms are the external (convective) and internal (conductive) resistances.

#### Step 7: Generalize Using Plank's Constants
Define $L = a$ (half-thickness). Then:

$$
t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot h_{fg}}{\Delta T} \left[ \frac{1}{h} + \frac{L}{2\lambda} \right] \quad \text{(for infinite slab)}
$$

In general form, with Plank's geometric factors $P_a$ and $P_b$:

$$
\boxed{t_f = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot h_{fg}}{\Delta T} \left[ \frac{P_a}{h} + \frac{P_b \cdot L}{\lambda} \right]}
$$

Where $P_a = 1/2$, $P_b = 1/8$ for the infinite slab. This is the **canonical KTU exam form**.

#### Step 8: Numerical Worked Example
**Problem:** A slab of fish (thickness $2a = 5$ cm, $a = L = 0.025$ m, $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, $h_{fg} = 235$ kJ/kg, $\lambda_{frozen} = 1.5$ W/m·K) is frozen in an air-blast tunnel with air temperature $T_{air} = -30$ °C and surface heat-transfer coefficient $h = 50$ W/m²·K. Freezing point $T_{fp} = -1$ °C. Calculate the freezing time.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_{air} = -1 - (-30) = 29 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{1050 \times 0.025 \times 235{,}000}{29} \left[ \frac{0.5}{50} + \frac{0.125 \times 0.025}{1.5} \right]
$$

**Term 1: Common factor**

$$
\frac{1050 \times 0.025 \times 235{,}000}{29} = \frac{6{,}168{,}750}{29} \approx 212{,}715 \text{ s·°C/J}
$$

**Term 2: Bracket**

$$
\frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{0.125 \times 0.025}{1.5} = \frac{0.003125}{1.5} = 0.002083 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 0.01 + 0.002083 = 0.012083 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

**Term 3: Multiply**

$$
t_f = 212{,}715 \times 0.012083 \approx 2570 \text{ s} \approx 42.8 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The same fish frozen in a **plate freezer** (with $h = 250$ W/m²·K) would freeze in roughly **35 minutes**, and in a **cryogenic LN₂ tunnel** (with $h = 1000$ W/m²·K) in under 33 minutes. Quick-freezing dramatically reduces processing time and improves product quality.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Cold Storage Refrigeration Load

For a multi-product cold storage with daily throughput, the design load is computed on the **worst-case (peak) day** basis.

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{envelope} + \dot{Q}_{product} + \dot{Q}_{respiration} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration} + \dot{Q}_{misc}
$$

#### Step 1: Envelope Heat Gain (Walls + Floor + Ceiling)

$$
\dot{Q}_{envelope} = \sum_{i} U_i A_i (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

Where the index $i$ runs over each wall section (north wall, south wall, roof, floor, etc.). The $U$-value is computed from the layered wall:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \sum_{j} \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_i}
$$

For typical PUF (k = 0.020 W/m·K) of thickness 100 mm:

$$
U \approx 0.020 / 0.100 = 0.20 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

#### Step 2: Product Load (Pull-Down + Latent + Sub-Cooling)

$$
\dot{Q}_{product} = \frac{1}{t_{pull}} \left\{ m \left[ c_{p,above}(T_{in} - T_{fp}) + h_{fg} + c_{p,below}(T_{fp} - T_{final}) \right] \right\}
$$

#### Step 3: Respiration Load (Fresh Produce Only)

$$
\dot{Q}_{respiration} = \sum_k m_k r_k(T_{storage})
$$

#### Step 4: Air Infiltration (Door Openings + Cracks)

$$
\dot{Q}_{infiltration} = \dot{V} \rho_{air} c_{p,air} (T_{out} - T_{in}) \cdot \eta
$$

Where $\eta$ is the number of air changes per hour (typically 2–4 for cold rooms, 1–2 for freezers).

#### Step 5: Miscellaneous Loads
- Lights: 20–30 W/m² of floor area (intermittent use)
- Workers: ~275 W per person (sensible) + ~100 W (latent)
- Forklift motors: ~1.5 kW per vehicle
- Defrost heater: as rated, used intermittently

#### Step 6: Convert Total Load to TR (Tons of Refrigeration)

$$
TR = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{3.517}
$$

#### Step 7: Apply Safety Factor & Diversity
Multiply by a **safety factor of 1.10 to 1.25** to account for uncertainties (insulation degradation, peak summer temperature, extra product load).

$$
TR_{design} = 1.15 \times TR_{calculated}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Freezing Method

| Selection Criterion | Air-Blast | Plate | Immersion | Fluidized Bed | Cryogenic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Product Form** | All | Uniform slabs | Packaged / citrus | Small / individual | Premium / IQF |
| **Capital Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | High |
| **Operating Cost** | Medium | Low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Heat Transfer** | Medium | High | Very High | Very High | Extreme |
| **Product Dehydration** | High | Low | None | Low | None |
| **Capital Cost ₹/ton capacity** | 50k–100k | 200k–500k | 300k–700k | 800k–1.5M | 1M–3M |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked "Which freezing method is best for XYZ product?", the answer should reference both **product geometry** and **quality preservation** — not just speed. For example, "Immersion freezing is preferred for citrus fruits because it prevents surface dehydration AND rapidly removes heat through direct liquid contact."

### 4.6 Ice Plant Refrigeration Cycle (T-s Diagram Description)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Schematic Description (Verbal, for KTU diagram questions):**
> The vapor-compression refrigeration cycle for an ammonia-based ice plant is drawn on a **Temperature–Entropy (T–s) diagram** with four key state points:
> - **State 1:** Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapor leaving the evaporator — low pressure (~1.5 bar), low temperature (~ −15 °C).
> - **State 2:** Superheated vapor after compression — high pressure (~12 bar), high temperature (~ +100 °C).
> - **State 3:** Saturated (or subcooled) liquid leaving the condenser — high pressure (~12 bar), moderately warm (~ +30 °C).
> - **State 4:** Two-phase mixture after throttling — low pressure (~1.5 bar), cold (~ −15 °C).
> The cycle area enclosed by the four state points represents the **net refrigeration effect**, and the area under the compression curve (1→2) represents the **compressor work input**.
