## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the major components of a domestic refrigerator and state the function of the capillary tube.*

**Model Answer:**

The major components of a domestic refrigerator are:
1. **Hermetically sealed reciprocating compressor** — compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapor to high pressure.
2. **Condenser** (wire-and-tube or finned coil) — rejects heat from the refrigerant to ambient air.
3. **Capillary tube** — expansion device; throttles high-pressure liquid refrigerant to low pressure, causing temperature drop.
4. **Evaporator** (roll-bond plate type) — absorbs heat from the cabinet interior as the refrigerant evaporates.
5. **Thermostat** (capillary bulb type) — senses cabinet temperature and cycles the compressor ON/OFF.
6. **Cabinet insulation** — Polyurethane Foam (PUF) reduces heat ingress from outside.

**Function of the Capillary Tube:** It is a small-bore (0.5–1.0 mm ID) copper tube of length 2–5 m that acts as a **fixed-expansion (throttling) device**. By virtue of its small cross-section and long length, it creates a large frictional pressure drop between the high-pressure liquid (downstream of condenser) and the low-pressure evaporator inlet. This pressure drop causes partial vaporization (flash gas) and a corresponding temperature drop, enabling the refrigerant to absorb heat in the evaporator.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the role of the **capillary tube with that of a thermostatic expansion valve (TXV)**. The capillary tube provides a **fixed mass flow rate** dependent on pressure differential, while a TXV modulates flow based on superheat at the evaporator outlet. Mention this distinction to score the full 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define quick-freezing. Mention any two advantages of quick-freezing over slow-freezing for food preservation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Quick-freezing is the process of rapidly reducing the temperature of a food product from above its freezing point to its final frozen-storage temperature (−18 °C or below) in a short duration, typically achieving a freezing rate greater than **2 cm/hour** through the zone of maximum ice-crystal formation (0 °C to −5 °C).

**Two Advantages of Quick-Freezing over Slow-Freezing:**

1. **Formation of Small Intracellular Ice Crystals:** Rapid freezing causes ice to form both inside and outside the cells simultaneously, producing small, uniform crystals that **do not rupture the cell walls**. On thawing, the food retains its original texture, moisture, and nutritional value. In contrast, slow freezing forms large extracellular crystals that puncture cell walls, causing drip loss (water leakage) and a soggy, deteriorated texture.

2. **Reduced Microbial and Enzymatic Activity:** Quick-freezing passes through the critical spoilage temperature zone (−1 °C to −5 °C, where microbial activity is maximum) very rapidly, thereby **minimizing the time during which bacteria, yeasts, and moulds can multiply**, and reducing the activity of spoilage enzymes. This results in a longer shelf life and better food safety.

3. **(Optional third point)** **Reduced Product Dehydration (Sublimation Loss):** Fast freezing locks surface moisture as ice before significant sublimation can occur, reducing weight loss and surface "freezer burn."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write only "**freezing is faster**" — this is a *definition*, not an *advantage*. You must explain **WHY** speed matters in terms of cell biology, microbiology, and food quality. That is where the marks lie.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **domestic refrigerator**. List the commonly used refrigerants for domestic refrigerators in compliance with the Montreal Protocol. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A domestic refrigerator has a food load of 15 kg to be cooled from 25 °C to 5 °C. The specific heat of food is 3.5 kJ/kg·K. The cabinet heat gain is 25 W, and the compressor runs for 12 minutes per hour. The refrigerator's overall COP is 1.8. Calculate:*
   *(i) the refrigeration load (kW),*
   *(ii) the compressor power input (kW),*
   *(iii) the daily energy consumption (kWh).* (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Domestic Refrigerator (7 Marks)

**Construction:**

A domestic refrigerator is a **thermally insulated cabinet** with the following key components (also see Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Cabinet:** Double-walled, with **30–60 mm PUF insulation** sandwiched between an outer painted-steel body and an inner food-grade ABS or GI lining. The cabinet has a **single door** (or double door in frost-free models) with a magnetic gasket seal.

2. **Compressor:** A **hermetically sealed reciprocating compressor** (typically 100–350 W), mounted at the bottom rear of the cabinet. It is driven by a single-phase induction motor.

3. **Condenser:** Either a **natural-draft wire-and-tube condenser** (mounted on the rear wall) or a **forced-draft finned-coil condenser** (bottom-mounted, fan-assisted).

4. **Capillary Tube:** A coiled copper tube of length 2–5 m and ID 0.5–1.0 mm, acting as the expansion device.

5. **Evaporator:** A **direct-expansion roll-bond plate evaporator** in the freezer compartment, often with an additional finned evaporator for the fresh food section in frost-free models.

6. **Thermostat:** A **capillary-bulb type thermostat** mounted on the evaporator surface, with a control knob accessible to the user.

7. **Defrost System:** In frost-free models, a **defrost heater + timer + thermal fuse** combination periodically melts accumulated frost.

**Working Principle:**

The refrigerant cycle proceeds as follows:
- Low-pressure vapor from the evaporator is **sucked into the compressor** (1 → 2), compressed to a high pressure, and discharged as a hot, high-pressure vapor.
- This vapor enters the **condenser**, where it rejects heat to the surrounding ambient air and **condenses to a liquid** (2 → 3).
- The liquid passes through a **drier-filter** (to remove moisture) and then through the **capillary tube**, where it undergoes **isenthalpic throttling** — its pressure and temperature drop sharply (3 → 4).
- The cold two-phase mixture enters the **evaporator**, absorbs heat from the cabinet interior as it evaporates, and the cycle repeats (4 → 1).
- The **thermostat** senses the cabinet temperature and cycles the compressor ON when the temperature rises above the setpoint, OFF when it reaches the setpoint.

**Montreal-Protocol-Compliant Refrigerants for Domestic Use:**
- **R-134a (HFC)** — the most widely used post-CFC refrigerant (zero ODP, GWP = 1430).
- **R-600a (Isobutane, HC)** — used in modern energy-efficient refrigerators (zero ODP, GWP = 3).
- **R-290 (Propane, HC)** — emerging as a low-GWP alternative (zero ODP, GWP = 3.3).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Food mass $m = 15$ kg
- Initial temperature $T_i = 25$ °C
- Final temperature $T_f = 5$ °C
- Specific heat of food $c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K
- Cabinet heat gain (steady) $Q_{cabinet} = 25$ W
- Compressor ON-time = 12 min/hr (duty cycle = 12/60 = 0.20)
- COP = 1.8

**Step 1: Calculate the Product Cooling Load (Pull-Down)**

We assume the food reaches the setpoint in **1 hour** for the purpose of this problem.

$$
Q_{product} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_f)}{t}
$$

$$
Q_{product} = \frac{15 \times 3.5 \times (25 - 5)}{3600}
$$

$$
Q_{product} = \frac{15 \times 3.5 \times 20}{3600} = \frac{1050}{3600} = 0.2917 \text{ kW} = 291.7 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Refrigeration Load**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_{product} + Q_{cabinet} = 291.7 + 25 = 316.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.317 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Compressor Power Input**

Using $COP = Q_L / W_{comp}$:

$$
W_{comp} = \frac{Q_{total}}{COP} = \frac{0.317}{1.8} = 0.176 \text{ kW} = 176 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Daily Energy Consumption**

The compressor runs for 12 minutes per hour, i.e., 0.20 hours per hour, i.e., **4.8 hours per day**.

$$
E_{day} = W_{comp} \times t_{running} = 0.176 \times 4.8 = 0.845 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Summary of Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Refrigeration load | **0.317 kW (316.7 W)** |
| Compressor power input | **0.176 kW (176 W)** |
| Daily energy consumption | **0.845 kWh/day** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistake:** The product load (1050 kJ) divided by 3600 seconds gives **0.2917 kW**, NOT 0.2917 kJ. Always carry the seconds-to-hours conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Duty cycle confusion:** Students often forget the "12 min/hr" condition and treat the compressor as running 24 hours. This **quadruples** the energy consumption — wrong by 300%. The 12 min/hr condition gives **4.8 hours/day**, not 24.
> 3. **Sign convention:** $T_i - T_f$ must be **positive** (cooling means temperature drops). If you get a negative value, you have the subtraction reversed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**

**(a)** *Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **plate freezer** for fish preservation. State its advantages over an air-blast freezer. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Calculate the time required to freeze a slab of meat (thickness = 8 cm, half-thickness $L = 0.04$ m) using Plank's equation. Given: density $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³, latent heat of freezing $h_{fg} = 240$ kJ/kg, thermal conductivity of frozen meat $\lambda = 1.4$ W/m·K, surface heat-transfer coefficient $h = 30$ W/m²·K, freezing point of meat $T_{fp} = -1.5$ °C, ambient air temperature $T_{air} = -25$ °C.* (7 marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Plate Freezer Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (refer Section 4.4 diagram):**

A plate freezer consists of an array of **hollow, rectangular metal plates** (usually aluminium or stainless steel) arranged in a vertical or horizontal stack. The internal passages of these plates carry the **boiling refrigerant** (commonly ammonia, R-404A, or R-507). Each plate is fitted with **hydraulic or pneumatic rams** that move them to clamp the food product firmly between adjacent plates.

**Key Constructional Features:**
- **Plate material:** Aluminium (high thermal conductivity ≈ 205 W/m·K).
- **Internal channels:** Serpentine tubing or hollow cavity inside each plate.
- **Refrigerant inlet/outlet headers:** Distribute refrigerant to all plates in parallel.
- **Insulation:** The space behind/around the plates is insulated to minimize heat gain.
- **Pressure system:** Hydraulic ram provides 100–500 kPa clamping pressure to eliminate the air gap between the product and the plate surface.
- **Defrost system:** Hot refrigerant gas is circulated through the plates to release the frozen blocks for unloading.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Loading:** The unfrozen food product (e.g., fish fillets, meat blocks, packaged vegetables) is placed in the spaces between the plates.
2. **Clamping:** The hydraulic ram moves the plates together, applying uniform pressure to the product to ensure **intimate thermal contact** (no insulating air gap).
3. **Refrigeration:** Boiling refrigerant at **−35 °C to −40 °C** circulates inside the plates. Heat flows rapidly from the food (warmer) through the high-conductivity aluminium plate (cold), giving a very high surface heat-transfer coefficient (100–300 W/m²·K).
4. **Quick Freezing:** The product freezes rapidly (typically 1–3 hours depending on thickness) from both sides simultaneously, forming small, uniform, intracellular ice crystals.
5. **Defrost & Unloading:** Once frozen, hot refrigerant gas is briefly circulated to loosen the product from the plates; the product is then removed and transferred to frozen storage.

**Advantages of Plate Freezer over Air-Blast Freezer:**

1. **Higher heat-transfer rate (3–5 times faster freezing)** due to direct contact and high surface conductance — resulting in **smaller ice crystals** and **better product quality**.
2. **Lower product dehydration / weight loss** — there is no cold, dry air blowing over the product surface, so sublimation losses are minimal (typically 0.5–1% versus 2–5% in air-blast).
3. **Lower energy consumption per kg of frozen product** — no need to circulate large volumes of air and no fan power consumption.
4. **Compact footprint** — the heat-transfer rate is high, so the freezing time is short and the equipment size is reduced.
5. **More uniform freezing** — the temperature gradient is symmetric from both surfaces of the slab.

**Disadvantages:** Higher capital cost; restricted to products of regular geometry (slabs/blocks); risk of product crushing if clamping pressure is too high.

---

#### Part (b) — Plank's Equation Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Half-thickness $L = 0.04$ m (since total thickness = 8 cm)
- $\rho = 1050$ kg/m³
- $h_{fg} = 240$ kJ/kg $= 240{,}000$ J/kg
- $\lambda = 1.4$ W/m·K
- $h = 30$ W/m²·K
- $T_{fp} = -1.5$ °C
- $T_{air} = -25$ °C

**Step 1: Calculate the Driving Temperature Difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_{fp} - T_{air} = -1.5 - (-25) = 23.5 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2: Identify Plank's Constants for an Infinite Slab**

For an infinite slab (geometry factor 1):
- $P_a = 0.5$
- $P_b = 0.125$

**Step 3: Apply Plank's Equation**

$$
t = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot h_{fg}}{\Delta T} \left[ \frac{P_a}{h} + \frac{P_b \cdot L}{\lambda} \right]
$$

**Step 4: Compute the First Factor**

$$
\frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot h_{fg}}{\Delta T} = \frac{1050 \times 0.04 \times 240{,}000}{23.5}
$$

$$
= \frac{10{,}080{,}000}{23.5} = 428{,}936.17 \text{ s·°C/J} \approx 4.289 \times 10^5
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Bracket**

**Term 1: External (convective) resistance**

$$
\frac{P_a}{h} = \frac{0.5}{30} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

**Term 2: Internal (conductive) resistance**

$$
\frac{P_b \cdot L}{\lambda} = \frac{0.125 \times 0.04}{1.4} = \frac{0.005}{1.4} = 0.003571 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

**Sum:**

$$
0.01667 + 0.003571 = 0.02024 \text{ m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

**Step 6: Multiply**

$$
t = 4.289 \times 10^5 \times 0.02024
$$

$$
t = 8680.7 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 7: Convert to Hours and Minutes**

$$
t = \frac{8680.7}{60} = 144.68 \text{ minutes} \approx 2 \text{ hours } 25 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Final Answer:** **$t \approx 8681$ seconds ≈ 2.41 hours**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Plank's Equation):**
> 1. **Half-thickness trap:** Students often use the FULL thickness (8 cm) instead of the HALF-thickness (4 cm = 0.04 m). This makes the answer 4× too large — an instant 3-mark penalty.
> 2. **Unit of $h_{fg}$:** Must be in J/kg (not kJ/kg) when using SI base units for $\rho$ (kg/m³), $L$ (m), and $\lambda$ (W/m·K). Convert 240 kJ/kg → 240,000 J/kg.
> 3. **Plank's constants memorization:** A common error is using $P_a = 1/2$ and $P_b = 1/8$ for a cylinder — those values are for the **infinite slab** only. For a cylinder, use $P_a = 1/4, P_b = 1/16$. For a sphere, $P_a = 1/6, P_b = 1/24$. Memorize this table.
> 4. **$\Delta T$ sign:** The driving temperature difference is always **POSITIVE** — defined as $T_{fp} - T_{air}$. Since $T_{fp} > T_{air}$ (freezing point is higher than air temperature), the difference is positive. Do not enter negative values.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultra-Compact Recap — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

**✔ Domestic Refrigerator:**
- Components: Hermetic compressor, condenser, capillary tube, evaporator, thermostat, PUF insulation.
- Refrigerants: R-134a, R-600a, R-290 (Montreal-Protocol compliant).
- Why capillary tube? Cheap, simple, silent, fixed flow rate — adequate for small household loads.
- Defrost types: Manual, automatic cyclic, frost-free (no-frost).

**✔ Water Cooler:**
- Storage type (with insulated tank) and instant type (no storage).
- Chilled water temperature: 10–15 °C; inlet water: 32–42 °C (tropical climate).
- Components: Hermetic compressor, air-cooled condenser, capillary tube, evaporator coil, storage tank, float valve, tap.
- Typical COP: 2.5–3.5.

**✔ Ice Plants:**
- Industrial refrigerant: **Ammonia (R-717)**.
- Ice types: Block (125 kg), cube (20–30 g), tube, flake, plate.
- Brine temperature: −10 to −15 °C; freezing time per 125 kg block: 12–18 hours.
- Formula: $\dot{Q}_{ice} = \dot{m}_{ice}[c_{pw}\Delta T_w + h_{fg,ice} + c_{ice}\Delta T_{ice}]$
- $h_{fg,ice} = 334$ kJ/kg at 0 °C.

**✔ Cold Storage:**
- Vapour barrier on **WARM side** (exterior for a cold store).
- Insulation: PUF (k = 0.020 W/m·K), thickness 50–200 mm.
- Total load = envelope + product + respiration + infiltration + miscellaneous.
- Respiration heat is **exclusive to fresh produce** — NOT applicable to frozen meat/fish.
- Design TR = 1.15 × calculated TR (safety factor).

**✔ Food Preservation & Freezing:**
- Slow freezing → large extracellular crystals → cell damage.
- Quick freezing (> 2 cm/hr) → small intracellular crystals → preserved texture.
- Plank's equation: $t = (\rho L h_{fg}/\Delta T)(P_a/h + P_b L/\lambda)$.
- Plank's constants — Slab: 1/2, 1/8. Cylinder: 1/4, 1/16. Sphere: 1/6, 1/24.
- Use **half-thickness** for slab (L = total thickness / 2).
- Plate freezing: h = 100–300 W/m²·K. Air-blast: h = 20–100. Cryogenic: h = 500–1500.

**✔ Unit Conversions (MUST memorise):**
- 1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) = 3.517 kW = 200 BTU/min = 3024 kcal/hr.
- 1 kcal = 4.187 kJ.
- $h_{fg}$ of water at 0 °C = 334 kJ/kg.
- $h_{fg}$ of R-134a at 0 °C ≈ 200 kJ/kg.

**✔ Key Acronyms:**
- **TR** = Ton of Refrigeration.
- **COP** = Coefficient of Performance.
- **ODP** = Ozone Depletion Potential.
- **GWP** = Global Warming Potential.
- **PUF** = Polyurethane Foam.
- **IQF** = Individual Quick Frozen.
- **IIFR** = Indian Institute of Food Refrigeration (now IIR India chapter).
- **BEE** = Bureau of Energy Efficiency (India).

**✔ Common 14-Mark Question Templates:**
1. *Draw and explain a domestic refrigerator / water cooler / cold storage.* — Always include: definition, working principle, components, refrigerant, energy efficiency note.
2. *Estimate the refrigeration load for a cold storage of capacity X tonnes holding Y tonnes of product at Z °C.* — Use the 8-component load formula, multiply by safety factor 1.15, convert to TR.
3. *Calculate the freezing time of a slab of meat using Plank's equation.* — Use the worked-example format above, watch out for half-thickness.
4. *Compare air-blast, plate, immersion, fluidized-bed, and cryogenic freezing methods.* — Use the analytical matrix from Section 3.3.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigeration System Components: Compressors, Condensers, Expansion Devices, Evaporators, Cooling Towers (Types and Applications), Refrigerant Leakage Detection, Refrigerant Charging, and System Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Refrigeration System Components: Compressors, Condensers, Expansion Devices, Evaporators, Cooling Towers (Types and Applications), Refrigerant Leakage Detection, Refrigerant Charging, and System Controls # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Industrial Refrigeration — The Big Picture > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > An **Industrial Refrigeration System** is an integrated assembly of mechanical, thermal, and control components arranged in a closed thermodynamic loop whose purpose is to extract heat (Q_L) from a process load at a low temperature reservoir (T_L) and reject that heat (Q_H) to a higher temperature sink (T_H), with the net work input (W_net) supplied by a prime mover (electric motor/engine). The principal components are the **Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Device → Evaporator**, augmented by **Cooling Towers**, **Leak Detection**, **Charging Apparatus**, and **System Controls**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Heart Analogy" Imagine your body as an industrial refrigeration plant: - The **compressor** is your **heart**, pumping "refrigerant blood" around the circuit. - The **condenser** is the **skin surface** that releases heat to the environment when you sweat. - The **expansion device** is the **throttle valve (capillary)** that controls blood flow into a small capillary bed. - The **evaporator** is your **lungs and skin capillaries** that absorb heat during inhalation and cooling. - The **cooling tower** is the **sweat evaporation system** on your skin. - **Leak detection** is your immune system spotting internal bleeding. - **Charging** is the act of drinking water to refill body fluids. - **System controls** are the **autonomic nervous system** (sympathetic/parasympathetic) keeping temperature, pressure, and flow in balance. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Performance Metric — Coefficient of Performance (COP):** > $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ > where temperatures are in **Kelvin (K)**. Industrial systems commonly operate at **COP between 2.0 and 4.5** depending on application. ## 1.3 Sub-Topic Map (Module 3 Coverage) | # | Sub-Topic | KTU Weightage (Typical) | |---|-----------|-------------------------| | 3.1 | Compressors (Types & Applications) | High | | 3.2 | Condensers (Types & Applications) | High | | 3.3 | Expansion Devices | Medium | | 3.4 | Evaporators | High | | 3.5 | Cooling Towers | Medium | | 3.6 | Refrigerant Leakage Detection | Low–Medium | | 3.7 | Refrigerant Charging | Low–Medium | | 3.8 | System Controls | Medium | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Compressors — The Heart of the System ### 2.1.1 Classification of Compressors Compressors are classified into **Positive Displacement** and **Dynamic (Centrifugal)** types. > [!NOTE] > **Positive Displacement Compressors** deliver a fixed volume of refrigerant per cycle, irrespective of discharge pressure (within design limits). > **Dynamic Compressors** deliver flow that varies with the pressure differential. ### 2.1.2 Types & Working Principles **A. Reciprocating Compressors** - **Working**: A piston (driven by a crankshaft) compresses refrigerant inside a cylinder. Suction → Compression → Discharge. - **Speed range**: 300–1800 RPM - **Capacity range**: 0.5 TR to 150 TR - **Industrial Applications**: Cold storage, dairy chilling, pharma, ice plants. **B. Screw (Rotary Helical) Compressors** - **Working**: Two intermeshing helical rotors (male and female) trap and compress refrigerant. Continuous suction, no reciprocating parts. - **Speed range**: 3000–3600 RPM - **Capacity range**: 20 TR to 1500 TR - **Industrial Applications**: Large cold storage, process cooling, marine refrigeration, ammonia plants. - **Capacity control**: Slide valve (10% to 100%) — high efficiency part-load performance. **C. Centrifugal Compressors** - **Working**: A high-speed impeller imparts kinetic energy to refrigerant vapor, converted to pressure in a diffuser. - **Speed range**: 3000–30000 RPM - **Capacity range**: 150 TR to 10000+ TR - **Industrial Applications**: District cooling, large petrochemical plants, LNG pre-cooling. **D. Scroll Compressors** - **Working**: One scroll (spiral) is fixed; the other orbits eccentrically, trapping and compressing refrigerant in crescent-shaped pockets. - **Capacity range**: 1 TR to 60 TR - **Industrial Applications**: Precision cooling, data centers, commercial chillers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Volumetric Efficiency of Reciprocating Compressor:** > $$\eta_v = \frac{V_{\text{actual}}}{V_{\text{swept}}} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$ > where $C$ = clearance ratio, $n$ = polytropic index (≈ 1.25–1.35 for refrigerant vapors). --- ## 2.2 Condensers — Heat Rejectors A condenser rejects latent heat (h_fg) released when high-pressure vapor condenses to liquid. ### 2.2.1 Types of Condensers | Type | Heat Rejection Mode | Typical Application | |------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Shell-and-Tube (Water-Cooled)** | Water inside tubes; refrigerant in shell | Large industrial systems, ammonia plants | | **Shell-and-Coil** | Coils immersed in refrigerant | Small commercial systems | | **Air-Cooled (Finned)** | Forced/draft air over finned coil | Small-to-medium chillers, rooftop units | | **Evaporative Condenser** | Water spray + air draft | Medium-to-large industrial, water-scarce areas | | **Double-Tube (Tube-in-Tube)** | Annular water flow | Small capacity, simple service | ### 2.2.2 Key Performance Equations **Heat Rejection in Condenser:** $$Q_H = \dot{m} \cdot (h_g - h_f) = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$$ > [!NOTE] > **LMTD (Log-Mean Temperature Difference):** > $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ > $\Delta T_1 = T_{\text{cond}} - T_{w,\text{out}}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{\text{cond}} - T_{w,\text{in}}$ --- ## 2.3 Expansion Devices — Throttling Elements The expansion device performs **isenthalpic throttling** ($h_3 = h_4$) to drop pressure from condenser to evaporator pressure. ### 2.3.1 Classification **A. Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV/TXV)** - **Working**: Bulb senses evaporator outlet temperature; modulates refrigerant flow to maintain constant superheat. - **Industrial use**: DX systems, supermarkets, cold rooms. **B. Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** - **Working**: Stepper-motor-driven valve controlled by a microcontroller based on superheat, pressure, or algorithm. - **Industrial use**: Modern chiller plants, precision cooling, variable speed systems. **C. Float Valve (High-Side / Low-Side)** - **Working**: A float controls refrigerant level in a low-side or high-side receiver. - **Industrial use**: Flooded evaporators, ammonia systems. **D. Capillary Tube** - **Working**: Fixed bore, fixed length — passive metering. - **Industrial use**: Small hermetic systems (domestic refrigerators), not recommended for variable-load industrial systems. **E. Automatic Expansion Valve (AEV)** - **Working**: Maintains constant evaporator pressure (not superheat). - **Industrial use**: Limited; mostly refrigeration cabinets. ### 2.3.2 Throttling Equation $$h_3 = h_4$$ $$T_4 < T_3 \text{ (Joule–Thomson effect)}$$ --- ## 2.4 Evaporators — Heat Absorbers The evaporator absorbs latent heat from the load at low pressure/temperature. ### 2.4.1 Types | Type | Description | Industrial Use | |------|-------------|----------------| | **Flooded Shell-and-Tube** | Liquid refrigerant floods shell | Ammonia industrial plants, dairy, brewery | | **DX Shell-and-Tube** | Boils inside tubes | Brine chilling, glycol cooling | | **Plate Heat Exchanger** | Compact brazed plate | Compact chillers, process cooling | | **Fin-and-Tube Coil** | Air-side heat exchanger | Cold rooms, AHU coils | | **Baudelot Cooler** | Plate-type liquid cooler | Milk cooling in dairy | ### 2.4.2 Cooling Capacity Equation $$Q_L = \dot{m} \cdot (h_g - h_f) = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}_{\text{evap}}$$ --- ## 2.5 Cooling Towers A cooling tower rejects condenser heat to atmosphere via **evaporative cooling**. ### 2.5.1 Classification | Type | Draft Mechanism | Capacity Range | |------|-----------------|----------------| | **Natural Draft (Hyperbolic)** | Atmospheric buoyancy | > 1000 TR (power plants) | | **Forced Draft** | Fan pushes air | 50–500 TR | | **Induced Draft** | Fan pulls air | 50–1000 TR | | **Counter-Flow** | Air ↑ opposite to water ↓ | High efficiency | | **Cross-Flow** | Air ⟂ to water ↓ | Low pumping head | ### 2.5.2 Key Performance — Range & Approach $$\text{Range} = T_{w,\text{out}} - T_{w,\text{in}}$$ $$\text{Approach} = T_{w,\text{out}} - T_{wb,\text{ambient}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Range** indicates tower size; **Approach** indicates tower efficiency. A smaller approach = better cooling tower (typically **Approach ≥ 4°C**). --- ## 2.6 Refrigerant Leakage Detection | Method | Principle | Best For | |--------|-----------|----------| | **Halide Torch** | Copper flame turns green with halogen refrigerants | CFC/HCFC field check | | **Electronic Detector (Heated Diode/Sensor)** | Heated sensor detects refrigerant | All refrigerants, high sensitivity | | **Soap-Bubble Solution** | Visual bubble formation | Pipe joints, accessible fittings | | **UV Dye + Blacklight** | Dye fluoresces at leak | Slow leaks, oil traces | | **Ultrasonic Detector** | Detects turbulent hiss | Pressurized systems, noisy plants | | **Pressure Decay Test** | Monitor pressure drop over time | Vessel/evaporator integrity | --- ## 2.7 Refrigerant Charging **Refrigerant Charge Equation:** $$m_{\text{charge}} = V_{\text{system}} \cdot \rho_{\text{ref,liq}} \cdot \text{charge factor}$$ where typical charge factor for industrial systems = **0.85 to 0.95** (due to non-100% liquid fill). **Charging Methods:** - **Liquid charging** (preferred for large charges — faster, no risk of diluting oil). - **Vapor charging** (safer for small systems; avoids liquid slugging at compressor). --- ## 2.8 System Controls **Primary Control Loops:** 1. **Temperature Control** (thermostat, RTD, thermistor) 2. **Pressure Control** (low-pressure switch, high-pressure cutout) 3. **Oil Pressure Safety** (compressor protection) 4. **Defrost Control** (electric, hot-gas, hot-water) 5. **Capacity Control** (cylinder unloading, VFD, slide valve) 6. **Flow & Level Control** (float switches, flow switches) 7. **Centralized DDC/PLC** (Building Management System) > [!NOTE] > **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER) vs COP:** > $$\text{EER} = \frac{Q_L \text{ (BTU/hr)}}{W \text{ (W)}}$$ > $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{EER}}{3.412}$$ ## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $Q_L = \dot{m}(h_g - h_f)$ | Refrigeration Effect | | 2 | $W_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor Work | | 3 | $Q_H = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$ | Heat Rejection | | 4 | $\text{COP} = Q_L / W_c = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ | Ideal Vapor Compression | | 5 | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ | Reciprocating VE | | 6 | $\text{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | HX Design | | 7 | $h_3 = h_4$ | Throttling Process | | 8 | Range = $T_{w,\text{out}} - T_{w,\text{in}}$ | Cooling Tower | | 9 | Approach = $T_{w,\text{out}} - T_{wb}$ | Cooling Tower | | 10 | $m = V \cdot \rho_{\text{liq}} \cdot \text{CF}$ | Refrigerant Charge | | 11 | $\text{EER} = 3.412 \cdot \text{COP}$ | Unit Conversion | ## 2.10 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Ammonia (R-717) Plants** → Screw compressors + shell-and-tube condensers + flooded evaporators. - **Food Processing** → Plate heat exchangers + glycol secondary loops. - **Pharmaceutical Cold Chain** → Dual compressor + electronic expansion valves + redundant controls. - **District Cooling** → Centrifugal chillers + hyperbolic cooling towers. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: COP of an Ideal Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRS) ### Given: - Evaporator temperature: $T_L$ (K) - Condenser temperature: $T_H$ (K) - All processes ideal (isentropic compression, isenthalpic expansion, no pressure drops) ### Step 1: Apply the First Law to each component **Evaporator (Heat absorbed at low temperature):** $$Q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(J/kg of refrigerant)}$$ **Compressor (Work input):** $$W_c = h_2 - h_1$$ **Condenser (Heat rejected):** $$Q_H = h_2 - h_3$$ **Expansion Valve (Throttling):** $$h_3 = h_4$$ ### Step 2: Apply Energy Balance to the entire cycle By the First Law for a cyclic process: $$Q_L + W_c = Q_H$$ ### Step 3: Define Coefficient of Performance $$\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_c}$$ Substituting the energy balance: $$\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ ### Step 4: Convert to Temperature Form (Reversible Cycle) For a reversible cycle, from the Second Law (Clausius equality): $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ For the two isothermal heat transfers (evaporator at $T_L$ and condenser at $T_H$): $$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$$ Therefore: $$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ ### Step 5: Substitute $Q_H$ into COP $$\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot (T_H / T_L) - Q_L}$$ Factor out $Q_L$: $$\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{(T_H / T_L) - 1} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ ### Step 6: Final Result $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ **Worked Numerical Example:** - $T_L = -10°C = 263.15 \text{ K}$ - $T_H = 40°C = 313.15 \text{ K}$ $$\text{COP} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} = 5.263$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** For every **1 kW** of compressor work, the ideal cycle delivers **5.263 kW** of cooling. Real cycles achieve 50–70% of this due to compressor inefficiency, pressure drops, and superheat/subcooling. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Efficiency of Reciprocating Compressor ### Given: - Clearance volume: $V_c$ - Swept volume: $V_s$ - Pressure ratio: $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ - Polytropic index: $n$ ### Step 1: Clearance Ratio Definition $$C = \frac{V_c}{V_s}$$ ### Step 2: Actual Volume of Refrigerant Induced **Stage 1 — Clearance volume at suction pressure expands polytropically to $P_1$:** $$V_4 = V_c \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = V_c \cdot r_p^{1/n}$$ **Stage 2 — Effective suction volume:** $$V_{\text{actual}} = V_s - (V_4 - V_c) = V_s - V_c \cdot (r_p^{1/n} - 1)$$ ### Step 3: Express in Terms of Clearance Ratio Divide both sides by $V_s$: $$\eta_v = 1 - C(r_p^{1/n} - 1) = 1 + C - C \cdot r_p^{1/n}$$ ### Step 4: Final Result $$\boxed{\eta_v = 1 + C - C \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}}$$ **Numerical Verification:** - $C = 0.04$, $P_1 = 2$ bar, $P_2 = 10$ bar, $n = 1.3$ $$\eta_v = 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \cdot (10/2)^{1/1.3}$$ $$\eta_v = 1.04 - 0.04 \cdot (5)^{0.7692}$$ $$5^{0.7692} \approx 3.486$$ $$\eta_v = 1.04 - 0.1394 = 0.9006 \approx 90.1\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Re-expansion loss** reduces the effective suction volume. Lower clearance ratio = higher volumetric efficiency. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a Condenser ### Given: - Hot fluid (refrigerant) at constant $T_{\text{cond}}$ - Cold fluid (cooling water) entering at $T_{w,i}$, leaving at $T_{w,o}$ - Counter-flow arrangement ### Step 1: Define End Temperature Differences $$\Delta T_1 = T_{\text{cond}} - T_{w,i} \quad \text{(hot end, large ΔT)}$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{\text{cond}} - T_{w,o} \quad \text{(cold end, small ΔT)}$$ ### Step 2: Differential Heat Transfer Analysis For an elemental area $dA$: $$\delta Q = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$$ For a counter-flow heat exchanger, the temperature difference varies logarithmically. Integrating the differential heat transfer over the length of the exchanger yields: ### Step 3: LMTD Formula $$\boxed{\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **LMTD Correction Factor (F-factor)** must be applied for multi-pass or cross-flow heat exchangers where temperature profiles cross. For pure counter-flow shell-and-tube condensers, $F = 1.0$. **Numerical Example (Condenser):** - $T_{\text{cond}} = 40°C = 313.15 \text{ K}$ - $T_{w,i} = 25°C$, $T_{w,o} = 33°C$ $$\Delta T_1 = 40 - 25 = 15°C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = 40 - 33 = 7°C$$ $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{15 - 7}{\ln(15/7)} = \frac{8}{\ln(2.143)} = \frac{8}{0.7621} = 10.50°C$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Cooling Tower Range and Approach — Mass & Energy Balance ### Given: - Water flow rate: $\dot{m}_w$ (kg/hr) - Air flow rate: $\dot{m}_a$ (kg/hr) - Air inlet wet-bulb: $T_{wb,i}$ (°C) - Water inlet: $T_{w,i}$, outlet: $T_{w,o}$ ### Step 1: Mass Balance on Water Water lost to evaporation = Make-up water required: $$\dot{m}_{\text{evap}} = \dot{m}_a \cdot (W_o - W_i)$$ where $W_i, W_o$ are humidity ratios. ### Step 2: Energy Balance (Heat Rejected) Heat rejected by water = Heat absorbed by air (sensible + latent): $$\dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}) = \dot{m}_a \cdot (h_{a,o} - h_{a,i})$$ ### Step 3: Define Range and Approach $$\text{Range} = T_{w,o} - T_{w,i}$$ $$\text{Approach} = T_{w,o} - T_{wb,i}$$ ### Step 4: Simplified Performance Equation For a typical cooling tower with $\dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_w$ ratio and effectiveness $\varepsilon$: $$\text{Range} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_w} \cdot \frac{c_{p,a}(T_{wb,o} - T_{wb,i})}{c_{p,w}}$$ **Numerical Example:** - $\dot{m}_w = 50000$ kg/hr - $\dot{m}_a = 60000$ kg/hr - $T_{w,i} = 37°C$, $T_{w,o} = 32°C$, $T_{wb,i} = 27°C$ $$\text{Range} = 32 - 37 = -5°C \text{ (cooled by 5°C, taken as Range = 5°C)}$$ $$\text{Approach} = 32 - 27 = 5°C$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A cooling tower with **Approach = 5°C** is considered **good performance**. Approach < 3°C indicates a **high-efficiency** tower. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Compressor Selection | Criterion | Reciprocating | Screw | Centrifugal | Scroll | |-----------|---------------|-------|-------------|--------| | **Capacity (TR)** | 0.5 – 150 | 20 – 1500 | 150 – 10000+ | 1 – 60 | | **Pressure Ratio** | High (up to 12) | Medium (up to 8) | Low-Medium (up to 4) | Medium | | **Isentropic Efficiency** | 70 – 85% | 75 – 85% | 80 – 88% | 75 – 85% | | **Capacity Control** | Cylinder unloader | Slide valve (10-100%) | Inlet guide vane + VFD | Modulation | | **Part-Load Performance** | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent (VFD) | Good | | **Maintenance** | High (valves, rings) | Low (no contact) | Low | Very Low | | **Industrial Use** | Cold storage, dairy | Process, marine, food | District, petrochem | Precision, AC | --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Expansion Device Selection | Criterion | Capillary | TEV | EEV | Float Valve | AEV | |-----------|-----------|-----|-----|-------------|-----| | **Load Variation Tolerance** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good | Fair | | **Superheat Control** | No | Yes (constant) | Yes (variable) | N/A (flooded) | No | | **Industrial Use** | Domestic only | DX chillers | Modern chillers | Flooded evap | Limited | | **Electronic Control** | No | No | Yes | No | No | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Evaporator
Q_L absorbed
T_L = -10 to 5 C] --> B[Compressor
W_c input
h1 to h2] B --> C[Condenser
Q_H rejected
T_H = 35 to 50 C] C --> D[Expansion Device
h3 = h4
Pressure drop] D --> A E[Cooling Tower
Approach 4 to 6 C] --> C C --> E F[Receiver
Liquid storage] --> D C --> F G[System Controls
Thermostat / Pressure / Defrost] -.-> B G -.-> D H[Leak Detection
Periodic inspection] -.-> A H -.-> C I[Charging Port
Service valve] -.-> F ``` ## 4.2 Compressor Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Industrial Refrigeration
Application] --> Q1{Required
Capacity TR?} Q1 -->|< 10 TR| Q2{Need Variable
Capacity?} Q1 -->|10 to 150 TR| Q3{Continuous
Duty?} Q1 -->|150 to 1500 TR| Q4{High Pressure
Ratio > 5?} Q1 -->|> 1500 TR| Cent[Centrifugal
Compressor] Q2 -->|Yes| Scroll1[Scroll / Digital
Compressor] Q2 -->|No| Recip1[Reciprocating
Hermetic] Q3 -->|Yes| Screw1[Screw Compressor
with Slide Valve] Q3 -->|No| Recip2[Reciprocating
Semi-Hermetic] Q4 -->|Yes| Recip3[Reciprocating
Multi-Stage] Q4 -->|No| Screw2[Screw or
Centrifugal] Cent --> Done[Selected for
District Cooling] Scroll1 --> Done Recip1 --> Done Screw1 --> Done Recip2 --> Done Recip3 --> Done Screw2 --> Done ``` ## 4.3 Industrial Refrigeration Plant — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Process_Load[Process Load - e.g. Cold Storage] P1[Cold Room 1] P2[Process Chiller] P3[Freezer Tunnel] end subgraph Refrigerant_Loop[Refrigerant Loop - NH3 R-717] E1[Evaporator 1
Flooded] E2[Evaporator 2

Shell and Tube] RV1[Expansion Valves
Float + TEV] end subgraph Secondary_Loop[Secondary Loop - Glycol / Brine] S1[Chilled Water Pump] S2[Glycol Cooler] end subgraph Heat_Rejection[Heat Rejection Subsystem] CT1[Cooling Tower
Induced Draft] CW1[Condenser Water Pump] end subgraph Auxiliaries[Auxiliary Subsystems] LK1[Leak Detector
NH3 Sensor] CH1[Charging Station
Recovery Cylinder] CTL1[DDC Controller
PLC + SCADA] SF1[Safety Interlocks
HP LP OP Cutouts] end P1 --> E1 P2 --> S2 P3 --> E2 E1 --> C1 E2 --> C1 C1 --> CD1 CD1 --> CT1 CD1 --> RV1 RV1 --> E1 RV1 --> E2 S2 --> E2 CT1 --> CW1 CW1 --> CD1 LK1 -.-> E1 LK1 -.-> CD1 CH1 -.-> C1 CTL1 -.-> C1 CTL1 -.-> RV1 SF1 -.-> C1 ``` ## 4.4 Cooling Tower — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Condenser Water
T_wi = 37 C] --> B[Water Distribution

T_wo = 32 C] F[Atmospheric Air

Water-Cooled] I -.-> A ``` ## 4.5 Refrigerant Leakage Detection — Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Suspected Refrigerant Leak] --> Q1{System
Pressurized?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Refrigerant
Type?} Q1 -->|No| PressureTest[Pressure Decay Test
Nitrogen or Dry Air] Q2 -->|CFC / HCFC| Halide[Halide Torch Test] Q2 -->|HFC / HFO| Electronic[Electronic Detector
Heated Diode] Q2 -->|Ammonia NH3| NH3Sensor[NH3 Specific Sensor
Electrochemical] Q2 -->|Unknown| Soap[Soap Bubble Solution
Universal Method] Halide --> Confirm[Locate and Repair] Electronic --> Confirm NH3Sensor --> Confirm Soap --> Confirm PressureTest --> Confirm UV[UV Dye Injection
for Slow Leaks] -.-> Confirm Ultra[Ultrasonic Detector
for Pressurized Systems] -.-> Confirm ``` ## 4.6 System Controls — Interlock Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Sensor1[Thermostat
Temperature Sensor] --> PLC Sensor2[High Pressure Switch
HPS] --> PLC Sensor3[Low Pressure Switch
LPS] --> PLC Sensor4[Oil Pressure Switch
OPS] --> PLC Sensor5[Flow Switch
Condenser Water] --> PLC PLC[PLC / DDC Controller
Brain of the System] --> Relay1 PLC --> Relay2 PLC --> Relay3 PLC --> VFD Relay1[Contactor Coil] --> Motor1[Compressor Motor] Relay2[Solenoid Valve Coil] --> Sol[Liquid Line Solenoid] Relay3[Defrost Heater Relay] --> Heater[Defrost Heater] VFD[Variable Frequency Drive] --> Motor2[Fan Motor - Condenser] Motor1 -.-> Status[Status Feedback] Sol -.-> Status Heater -.-> Status Motor2 -.-> Status Status -.-> PLC ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Explain the working of a thermostatic expansion valve (TEV) with a neat sketch. Why is constant superheat maintained?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) is an automatic throttling device that regulates refrigerant flow into the evaporator to maintain a constant superheat at the evaporator outlet. **Working Principle:** 1. The **bulb** is clamped at the evaporator outlet, sensing the temperature of superheated vapor. 2. The bulb pressure ($P_b$) acts on top of the diaphragm. 3. Spring pressure ($P_{\text{spring}}$) and evaporator pressure ($P_e$) act from below. 4. The valve modulates open or closed based on the force balance: $$P_b = P_e + P_{\text{spring}}$$ 5. When load increases → suction superheat rises → bulb pressure rises → valve opens more → more refrigerant flows → superheat returns to set value. **Why constant superheat is maintained:** - To ensure **complete evaporation** of liquid refrigerant before it reaches the compressor. - Prevents **liquid slugging** at compressor suction (which causes valve damage). - Ensures **maximum utilization** of evaporator surface. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often forget to mention the **three forces** acting on the diaphragm. Always state: Bulb Pressure (↑) vs Evaporator Pressure + Spring Pressure (↓). Partial mark deduction if omitted. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model]` **Compare reciprocating and screw compressors with respect to capacity range, capacity control, and industrial applications.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Reciprocating | Screw | |-----------|---------------|-------| | **Capacity Range** | 0.5 – 150 TR | 20 – 1500 TR | | **Capacity Control** | Cylinder unloading (on/off, stepped) | Slide valve (continuous 10%–100%) | | **Industrial Applications** | Small cold storage, dairy, ice plants, pharma | Large cold storage, food processing, marine, process cooling, ammonia plants | | **Maintenance** | High (pistons, rings, valves) | Low (no contact between rotors) | | **Part-Load Efficiency** | Moderate | Excellent | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the construction and working of a **screw compressor** used in industrial refrigeration. Discuss its **capacity control** mechanism. (7 Marks) **(b)** A refrigeration plant uses **R-134a** as refrigerant. The compressor operates between evaporator temperature $T_L = -10°C$ and condenser temperature $T_H = 40°C$. Calculate the **ideal COP** of the cycle. Also compute the **refrigeration effect per kg** and **compressor work per kg** using the following enthalpy values: - $h_1$ (saturated vapor at $-10°C$) = 392.7 kJ/kg - $h_2$ (superheated vapor at 1.0 MPa, $40°C$ condenser) = 430.5 kJ/kg - $h_3$ (saturated liquid at $40°C$) = 256.4 kJ/kg - $h_4 = h_3 = 256.4$ kJ/kg (throttling) (7 Marks) --- #### Part (a) Model Answer — Screw Compressor (7 Marks) **Construction:** A screw compressor consists of **two intermeshing helical rotors** housed in a casing: - **Male Rotor** (driving rotor): 3–6 lobes, smaller in size. - **Female Rotor** (driven rotor): 5–7 flutes, larger in size. - Rotors are separated by a small clearance (~few microns). - A **slide valve** mounted on the casing enables capacity control. - **Suction port**, **discharge port**, and **oil injection port** (oil-cooled type). **Working Principle (4 stages of compression):** 1. **Suction Stage:** Refrigerant vapor enters the gap between the rotors through the suction port as the rotors unmesh. The volume of the trapped gas pocket increases (suction). 2. **Compression Stage:** As rotors continue to mesh, the trapped refrigerant is carried along the casing and compressed. Volume decreases, pressure rises. 3. **Discharge Stage:** When the trapped pocket reaches the discharge port, high-pressure refrigerant is pushed out. 4. **Slide Valve Action:** The slide valve (a movable block on the casing) creates a bypass opening, allowing part of the refrigerant to return to the suction side — this varies effective displacement. **Capacity Control:** - **Slide Valve** moves axially to vary the effective suction volume. - Capacity range: **10% to 100%** of full load. - The un-loaded portion of refrigerant bypasses back to suction — continuous modulation. - **VFD option:** Variable frequency drive on motor for wider turndown. **Industrial Applications:** - Large cold storage (> 100 TR) - Food processing (dairy, meat, fish) - Marine refrigeration - Process cooling in chemical/petrochemical plants - Ammonia (R-717) industrial systems - District cooling > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Part a):** Students often draw a piston-cylinder diagram and call it a screw compressor. This is a major error leading to **2–3 mark deduction**. The helical rotor profile and intermeshing meshing must be clearly shown. --- #### Part (b) Model Solution — COP & Performance (7 Marks) **Step 1: Compute Refrigeration Effect (RE):** $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 392.7 - 256.4 = 136.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Compute Compressor Work (W_c):** $$W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 430.5 - 392.7 = 37.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3: Compute Heat Rejection (Q_H):** $$Q_H = h_2 - h_3 = 430.5 - 256.4 = 174.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4: Verify Energy Balance:** $$Q_L + W_c = 136.3 + 37.8 = 174.1 \text{ kJ/kg} = Q_H \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5: Compute Actual COP (this cycle):** $$\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_c} = \frac{136.3}{37.8} = 3.606$$ **Step 6: Compute Ideal (Carnot) COP:** Convert temperatures to Kelvin: $$T_L = -10 + 273.15 = 263.15 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15 \text{ K}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263.15}{313.15 - 263.15} = \frac{263.15}{50.00} = 5.263$$ **Step 7: Compute Second-Law (Efficiency) Ratio:** $$\eta_{\text{II}} = \frac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}}} = \frac{3.606}{5.263} = 0.6852 = 68.52\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** > 1. Do NOT compute COP using temperatures directly in °C — always convert to **Kelvin**. > 2. Show **units** at every step (kJ/kg). > 3. State the energy balance check $Q_L + W_c = Q_H$ as a verification — this is the **examiner's favorite** step. > 4. Final answer must include **both** COP_actual and COP_ideal for full marks. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` **(a)** Describe the construction and working of an **induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower** with a neat diagram. Explain **Range** and **Approach** with reference to cooling tower performance. (7 Marks) **(b)** A water-cooled condenser in an industrial refrigeration plant operates at the following conditions: - Condensing temperature: $T_{\text{cond}} = 40°C$ - Cooling water inlet: $T_{w,i} = 28°C$ - Cooling water outlet: $T_{w,o} = 33°C$ - Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U = 1500$ W/m²·K Calculate the **LMTD**, **heat rejected (Q_H)** per unit area, and the **condenser area** required to reject 50 kW of heat. (7 Marks) --- #### Part (a) Model Answer — Induced-Draft Counter-Flow Cooling Tower (7 Marks) **Construction:** An **induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower** consists of: 1. **Casing / Shell** — typically FRP (Fiber-Reinforced Plastic) or concrete. 2. **Water Distribution System** — spray nozzles at the top to sprinkle water uniformly. 3. **Fill / Packing** — PVC splash or film fills that maximize air-water contact. 4. **Air Inlet Louvers** — located at the base of the tower, allowing atmospheric air entry. 5. **Fan Assembly (Induced Draft)** — axial-flow fan mounted at the top, pulling air upward. 6. **Drift Eliminators** — located above the fill to capture entrained water droplets. 7. **Cold Water Basin** — at the bottom, collects cooled water and sends it back to the condenser. **Working Principle:** - Warm water from the condenser is pumped to the top. - Spray nozzles distribute water over the **fill** as a thin film. - The induced-draft fan **pulls** atmospheric air upward through the louvers. - Air flows **upward** (counter to the falling water) — this is the **counter-flow** configuration. - Heat exchange occurs in three modes: - **Sensible heat transfer** (water → air by temperature difference) - **Latent heat transfer** (evaporation of a small fraction of water) - **Mass transfer** (water vapor leaves with the air) - Cooled water collects in the basin and is recirculated. - Warm saturated air exits at the top through the fan stack. **Range and Approach:** - **Range** = $T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}$ (water temperature drop through the tower) - Indicates the **size/heat duty** of the tower. - Typical: 5°C to 10°C. - **Approach** = $T_{w,o} - T_{wb,\text{ambient}}$ (closeness of cooled water to ambient wet-bulb) - Indicates the **efficiency** of the tower. - Lower approach = better performance. - Typical: 4°C to 6°C (good); below 3°C (excellent). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Part a):** > 1. Confusing **forced draft** (fan at bottom) with **induced draft** (fan at top) — marks deducted. > 2. Not specifying **counter-flow** vs **cross-flow** — this is the most common error. > 3. Forgetting to mention **drift eliminators** — they prevent water loss and Legionella risk. --- #### Part (b) Model Solution — LMTD and Condenser Area (7 Marks) **Step 1: Calculate End Temperature Differences** $$\Delta T_1 = T_{\text{cond}} - T_{w,i} = 40 - 28 = 12°C$$ $$\Delta T_2 = T_{\text{cond}} - T_{w,o} = 40 - 33 = 7°C$$ **Step 2: Calculate LMTD** $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{12 - 7}{\ln(12/7)}$$ $$\text{LMTD} = \frac{5}{\ln(1.7143)} = \frac{5}{0.5390} = 9.276°C$$ **Step 3: Calculate Heat Flux (Q_H per unit area)** $$\frac{Q_H}{A} = U \cdot \text{LMTD} = 1500 \times 9.276 = 13{,}914 \text{ W/m}^2 = 13.914 \text{ kW/m}^2$$ **Step 4: Calculate Required Condenser Area** $$A = \frac{Q_H}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ W}}{1500 \times 9.276}$$ $$A = \frac{50{,}000}{13{,}914} = 3.594 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5: Verify with Sanity Check (Approximate):** - LMTD ≈ 9.3°C - A = 50 kW / (1.5 kW/m²·K × 9.3 K) ≈ 3.58 m² ✓ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall (Part b):** > 1. Using **arithmetic mean** $(12+7)/2 = 9.5°C$ instead of **log-mean** is a major error — shows lack of conceptual clarity. > 2. Forgetting unit conversions (W vs kW). > 3. Not showing the **ln** computation step. --- ## 5.3 Additional KTU-Style Practice Questions | Q# | Question Statement | Marks | Module Tag | |----|-------------------|-------|------------| | 1 | Differentiate between flooded and DX evaporators. | 3 | M3 | | 2 | Explain the working of an electronic expansion valve (EEV) and state two industrial applications. | 3 | M3 | | 3 | List four methods of refrigerant leak detection and explain any one in detail. | 3 | M3 | | 4 | What is refrigerant charging? Explain liquid and vapor charging methods. | 3 | M3 | | 5 | Explain the function of high-pressure cutout, low-pressure cutout, and oil pressure switch in a refrigeration system. | 7 | M3 | | 6 | A cooling tower handles 100,000 kg/hr of water. Inlet temperature = 38°C, outlet = 30°C, ambient wet-bulb = 26°C. Find Range, Approach, and effectiveness. | 7 | M3 | --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3** ### A. Compressors - [ ] **Reciprocating** — high compression ratio, low-to-medium capacity, cylinder unloading control. - [ ] **Screw** — medium-large capacity, slide valve control (10–100%), continuous modulation. - [ ] **Centrifugal** — very large capacity, low compression ratio, VFD/inlet guide vane control. - [ ] **Scroll** — compact, oil-free, used in precision cooling and AC. - [ ] **Volumetric efficiency formula:** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$. ### B. Condensers - [ ] Water-cooled (shell-and-tube) — most efficient for large plants. - [ ] Air-cooled — limited by ambient dry-bulb temperature. - [ ] Evaporative — combines air-cooled and water-spray cooling. - [ ] **LMTD formula** is the examiner's favorite question. ### C. Expansion Devices - [ ] **TEV** — maintains constant superheat (most common in DX). - [ ] **EEV** — electronic, fast response, modern chillers. - [ ] **Float valve** — used in flooded evaporators. - [ ] **Capillary tube** — fixed, small systems only. - [ ] **Throttling:** $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic). ### D. Evaporators - [ ] **Flooded** — high efficiency, used with ammonia and chillers. - [ ] **DX** — simpler, used in cold rooms and AC. - [ ] **Plate heat exchanger** — compact, high U-value. - [ ] **Q_L** is always calculated at evaporator using $h_g - h_f$. ### E. Cooling Towers - [ ] **Induced draft** — fan at top; better air distribution. - [ ] **Forced draft** — fan at bottom; cheaper, more recirculation. - [ ] **Natural draft** — hyperbolic, used in power plants. - [ ] **Range** = water cooling; **Approach** = tower efficiency. ### F. Leak Detection - [ ] **Electronic detectors** — most sensitive, all refrigerants. - [ ] **Halide torch** — only for CFC/HCFC (not for HFC). - [ ] **Soap bubble** — universal, simple. - [ ] **UV dye** — best for slow oil-traced leaks. - [ ] **Ultrasonic** — best for high-pressure systems. ### G. Refrigerant Charging - [ ] **Liquid charging** — fast, for large amounts; never into suction line. - [ ] **Vapor charging** — slower, safer for small amounts. - [ ] **Charge formula:** $m = V \cdot \rho_{\text{liq}} \cdot \text{charge factor}$ (CF = 0.85–0.95). - [ ] Always **recover** refrigerant before opening system (environmental compliance). ### H. System Controls - [ ] **Thermostat** — primary temperature control. - [ ] **HPC / LPC** — pressure safety cutouts. - [ ] **OPS** — oil pressure differential switch. - [ ] **Defrost** — electric / hot-gas / hot-water. - [ ] **Capacity control** — slide valve, VFD, cylinder unloading. - [ ] **DDC/PLC** — modern centralized control with SCADA. ### I. Numerical Must-Knows - [ ] **COP** = $Q_L / W_c$ = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ (ideal). - [ ] **EER** = 3.412 × COP. - [ ] **LMTD** = $(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$. - [ ] **Refrigeration effect** = $h_1 - h_4$. - [ ] **Compressor work** = $h_2 - h_1$. - [ ] **Heat rejection** = $h_2 - h_3$. ### J. Examiner's Do's and Don'ts - [ ] **DO** convert °C to K for all temperature-based formulas. - [ ] **DO** show energy balance check ($Q_L + W_c = Q_H$). - [ ] **DO** label all diagrams with parts and process states. - [ ] **DON'T** use arithmetic mean instead of log-mean. - [ ] **DON'T** confuse induced-draft with forced-draft cooling towers. - [ ] **DON'T** forget to state units (kW, kJ/kg, °C, K, m²). --- > **End of Module 3 Notes — PEIET412 Industrial Refrigeration and Air Conditioning** > **Prepared under KTU 2024 Scheme | NEP 2020 Aligned Outcome-Based Education**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Air (Saturated, Unsaturated, Dew Point, Humidity Levels, Thermodynamic Equations, Adiabatic Saturation Process, Psychrometric Charts, Processes - Sensible Heating/Cooling, Humidifying/Dehumidifying, Air Washers, Bypass Factors, Sensible Heat Factor, RSHF, GSHF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Moist Air" (Psychrometrics)?

In **Air Conditioning Engineering**, the term "air" never refers to pure, dry, ideal gas. It always means a binary mixture of two real gases: **Dry Air (D.A.)** + **Water Vapour (W.V.)**. The study of the thermodynamic properties of this moist mixture is called **Psychrometrics** (from Greek: *psychro* = cold, *metron* = measure). The instrument used to measure these properties is a **Psychrometer** (or Hygrometer), and the chart used to graphically read all properties simultaneously is a **Psychrometric Chart**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *Psychrometrics is the science which deals with the study of thermodynamic properties of a mixture of dry air and water vapour, and the changes taking place in those properties when the mixture undergoes any of the standard air-conditioning processes.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sealed Soda Bottle"

Imagine a sealed plastic bottle of cold-drink (soda). The space above the liquid is filled with **CO₂ gas + water vapour + air**. If you heat the bottle gently (without shaking it), the air can hold more moisture — the humidity inside rises. The moment you open the cap, the pressure drops, the mixture cools, and water droplets instantly condense on the outside — that's a **dew point** being crossed. The dry air is the "boring, steady" part that just occupies space, while the water vapour is the "excitable, changeable" part that absorbs heat, condenses, evaporates, and drives all our cooling-load calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study it?**
> Human thermal comfort, industrial drying, pharmaceutical storage, textile mills, and cold storage rooms all depend on controlling *not just the temperature, but the moisture content* of air. The KTU paper will test both the conceptual meaning of each property *and* the calculation of cooling/heating loads.

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental States of Moist Air

| State | Definition | Real-world example |
|---|---|---|
| **Saturated Air** | Air which **cannot hold any more water vapour** at its current temperature. Partial pressure of vapour = saturation pressure (Pₛ). Relative Humidity = **100%**. | A misty morning in Munnar, fog inside a freezer room door, dew on grass at dawn. |
| **Unsaturated Air** | Air that **can still absorb additional moisture** at its current temperature. Partial pressure of vapour < saturation pressure. RH < **100%**. | Normal AC-conditioned office room, desert air at noon, indoor living spaces. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Trap:** A common examiner trap is: *"Is saturated air dry or moist?"* — Saturated air is the **moistest possible** air for that given temperature. It contains the **maximum** water vapour. It is NOT dry. Mist and clouds are made of saturated air, not dry air.

### 1.4 Standard Reference (KTU Board Convention)

For all KTU numericals, unless otherwise stated:
- **Atmospheric Pressure:** P = **1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa**
- **Dry Air Gas Constant:** Rₐ = **0.287 kJ/kg·K**
- **Water Vapour Gas Constant:** Rᵥ = **0.4615 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific Heat of Dry Air:** cₚₐ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific Heat of Water Vapour:** cₚᵥ = **1.88 kJ/kg·K**
- **Standard DBT Reference:** 0 °C = 273.15 K

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master "Six Properties" of Moist Air (KTU Mandatory List)

For every state point on the psychrometric chart, exactly **six properties** fully describe the air. Memorize these in order — every numerical problem revolves around them.

1. **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)** — `T` in °C
2. **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)** — `T*` in °C
3. **Dew Point Temperature (DPT)** — `T_dp` in °C
4. **Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio** — `ω` in kg water / kg dry air
5. **Relative Humidity (RH / φ)** — dimensionless (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%)
6. **Specific Enthalpy** — `h` in kJ/kg of dry air

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (for identifying a state):** Any **two independent properties** are sufficient to fully fix the state. The pair (DBT, WBT) is the most common because a psychrometer directly gives both.

### 2.2 Detailed Property-by-Property Explanation

#### (a) Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)
The temperature of the air measured by an **ordinary mercury-in-glass thermometer** whose bulb is kept **dry** and shielded from radiation. It is the true thermodynamic temperature of the moist air mixture. DBT is plotted on the **horizontal X-axis** of the psychrometric chart.

#### (b) Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)
The temperature reached when air flows rapidly over a thermometer bulb covered with a wet cotton wick. As water evaporates from the wick, it draws its latent heat of vaporization from the bulb, cooling it down. The **lower the relative humidity, the more evaporation, the lower the WBT** compared to DBT. For saturated air: DBT = WBT = DPT.

#### (c) Dew Point Temperature (DPT)
The temperature at which the air becomes **saturated** when cooled *at constant pressure and constant specific humidity (ω)*. Below this temperature, water vapour begins to condense as liquid droplets (dew/fog). It is plotted on the **saturation curve** (100% RH line) on the chart.

#### (d) Specific Humidity (ω) — Humidity Ratio
The mass of water vapour (mᵥ) carried per unit mass of dry air (mₐ). It is **the most important moisture property** for AC load calculations.

$$
\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \, \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}
$$

where Pᵥ is the partial pressure of water vapour in the mixture and P is the total atmospheric pressure.

#### (e) Relative Humidity (RH or φ)
The ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation pressure at the **same DBT**.

$$
\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s} = \frac{\omega \, P}{(0.622 + \omega) \, P_s}
$$

- φ = 0% → completely dry air
- φ = 100% → saturated air
- φ > 70% → feels "sticky/humid" to humans

#### (f) Specific Enthalpy (h)
Total heat content per kg of **dry air**. Reference point: 0 °C, 0 kJ/kg for both dry air and water.

$$
h = c_{pa} \, T + \omega \, (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \, T)
$$

Typically simplified at 0 °C reference as:

$$
h \approx 1.005 \, T + \omega \, (2500 + 1.88 \, T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of dry air]}
$$

### 2.3 The Complete KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All the following formulas are non-negotiable for KTU 2024.** Each one is a potential 2-mark or 7-mark sub-question.

| # | Property / Equation | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Humidity (ω) | $\omega = 0.622 \, \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg/kg of d.a. |
| 2 | Partial pressure from ω | $P_v = \dfrac{\omega \, P}{0.622 + \omega}$ | kPa or bar |
| 3 | Relative Humidity (φ) | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_s} = \dfrac{\omega P}{(0.622+\omega) P_s}$ | — |
| 4 | Degree of Saturation (μ) | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{\omega P_s}{0.622 \, P_v + P_v\,(0.622+\omega)\cdot...}$ ≈ $\omega/\omega_s$ | — |
| 5 | Specific Enthalpy (h) | $h = 1.005\,T + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,T)$ | kJ/kg d.a. |
| 6 | Specific Volume (v) | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P - P_v} = \dfrac{0.287\,T(1+1.6078\,\omega)}{P}$ | m³/kg d.a. |
| 7 | Density of moist air (ρ) | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{v}$ | kg/m³ |
| 8 | DBT ↔ WBT (approx) | $T^* = T - \dfrac{(P_s^* - P_v)(1538 - 1.6\,T^*)}{P - P_v}$ | °C (Carrier's eq.) |
| 9 | Sensible Heat (SH) | $SH = m_a \, c_{pa} \, \Delta T = 1.005 \, \dot{V} \, \Delta T$ | kW |
| 10 | Latent Heat (LH) | $LH = m_a \, (\omega_2 - \omega_1) \, h_{fg}$ | kW |
| 11 | Total Heat (TH) | $TH = m_a \, \Delta h$ | kW |
| 12 | Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) | $SHF = \dfrac{SH}{SH + LH} = \dfrac{SH}{TH}$ | — |
| 13 | RSHF | $RSHF = \dfrac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}$ | — |
| 14 | GSHF | $GSHF = \dfrac{GSH}{GSH + GLH}$ | — |
| 15 | Bypass Factor (BPF) | $BPF = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1} = \dfrac{h_2 - h_1}{h_3 - h_1}$ | — |

where subscripts 1, 2, 3 denote **entering (room)**, **leaving (supply)**, and **apparatus (coil surface)** conditions respectively.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Load Calculation:** Room Sensible Heat (RSH) + Room Latent Heat (RLH) = Total Room Heat, used to size the cooling coil.
- **Duct Sizing:** Uses specific volume (v) to convert mass flow to volume flow (m³/s or CFM).
- **Cold Storage & Pharma:** Strict control of RH prevents product spoilage, microbial growth, or static-electricity damage.
- **Industrial Drying:** Knowing ω helps select the right exhaust air conditions.
- **Comfort Index:** Operative temperature and humidity drive thermal comfort (PMV/PPD) calculations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Specific Humidity Formula (ω)

Let:
- mₐ = mass of dry air (kg)
- mᵥ = mass of water vapour (kg)
- Pₐ = partial pressure of dry air
- Pᵥ = partial pressure of water vapour
- P = total pressure = Pₐ + Pᵥ (Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures)

By **Ideal Gas Law** applied separately to each gas (since both behave ideally at AC conditions):

$$
P_a V = m_a R_a T \quad \text{...(1)}
$$
$$
P_v V = m_v R_v T \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

Dividing equation (2) by equation (1), the volume V and temperature T cancel out:

$$
\frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{m_v \, R_v}{m_a \, R_a}
$$

Solving for the humidity ratio ω = mᵥ/mₐ:

$$
\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{R_a}{R_v} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{R_a}{R_v} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}
$$

Substituting the values **Rₐ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** and **Rᵥ = 0.4615 kJ/kg·K**:

$$
\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{0.287}{0.4615} = 0.62197 \approx 0.622
$$

Therefore, the standard psychrometric equation:

$$
\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \, \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The 0.622 factor is simply the inverse ratio of the molecular weights of water (18 kg/kmol) and air (28.97 kg/kmol): 18/28.97 = 0.622.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Specific Enthalpy Equation (h)

By definition, the total enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air = enthalpy of dry air + enthalpy of water vapour, both measured **per kg of dry air**.

$$
h = h_a + \omega \, h_v
$$

Assuming linear specific heats (a valid assumption for the 0–60 °C AC range):

- Enthalpy of dry air (reference 0 °C = 0): $h_a = c_{pa} \, T = 1.005 \, T$
- Enthalpy of water vapour (reference 0 °C liquid = 0): $h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \, T = 2500 + 1.88\,T$

Substituting:

$$
h = 1.005\,T + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg of dry air]}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $h_{fg,0} = 2500$ kJ/kg is the latent heat of vaporization of water at the 0 °C reference. Some textbooks use 2501; KTU accepts either 2500 or 2501.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Specific Volume (v) of Moist Air

By definition, $v$ is the volume occupied per kg of **dry air**. From equation (1):

$$
v = \frac{V}{m_a} = \frac{R_a \, T}{P_a} = \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_v}
$$

Expressing in terms of humidity ratio ω (using $P_v = \omega P / (0.622+\omega)$):

$$
v = \frac{R_a T}{P} \left(1 + \frac{\omega}{0.622}\right) = \frac{R_a T (1 + 1.6078\,\omega)}{P}
$$

With **Rₐ = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** and **P = 101.325 kPa** (standard atmosphere):

$$
v = \frac{0.287 \, T (1 + 1.6078\,\omega)}{101.325} \quad \text{[m³/kg d.a.]}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The 1.6078 = 1/0.622 factor converts the humidity ratio to "air-equivalent" partial volume. This formula is what we use to compute the volume flow rate of supply air for a given cooling load.

### 3.4 The Adiabatic Saturation Process (AS) — Derivation of WBT

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Adiabatic Saturation"?** It is the *theoretical* process that defines the WBT. In practice, the wet-bulb thermometer does *not* follow this exactly (which is why Carrier's empirical equation differs from the AS derivation), but the AS process is what defines the **WBT on the psychrometric chart**.

Consider a long, perfectly insulated chamber (adiabatic walls) in which unsaturated air flows steadily over a large pool of recirculated water. Eventually the air leaves the chamber **saturated** (φ = 100%) at the adiabatic saturation temperature T* (= WBT), having absorbed the maximum possible moisture.

**Steady-flow Energy Balance** (per kg of dry air passing through):

Heat gained by water to evaporate = Heat lost by air (since adiabatic, net ΔH = 0)

$$
h_1 + (\omega_s^* - \omega_1) \, h_f^* = h_2^*
$$

where:
- Subscript 1 = entering unsaturated air
- Subscript 2* = leaving saturated air at T*
- ωₛ* = saturation humidity ratio at T*
- h_f* = saturated liquid enthalpy of water at T* (≈ 4.186 × T* kJ/kg, or simply c_pw·T*)

Rearranging for the **leaving enthalpy**:

$$
h_2^* = h_1 - (\omega_s^* - \omega_1) \, h_f^*
$$

This transcendental equation is solved iteratively: guess T*, compute ωₛ* from steam tables, then check if h₂* computed from the energy balance equals 1.005·T* + ωₛ*(2500 + 1.88·T*). Adjust T* and repeat.

> **Conversion Logic:** The AS process is *theoretically* the inverse of what the wet bulb experiences, but in practice the wet bulb is a tiny wick — heat and mass transfer are simultaneous. The AS process assumes the *bulk* air reaches saturation, which is why a real wick reads a few degrees *above* the true AS value at low humidity.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Relationships Between Properties

| Given two properties (DBT and WBT) | To find | Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| T, T* | Pᵥ (for ω) | $P_v = P_s^* - (P - P_s^*)\dfrac{c_{pw}(T - T^*)}{h_{fg}^*}$ |
| T, T* | ω | Use $P_v$ from above, then $\omega = 0.622 P_v / (P - P_v)$ |
| T, ω | h | Direct: $h = 1.005T + \omega(2500+1.88T)$ |
| T, φ | ω | Get $P_s$ at T from steam tables; $P_v = \phi P_s$; then ω = 0.622 Pᵥ/(P - Pᵥ) |
| T, ω | φ | Reverse the above |
| T, ω | DPT | Find T at which saturation ωₛ equals the given ω. This T is DPT. |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Master Map of Moist-Air Property Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputState[Input Air State]
        DBT[DBT: T deg C]
        WBT[WBT: T star deg C]
    end

    subgraph CoreProps[Core Psychrometric Properties]
        PHum[Partial Pressure Pv]
        SHum[Specific Humidity omega]
        RH[Relative Humidity phi]
        DPT[Dew Point Tdp]
        SE[Specific Enthalpy h]
        SV[Specific Volume v]
    end

    subgraph DerivedProps[Derived AC Quantities]
        SH[Sensible Heat SH]
        LH[Latent Heat LH]
        TH[Total Heat TH]
        SHF[Sensible Heat Factor SHF]
        RSHF[Room SHF]
        GSHF[Grand SHF]
        BPF[Coil Bypass Factor BPF]
    end

    DBT --> SHum
    WBT --> PHum
    PHum --> SHum
    SHum --> RH
    SHum --> DPT
    DBT --> SE
    SHum --> SE
    DBT --> SV
    SHum --> SV
    DBT --> SH
    SHum --> LH
    SH --> TH
    LH --> TH
    SH --> SHF
    LH --> SHF
    SHF --> RSHF
    SHF --> GSHF
    DBT --> BPF
    SHum --> BPF
    SE --> BPF
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Adiabatic Saturation Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASChamber[Adiabatic Saturation Chamber]
        direction TB
        Inlet[Unsaturated Air Inlet: T1, omega1]
        Recirc[Recirculated Water]
        Pad[Water Spray Pads]
        Outlet[Saturated Air Outlet: T star, omega s star]
        Wet[Water Pool at T star]
    end

    Inlet --> Pad
    Pad --> Wet
    Wet --> Outlet
    Recirc --> Pad
    Recirc --> Wet
```

**Functional Description:** Dry, unsaturated air enters from the left at DBT = T₁ and humidity ratio ω₁. It flows over the recirculated water pads; some water evaporates into the air, increasing its humidity and decreasing its DBT until the air leaves **saturated** (φ = 100%) at the adiabatic saturation temperature T* (= WBT on the chart). The chamber walls are perfectly insulated (adiabatic), so the **dry-bulb temperature drop equals the latent heat supplied by evaporation**.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: The Six Psychrometric Processes (AC Operations)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Process 1: Sensible Heating] -->|T rises, omega constant| P2[Process 2: Sensible Cooling]
    P2 -->|T falls, may condense| P3[Process 3: Cooling and Dehumidification]
    P3 -->|Water spray| P4[Process 4: Humidification only]
    P4 -->|Heat + water spray| P5[Process 5: Heating and Humidification]
    P5 -->|Cool coil after humid| P6[Process 6: Cooling and Humidification]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Cooling & Dehumidification Coil (Bypass Factor concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoilBlock[Cooling and Dehumidification Coil]
        direction TB
        RoomAir[Room Air: T1, omega1, h1, RH lt 100 percent] --> MixPoint[Mixing in Coil]
        CoilSurf[Coil Surface Temperature: T3, omega s3] --> MixPoint
        MixPoint --> Supply[Supply Air: T2, omega2, h2, RH higher than inlet]
    end

    BPFNote[Bypass Factor BPF = T2 minus T1 divided by T3 minus T1 equals h2 minus h1 divided by h3 minus h1]
    MixPoint -.-> BPFNote
```

**Engineering Note:** A portion of the room air **bypasses** the coil (slips through gaps, fin clearances) and never actually contacts the cold surface. The result is that the leaving air (state 2) is between the entering air (state 1) and the apparatus air (state 3 on the saturation curve at the coil surface temperature T₃). A **lower BPF = better coil contact = more effective cooling**.

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Air-Washer / Spray Chamber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AirWasher[Air Washer Unit]
        direction TB
        InAir[Hot Dry Unsaturated Air Inlet: T1, omega1] --> SprayNoz[Water Spray Nozzles]
        SprayNoz --> PlenumChamber[Plenum Chamber with Eliminator Plates]
        RecircPump[Recirculation Pump and Tank with Chilled Water] --> SprayNoz
        PlenumChamber --> OutAir[Cooled and Humidified Air Outlet: T2 lt T1, omega2 gt omega1]
    end
```

**Modes of Operation:** Depending on water temperature, the air washer can perform all three: (i) **Cooling + Humidification** (cold water spray), (ii) **Cooling + Dehumidification** (very cold water), (iii) **Heating + Humidification** (hot water spray).
