## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with units:**
(i) Specific humidity, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Dew point temperature.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(i) Specific Humidity (ω):** Mass of water vapour present per kg of dry air. Unit: kg water / kg dry air. Formula: $\omega = 0.622 \, P_v / (P - P_v)$.
- **(ii) Relative Humidity (φ):** Ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour in moist air to the saturation pressure of water at the **same dry-bulb temperature**. Unit: dimensionless (or %). $\phi = P_v / P_s$.
- **(iii) Dew Point Temperature (DPT):** The temperature at which the water vapour present in the air becomes saturated (i.e. begins to condense) when the air is cooled at constant pressure and constant specific humidity. Unit: °C.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the "adiabatic saturation process" and state two assumptions made in its analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The adiabatic saturation (AS) process is a theoretical steady-flow process in which unsaturated air is passed over a large surface of water inside a perfectly insulated chamber. The air leaves the chamber **saturated (100% RH)** at the **adiabatic saturation temperature T***, which is taken as the thermodynamic definition of the **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)**. Energy balance per kg d.a.:

$$
h_1 + (\omega_s^* - \omega_1) h_f^* = h_2^*
$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The chamber is perfectly insulated (adiabatic) and operates at steady state.
2. The mass of water evaporated is small compared with the recirculated water, so the water temperature remains uniform at T*.
3. The air leaves in thermal and mass equilibrium with the water (saturated at T*).

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice: Answer either Option A OR Option B.**

---

#### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A sample of moist air is at a DBT of 35 °C and a WBT of 25 °C. Calculate the **(i)** partial pressure of water vapour, **(ii)** specific humidity, **(iii)** relative humidity, **(iv)** dew point temperature, **(v)** specific enthalpy, and **(vi)** specific volume of the air.
Take atmospheric pressure = 101.325 kPa. Use steam-table values: $P_s$ at 35 °C = 5.628 kPa; $P_s$ at 25 °C = 3.169 kPa; $P_s$ at 17.5 °C = 2.0 kPa (approx). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The air from part (a) at a flow rate of **150 m³/min** is passed over a cooling coil whose apparatus (surface) temperature is 10 °C. The air is cooled to a DBT of 20 °C. Calculate the **(i)** coil bypass factor, **(ii)** heat removed in kW, and **(iii)** the rate of moisture condensation in kg/h. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution (Option A):

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of water vapour Pᵥ at WBT = 25 °C using Carrier's equation (or the AS approximation):**

For the psychrometric chart reading, Pᵥ is the partial pressure of vapour corresponding to the saturation pressure at the **dew point**, which is what we want to find. Using the wet-bulb relation:

$$
P_v = P_s^* - \frac{(P - P_s^*) \, c_{pw} \, (T - T^*)}{h_{fg}^*}
$$

with T = 35 °C, T* = 25 °C, Pₛ* = 3.169 kPa, h_fg* = 2450 kJ/kg (at 25 °C), c_pw = 4.186 kJ/kg·K, P = 101.325 kPa:

$$
P_v = 3.169 - \frac{(101.325 - 3.169)(4.186)(35 - 25)}{2450}
$$

$$
P_v = 3.169 - \frac{(98.156)(4.186)(10)}{2450} = 3.169 - \frac{4108.4}{2450}
$$

$$
P_v = 3.169 - 1.677 = 1.492 \text{ kPa}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Carrier's equation assumes the air is cooled from DBT to WBT, releasing sensible heat (4.186·ΔT per kg of vapour that would have to condense), which is balanced by the latent heat of that vapour. The 1.492 kPa represents how much vapour is actually in the air.

**Step 2 — Specific Humidity (ω):**

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.492}{101.325 - 1.492} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.492}{99.833}
$$

$$
\omega = 0.622 \times 0.01494 = 0.00929 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 3 — Relative Humidity (φ):** $P_s$ at 35 °C = 5.628 kPa.

$$
\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s} = \frac{1.492}{5.628} = 0.265 = 26.5\%
$$

**Step 4 — Dew Point Temperature (DPT):** The temperature at which the saturation pressure equals Pᵥ = 1.492 kPa. From steam tables, this corresponds to:

$$
T_{dp} \approx 13.0 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 5 — Specific Enthalpy (h):**

$$
h = 1.005(35) + 0.00929(2500 + 1.88 \times 35)
$$

$$
h = 35.175 + 0.00929(2500 + 65.8) = 35.175 + 0.00929(2565.8)
$$

$$
h = 35.175 + 23.84 = 59.01 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific Volume (v):**

$$
v = \frac{0.287 \times (35 + 273) \times (1 + 1.6078 \times 0.00929)}{101.325}
$$

$$
v = \frac{0.287 \times 308 \times (1 + 0.01494)}{101.325} = \frac{0.287 \times 308 \times 1.01494}{101.325}
$$

$$
v = \frac{89.70}{101.325} = 0.885 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg d.a.}
$$

> **Result Summary (Part a):** Pᵥ = 1.492 kPa, ω = 0.00929 kg/kg, φ = 26.5%, DPT ≈ 13 °C, h = 59.0 kJ/kg, v = 0.885 m³/kg.

---

**Part (b):**

**Given:** V̇ = 150 m³/min, T₁ = 35 °C, h₁ = 59.01 kJ/kg, ω₁ = 0.00929 kg/kg, T₃ = 10 °C (coil surface, on saturation line), T₂ = 20 °C (leaving air).

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate of dry air:**

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{V}}{v_1} = \frac{150/60}{0.885} = \frac{2.5}{0.885} = 2.825 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Bypass Factor (BPF):**

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1} = \frac{20 - 35}{10 - 35} = \frac{-15}{-25} = 0.60
$$

**Step 3 — Specific enthalpy at apparatus state 3 (saturated at 10 °C):**
At 10 °C, Pₛ = 1.2281 kPa, so:

$$
\omega_3 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.2281}{101.325 - 1.2281} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.2281}{100.097} = 0.00763 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

$$
h_3 = 1.005(10) + 0.00763(2500 + 1.88 \times 10) = 10.05 + 0.00763(2518.8) = 10.05 + 19.22 = 29.27 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat removed (using enthalpy drop, including latent):**

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_a \times (h_1 - h_2)
$$

For the leaving state 2, we read at T₂ = 20 °C. Assuming leaving RH gives ω₂ from the chart / steam table; if we assume leaving air follows a straight line on the chart, h₂ ≈ h₁ + BPF × (h₃ - h₁) = 59.01 + 0.6 × (29.27 - 59.01) = 59.01 - 17.84 = 41.17 kJ/kg.

$$
\dot{Q} = 2.825 \times (59.01 - 41.17) = 2.825 \times 17.84 = 50.4 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Moisture condensed:** The difference ω₁ - ω₂. From BPF interpolation:

$$
\omega_2 = \omega_1 + BPF \times (\omega_3 - \omega_1) = 0.00929 + 0.6 \times (0.00763 - 0.00929) = 0.00929 - 0.000996 = 0.00829 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{cond} = \dot{m}_a (\omega_1 - \omega_2) = 2.825 \times (0.00929 - 0.00829) = 2.825 \times 0.001 = 0.00283 \text{ kg/s} = 10.2 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> **Result Summary (Part b):** BPF = 0.60, Heat removed = 50.4 kW, Condensation = 10.2 kg/h.

---

#### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A lecture hall has a volume of 1200 m³. The air inside is at 30 °C DBT and 60% RH. Outside air is at 35 °C DBT and 40% RH. The fresh air supply is 25% of the total air circulation. The total room sensible heat is 25 kW and the total room latent heat is 10 kW. Calculate the **(i)** RSHF, **(ii)** GSHF (assuming return air is mixed with 25% outside air before entering the cooling coil), and **(iii)** the mass of dry air circulated per second. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cooling coil is selected such that its Bypass Factor is 0.20. The mixed air enters at 28 °C and the coil surface is at 8 °C. Determine the **(i)** supply air temperature, **(ii)** the apparatus dew point (ADP), and **(iii)** the total cooling capacity if the air mass flow rate is 4.5 kg/s and the inlet humidity ratio is 0.012 kg/kg. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution (Option B):

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — RSHF (Room Sensible Heat Factor):**

$$
RSHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH} = \frac{25}{25 + 10} = \frac{25}{35} = 0.714
$$

**Step 2 — GSHF (Grand Sensible Heat Factor):**
GSHF accounts for the sensible heat from **outside air** in addition to room loads. The outside air sensible heat (OAS) is the heat brought in by the 25% outside air at 35 °C compared to the room return air at 30 °C. We must compute the mass-weighted DBT of the mixed air.

Approach (simplified KTU standard): Find the **mixed air enthalpy & DBT** when 25% outside air is mixed with 75% return air. With same specific volume assumption, the DBT of mixed air is:

$$
T_{mix} = 0.75 \times 30 + 0.25 \times 35 = 22.5 + 8.75 = 31.25 \text{ °C}
$$

Now compute the **Outside Air Sensible Heat** (OAS) and **Outside Air Latent Heat** (OAL) using the mixed-air condition. The outside-air sensible heat is what the outside air brings *above* the room condition. As a quick KTU-board estimate:

Approximate GSHF considering that 25% outside air adds extra sensible heat but slightly more latent heat (40% outside vs 60% return is drier, so the mix has *less* latent heat than the room). With detailed calculation beyond scope, an acceptable KTU numerical approach uses:

$$
GSHF = \frac{RSH + 0.25 \times \dot{m}_a \, c_{pa} (T_o - T_R)}{RSH + 0.25 \times \dot{m}_a \, c_{pa}(T_o - T_R) + RLH + 0.25 \times \dot{m}_a (h_{fg})(\omega_o - \omega_R)}
$$

Plugging in standard psychrometric values at the given conditions: T_o = 35 °C, T_R = 30 °C, ω_o ≈ 0.0142 (at 35 °C, 40% RH), ω_R ≈ 0.0160 (at 30 °C, 60% RH). For simplicity, a board-level answer:

$$
GSHF \approx 0.71 \text{ to } 0.73 \text{ (slightly different from RSHF)}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** GSHF ≥ RSHF because outside air is typically hotter/dryer. If GSHF > RSHF, the coil selection point on the psychrometric chart shifts.

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate:** If we assume total cooling load = 35 kW and h_drop = 25 kJ/kg d.a. (typical chilled air):

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{35}{25} = 1.4 \text{ kg/s}
$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Supply air temperature T₂:**

$$
BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1} \Rightarrow 0.20 = \frac{T_2 - 28}{8 - 28}
$$

$$
T_2 = 28 + 0.20 \times (8 - 28) = 28 + 0.20 \times (-20) = 28 - 4 = 24 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):** ADP is the temperature of the air when it is in equilibrium with the coil surface, located on the **saturation curve (100% RH line)**. The ADP is the same as the coil surface temperature T₃ = 8 °C *only if* the coil surface condition is itself saturated. For a typical DX/CHW coil, ADP is conventionally taken as the **intersection of the coil saturation line with the process line on the psychrometric chart**, which corresponds to T₃ here.

$$
ADP = T_3 = 8 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3 — Total cooling capacity:**
The apparatus (coil) state at T = 8 °C, saturated: Pₛ = 1.0729 kPa.

$$
\omega_3 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.0729}{101.325 - 1.0729} = 0.622 \times 0.01070 = 0.00666 \text{ kg/kg}
$$

$$
h_3 = 1.005(8) + 0.00666(2500 + 1.88 \times 8) = 8.04 + 0.00666(2515.04) = 8.04 + 16.75 = 24.79 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

The inlet state 1 at T = 28 °C, ω = 0.012:

$$
h_1 = 1.005(28) + 0.012(2500 + 1.88 \times 28) = 28.14 + 0.012(2552.64) = 28.14 + 30.63 = 58.77 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

By BPF, h₂ = h₁ + BPF × (h₃ - h₁) = 58.77 + 0.20 × (24.79 - 58.77) = 58.77 - 6.80 = 51.97 kJ/kg.

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) = 4.5 \times (58.77 - 51.97) = 4.5 \times 6.80 = 30.6 \text{ kW}
$$

> **Result Summary (Part b):** T₂ = 24 °C, ADP = 8 °C, Total cooling capacity = 30.6 kW.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert m³/min to m³/s** (divide by 60) before using the mass flow formula. KTU examiners mark this as a **direct 1-mark deduction**.
> 2. **Using WBT instead of DBT in the h formula.** Enthalpy uses **DBT only**, not WBT.
> 3. **Mixing up RSHF and GSHF.** RSHF = *room* loads only. GSHF includes *outside-air* loads (the 25–30% fresh air load). Many students compute RSHF and write "GSHF" on the answer sheet.
> 4. **In BPF problems, students forget that the apparatus state lies on the 100% RH (saturation) curve.** The coil surface condition is by definition saturated, even if the leaving air is not.
> 5. **Wrong sign convention in SHF:** SHF = SH / (SH + LH) is always a **positive number between 0 and 1**. Some students write SH/LH, which is wrong.
> 6. **Units of ω:** Always quote in **kg water / kg dry air**, not % or kg/m³.

---

### 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Recall Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] Moist air = **Dry air + Water vapour** (binary mixture).
- [ ] **Six master properties:** DBT, WBT, DPT, ω, φ, h. *Any two independent* fix the state.
- [ ] Master formula: $\omega = 0.622 P_v / (P - P_v)$. Remember 0.622 = 18/28.97.
- [ ] Master formula: $h = 1.005\,T + \omega(2500 + 1.88\,T)$ in **kJ/kg d.a.** with T in °C.
- [ ] Master formula: $v = 0.287\,T(1 + 1.6078\,\omega)/P$ in m³/kg d.a. with T in K.
- [ ] **DPT** = the temperature on the saturation curve corresponding to the same ω as the actual air.
- [ ] **WBT** = the temperature reached by adiabatic saturation of the air.
- [ ] **Saturated air:** φ = 100%, DBT = WBT = DPT, no more moisture can be absorbed.
- [ ] **BPF** = (T₂ - T₁)/(T₃ - T₁) = (h₂ - h₁)/(h₃ - h₁). Range: 0 (perfect contact) to 1 (no contact).
- [ ] **SHF = SH/(SH+LH) = SH/TH.** Always between 0 and 1.
- [ ] **RSHF** = Room sensible heat factor (room loads only).
- [ ] **GSHF** = Grand sensible heat factor (room + outside-air loads).
- [ ] **Air washer** can cool, dehumidify, or humidify+heat depending on water temperature.
- [ ] **Sensible heating/cooling:** vertical line on chart (ω constant, h changes by 1.005·ΔT per kg d.a.).
- [ ] **Humidification only:** horizontal-right line (WBT or ω rises, T constant or rises).
- [ ] **Dehumidification only:** horizontal-left line.
- [ ] **Cooling + dehumidification:** process line slopes down-and-left, ends above the saturation curve.
- [ ] **Adiabatic saturation** is the theoretical model that defines WBT. Carrier's empirical equation gives the practical WBT.
- [ ] **Reference state for h:** 0 °C, dry air enthalpy = 0, water liquid enthalpy = 0.
- [ ] **Total pressure** for KTU problems is always **101.325 kPa** unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning Design: Comfort and Industrial Air Conditioning, Comfort Air Conditioning Factors, Effective Temperature, Comfort Charts, Cooling Load Estimation, Design Conditions, Supply Conditions, Fresh Air Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET412 - INDUSTRIAL REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Design: Comfort and Industrial Air Conditioning, Comfort Air Conditioning Factors, Effective Temperature, Comfort Charts, Cooling Load Estimation, Design Conditions, Supply Conditions, Fresh Air Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Air Conditioning (ASHRAE)

**Air Conditioning** is the process of treating air so as to control simultaneously its **temperature, humidity, cleanliness, and distribution** to meet the requirements of the conditioned space and the people occupying it (ASHRAE Standard 55).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes the transition from "refrigeration" to "air conditioning application design." You must distinguish clearly between **Comfort AC** (people-oriented) and **Industrial AC** (process-oriented).

### 1.2 Comfort Air Conditioning (CAC)

Comfort air conditioning deals with the **conditioning of air for human comfort** — i.e., maintaining conditions in a space primarily occupied by people for their thermal well-being, productivity, and health.

**Real-World Analogy — "The Car Cabin Analogy"**
Imagine sitting in a car on a hot Kerala afternoon. You turn on the AC, set the blower, and after a few minutes the cabin feels "just right" — not too cold, not too warm, the windows don't fog. The system isn't just cooling; it is:
- Removing humidity (so you don't feel sticky)
- Filtering dust (cleaner air)
- Circulating air at a mild velocity (so you don't get a chill)
- Replacing exhaled CO₂ with fresh O₂

That entire orchestrated behavior is **Comfort Air Conditioning**.

**Typical applications:** Homes, offices, hospitals, theaters, hotels, shopping malls, schools.

### 1.3 Industrial Air Conditioning (IAC)

Industrial air conditioning is the conditioning of air primarily to **sustain a manufacturing or storage process**, where human comfort is secondary to product/process integrity.

**Real-World Analogy — "The Bakery vs. The Cold Storage"**
- A **bakery** needs a warm, slightly humid environment for dough proofing (process requirement).
- A **cold storage** needs precise low temperatures (-18 °C) to preserve frozen food.
- A **pharmaceutical plant** needs tight humidity control (40–45% RH) for tablet coating.
- A **textile mill** needs controlled humidity (70–80% RH) to prevent yarn breakage.

In each, the "occupant" being served is the **process**, not the person.

**Typical applications:** Textile mills, pharmaceutical plants, paper mills, clean rooms (ISO 14644), server rooms, food processing, cold storage, electronics manufacturing (semiconductor fabs), printing presses, hospitals (operation theatres).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - **CAC** → parameter-of-importance = **human thermal sensation** (Effective Temperature).
> - **IAC** → parameter-of-importance = **process tolerance** (e.g., ±0.5 °C, ±2% RH).

### 1.4 The Six Factors Governing Human Comfort

According to ASHRAE Standard 55, six primary factors affect thermal comfort:

| # | Factor | Symbol/Unit | Description |
|---|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Air Temperature** | T_db (°C or °F) | The dry-bulb temperature of ambient air |
| 2 | **Radiant Temperature** | T_r (°C) | Average temperature of surrounding surfaces |
| 3 | **Air Velocity** | V (m/s or fpm) | Speed of air movement across skin |
| 4 | **Humidity** | RH (%) / W (kg/kg d.a.) | Moisture content of air |
| 5 | **Metabolic Rate** | Met (unit) | Heat generated by occupant's activity (1 Met = 58.2 W/m²) |
| 6 | **Clothing Insulation** | Clo (unit) | Thermal resistance of clothing (1 Clo = 0.155 m²·K/W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A comfort AC system must control **at least 4 of the 6 factors** simultaneously to be effective. Just dropping air temperature without addressing humidity leaves occupants feeling "cold yet clammy."

### 1.5 Effective Temperature (ET) — Definition

**Effective Temperature (ET)** is an empirically derived **single index** that combines the effects of air **temperature, humidity, and air velocity** into a single number representing the **equivalent sensation of warmth** experienced by an average sedentary person wearing standard indoor clothing.

- ET was developed by **Houghton & Yaglou (1923)** at the ASHVE (predecessor of ASHRAE) research lab at Pittsburgh.
- It is plotted on the **ASHRAE Comfort Chart** as a family of curves on the psychrometric chart.
- The numerical value of ET equals the temperature of *still, saturated air* that would produce the same sensation.

> [!NOTE]
> **ET ≠ DBT.** Two rooms at 27 °C DBT can have different ET values depending on RH and air movement.
</br>
> Example: 27 °C DBT at 90% RH (stuffy) feels warmer than 27 °C DBT at 30% RH (dry), so the **ET of the first is higher**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)

The original ET had a flaw: it was based on subjects at rest, fully clothed, and exposed to air velocities of 15–250 fpm with skin wetted for evaporation. In real conditions, **MRT (Mean Radiant Temperature)** and **air motion** independently affect sensation.

**Corrected Effective Temperature (CET)** incorporates **Mean Radiant Temperature (MRT)** as a separate parameter, making it a more accurate index.

$$\text{CET} = f(T_{db},\, T_{wb},\, \bar{T}_r,\, V)$$

where $\bar{T}_r$ is the **Mean Radiant Temperature** of the surrounding surfaces.

**Why CET Matters:**
- In a room with large **glass facades** (e.g., KTU auditorium in afternoon sun), the wall temperature can be 45 °C while DBT is 28 °C. ET alone underestimates the heat stress.
- CET accounts for this **radiant asymmetry**.

### 2.2 Thermal Comfort Zone

The **Comfort Zone** on the ASHRAE chart is a region where at least **80% of sedentary occupants** (performing light office work, ~1.0–1.2 Met) find the environment thermally acceptable.

**Recommended Indoor Design Conditions (ASHRAE 55 — Summer):**
- Operative Temperature (T_o): **22.5 – 26 °C**
- RH range: **30 – 60%** (preferably below 60% for microbial control)
- Air velocity: **≤ 0.25 m/s** (50 fpm) for still air; up to 0.8 m/s in elevated air movement

$$\text{Operative Temperature:}\quad T_o = \frac{T_{db} + \bar{T}_r}{2} \quad \text{(for } V \le 0.2 \text{ m/s)}$$

### 2.3 Classification of Cooling Loads

Cooling load is the **rate at which heat must be removed** from a space to maintain constant indoor design conditions. It has two broad categories:

**A. Space Heat Gains (Sensible):**
1. Heat gain through **walls, roof, partitions** (conduction)
2. Heat gain through **glass** (conduction + solar radiation)
3. Heat gain due to **infiltration** of outdoor air
4. Heat gain due to **ventilation** air (fresh air)
5. Heat gain from **people** (sensible)
6. Heat gain from **lights**
7. Heat gain from **appliances/equipment** (sensible)
8. Heat gain from **miscellaneous** (pipes, doors)

**B. Latent Heat Gains:**
1. Heat gain from **people** (respiration + perspiration)
2. Heat gain from **infiltration/ventilation** (latent)
3. Heat gain from **equipment** (e.g., gas stoves, steam)
4. Heat gain from **processes** (industrial)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 4)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are mathematically verifiable and aligned with KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) pattern.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Heat gain through opaque surface (walls/roof) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}$ | W or kW | $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $A$ = area (m²), CLTD = Cooling Load Temperature Difference (°C) |
| 2 | CLTD correction for sol-air | $\text{CLTD}_{\text{corr}} = (T_{sa} - T_{in}) - \delta_T$ | °C | Accounts for time lag & decrement |
| 3 | Heat gain through glass (conduction) | $Q_g = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \text{CLTD}_g$ | W | $U_g$ for glass ≈ 5.3–5.8 W/m²K |
| 4 | Solar heat gain through glass | $Q_s = A \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I$ | W | SHGC = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient |
| 5 | Heat from occupants (sensible) | $Q_{ps} = n \cdot q_s$ | W | $n$ = number of people, $q_s$ from table (W/person) |
| 6 | Heat from occupants (latent) | $Q_{pl} = n \cdot q_l$ | W | $q_l$ from table |
| 7 | Heat from lights | $Q_L = W_L \cdot F_u \cdot F_{sa}$ | W | $W_L$ = lamp wattage, $F_u$ = usage factor, $F_{sa}$ = special allowance factor |
| 8 | Heat from equipment | $Q_E = P_{\text{input}} \cdot F_u \cdot F_R$ | W | $F_R$ = radiation factor |
| 9 | Infiltration/ventilation load (sensible) | $Q_{vs} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_o - T_i)$ | W | $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow (m³/s) |
| 10 | Infiltration/ventilation load (latent) | $Q_{vl} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_a \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_o - W_i)$ | W | $h_{fg}$ ≈ 2500 kJ/kg |
| 11 | Total Sensible Heat (TSH) | $\text{TSH} = \sum Q_{sensible}$ | W | All sensible heat gains summed |
| 12 | Total Latent Heat (TLH) | $\text{TLH} = \sum Q_{latent}$ | W | All latent heat gains summed |
| 13 | **Grand Total Heat (GTH)** | $\text{GTH} = \text{TSH} + \text{TLH}$ | W | Total cooling load on coil |
| 14 | **Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF)** | $\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RHL}}$ | — | RSH = room sensible heat, RHL = room latent heat |
| 15 | **Effective Sensible Heat Factor (ESHF)** | $\text{ESHF} = \dfrac{\text{TSH} - 0.68 \cdot \text{TLH}}{\text{TSH} + 0.2 \cdot \text{TLH}}$ | — | For by-pass factor calculations |
| 16 | **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** | From psychrometric chart using RSHF line | °C | Intersection of RSHF line with saturation curve |
| 17 | **Bypass Factor (BF)** | $\text{BF} = \dfrac{T_{db,i} - T_{db,ADP}}{T_{db,o} - T_{db,ADP}}$ | — | Coil performance parameter |
| 18 | Air quantity from sensible load | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{1.2 \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s)}$ | m³/s | $c_p$ = 1.005 kJ/kgK, 1.2 = density of air |
| 19 | CFM form (US units) | $\text{CFM} = \dfrac{\text{SHL (BTU/h)}}{1.08 \cdot \Delta T (°F)}$ | cfm | SHL = sensible heat load |
| 20 | Fresh air (per person) | $\dot{V}_{fa} = R_p \cdot N$ | m³/h | $R_p$ = ASHRAE 62.1 rate |
| 21 | Ventilation CFM | $\text{CFM} = \dfrac{\text{RSH} + \text{RLH}}{4.5 \cdot \Delta h}$ | cfm | Using enthalpy difference |
| 22 | Mixed air temperature | $T_{ma} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_R \cdot T_R + \dot{V}_O \cdot T_O}{\dot{V}_R + \dot{V}_O}$ | °C | Recirculated (R) + outdoor (O) air |

### 2.5 Design Conditions for India (ISHRAE Data)

**Outdoor Design Conditions for Major Indian Cities (Summer — 0.4% basis):**

| City | DBT (°C) | WBT (°C) | DB Range (°C) | MCDB (°C) |
|------|----------|----------|---------------|-----------|
| Chennai | 36.7 | 27.9 | 30.5 – 38.5 | 33.5 |
| Mumbai | 33.5 | 28.5 | 27.5 – 36.0 | 32.0 |
| Delhi | 39.6 | 26.0 | 33.0 – 43.0 | 38.0 |
| Kolkata | 35.9 | 28.7 | 29.5 – 38.0 | 33.5 |
| Bengaluru | 33.0 | 22.5 | 27.0 – 35.5 | 30.0 |
| Thiruvananthapuram | 32.0 | 26.5 | 27.5 – 34.0 | 30.0 |
| Kochi | 33.0 | 27.0 | 27.5 – 35.0 | 31.0 |

**Indoor Design (Comfort):**
- DBT: **25 ± 1 °C**
- RH: **50 ± 5%**
- Air velocity: **≤ 0.5 m/s**

### 2.6 Supply Air Conditions

Supply air leaves the cooling coil and is delivered into the room. It must:
- Be **cold enough** to absorb the room's sensible heat.
- Be **dry enough** to absorb the room's latent heat.
- Not cause **drafts** (i.e., supply air velocity < 0.5 m/s at occupant level).

$$\text{Supply air temperature:}\quad T_s = T_i - \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical supply air temperature in comfort systems:** 12–15 °C (i.e., $\Delta T = T_i - T_s \approx 10$–13 °C).
> **For close-control industrial systems:** 8–10 °C supply air.
</br>
> **Engineer's Rule-of-Thumb:** Supply air $\Delta T$ should not exceed 10 °C to avoid drafts in comfort applications.

### 2.7 Fresh Air (Ventilation) Requirements — ASHRAE 62.1

The **minimum outdoor air flow rate** must satisfy both:
- **Breathing zone outdoor airflow ($V_{bz}$):**
$$V_{bz} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$$
where $R_p$ = outdoor air rate per person (L/s·person), $P_z$ = number of occupants, $R_a$ = outdoor air rate per unit area (L/s·m²), $A_z$ = zone area.

- **System outdoor air flow ($V_{ot}$):**
$$V_{ot} = \dfrac{V_{bz}}{E_v} = \dfrac{V_{bz}}{1 - X_s}$$
where $E_v$ = ventilation efficiency, $X_s$ = outdoor air fraction in supply.

| Space Type | $R_p$ (L/s·person) | $R_a$ (L/s·m²) |
|------------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Office | 2.5 | 0.3 |
| Classroom | 5.0 | 0.6 |
| Conference room | 2.5 | 0.3 |
| Hospital patient room | 2.5 | 0.5 |
| Restaurant dining | 3.5 | 0.5 |
| Auditorium (seated) | 2.5 | 0.3 |

**KTU Practical Note:** For Kerala's hot, humid climate, ISHRAE recommends **15–20 cfm per person** (≈ 25–34 m³/h per person) of outdoor air in offices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Conduction Heat Gain Through a Wall (CLTD Method)

**Starting from Fourier's Law of Conduction:**

$$Q = \dfrac{kA(T_o - T_i)}{x}$$

where $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $x$ = thickness (m), $A$ = area (m²).

**Step 1 — Combine convective + conductive resistance into U-value:**

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$$

$$U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

where $h_i$ = inside film coefficient (≈ 9.3 W/m²K for still air), $h_o$ = outside film coefficient (≈ 18–23 W/m²K depending on wind).

**Step 2 — Express in terms of air temperatures using Sol-Air Temperature:**

The sol-air temperature ($T_{sa}$) accounts for solar radiation absorbed by the outer surface:

$$T_{sa} = T_{ao} + \dfrac{\alpha_s \cdot I_t}{h_o}$$

where $\alpha_s$ = absorptivity, $I_t$ = total solar radiation (W/m²) incident on surface.

**Step 3 — Combine the time-lag effect using CLTD:**

The actual cooling load on the inside surface lags behind the outdoor temperature peak (due to thermal mass of the wall). ASHRAE tabulates this as **CLTD (Cooling Load Temperature Difference):**

$$\text{CLTD} = T_{sa} - T_i - \delta_T$$

where $\delta_T$ accounts for time lag, decrement, and indoor-outside temperature swing.

**Step 4 — Final conduction load equation:**

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{wall}} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_{\text{corr}}}$$

**KTU Note:** Always include the **room-side CLTD correction** by adding the indoor–outdoor mean temperature difference.

### 3.2 Derivation of Bypass Factor (BF) and ADP

**Consider an air-handling unit (AHU) cooling coil:**

Let:
- 1 = air entering coil (mixed air: $T_{db,1}$, $T_{wb,1}$)
- 2 = air leaving coil (supply air: $T_{db,2}$)
- 3 = air at the apparatus dew point (ADP, $T_{db,3} = T_{wb,3}$)

A fraction of the entering air **bypasses** the cooling surfaces (mixes directly with the cooled air stream).

**Definition of Bypass Factor:**

$$\boxed{\text{BF} = \dfrac{T_{db,2} - T_{db,3}}{T_{db,1} - T_{db,3}}}$$

**Derivation from Mass/Energy Balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_a \cdot c_p (T_{db,1} - T_{db,2}) &= \dot{m}_a (1 - \text{BF}) \cdot c_p (T_{db,1} - T_{db,3}) \\
T_{db,1} - T_{db,2} &= (1 - \text{BF}) (T_{db,1} - T_{db,3}) \\
1 - \dfrac{T_{db,1} - T_{db,2}}{T_{db,1} - T_{db,3}} &= \text{BF} \\
\therefore \quad \text{BF} &= \dfrac{T_{db,2} - T_{db,3}}{T_{db,1} - T_{db,3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance:**
- BF = 0 → ideal coil, air leaves at ADP.
- BF = 1 → no cooling, air passes through unchanged.
- Typical BF for commercial coil = **0.10 – 0.30** (good coil = low BF).

### 3.3 Derivation of Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF) and ADP

**RSHF is a thermodynamic design parameter:**

$$\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RSH} + \text{RHL}}$$

**Graphical procedure to find ADP:**

1. Plot room conditions ($T_{db,i}$, $T_{wb,i}$) on psychrometric chart.
2. Compute RSHF.
3. Draw a line through the room condition point with slope = RSHF/(1−RSHF) on enthalpy–humidity axes.
4. Extend the line to the **saturation curve** (100% RH).
5. The intersection is the **ADP**.

**Analytical Form (Approximate):**

The RSHF line equation, with enthalpy (h) on the y-axis and humidity ratio (W) on the x-axis:

$$\dfrac{h - h_i}{W - W_i} = \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{\text{RHL}} = \dfrac{1 - \text{RSHF}}{\text{RSHF}}$$

Setting $W = W_s(T_{ADP})$ (saturation) gives the ADP by solving:

$$h_{ADP} = h_i + \dfrac{1 - \text{RSHF}}{\text{RSHF}} (W_{ADP} - W_i)$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Air Quantity from Sensible Heat Load

The sensible heat load of the room must be absorbed by the supply air stream as it warms from $T_s$ (supply) to $T_i$ (room):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RSH} &= \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_i - T_s) \\
\dot{m}_a &= \dfrac{\text{RSH}}{c_p (T_i - T_s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For moist air at standard conditions, $\rho_a \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $c_p \approx 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$:

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \dfrac{\text{RSH (kW)}}{1.2 \times 1.005 \times \Delta T}$$

In US units (SHL in BTU/h, $\Delta T$ in °F):

$$\boxed{\text{CFM} = \dfrac{\text{SHL (BTU/h)}}{1.08 \times \Delta T (°F)}}$$

The constant **1.08** in US units = $\rho_a \cdot c_p \cdot 60 = 0.075 \times 0.24 \times 60 = 1.08$.

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-style 14-Mark Problem Setup)

**Problem:** A 12 m × 8 m × 3.5 m office in Thiruvananthapuram has 20 occupants, 12 fluorescent tube lights (each 40 W), and 2 personal computers (200 W each). Indoor conditions: 25 °C DBT, 50% RH. Outdoor: 32 °C DBT, 26.5 °C WBT. Infiltration = 0.5 air changes/h. Estimate cooling load.

**Given Data:**
- $n = 20$ people, $W_L = 12 \times 40 = 480$ W, $W_E = 2 \times 200 = 400$ W
- Volume = $12 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 336 \text{ m}^3$
- $\rho_a = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $h_{fg} = 2500 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 1 — From Psychrometric Chart:**

Outdoor: $T_{db} = 32\text{ °C}, T_{wb} = 26.5\text{ °C} \rightarrow W_o = 0.0165 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}$
Indoor: $T_{db} = 25\text{ °C}, \phi = 50\% \rightarrow W_i = 0.0099 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}$

**Step 2 — Occupant Load (Office work, 1.2 Met):**

From ASHRAE table, per person (DBT ≈ 25 °C): $q_s = 60$ W, $q_l = 55$ W

$$Q_{ps} = 20 \times 60 = 1200 \text{ W (sensible)}$$

$$Q_{pl} = 20 \times 55 = 1100 \text{ W (latent)}$$

**Step 3 — Lighting Load (fluorescent, $F_u = 1.0$, $F_{sa} = 1.2$ for ceiling return):**

$$Q_L = 480 \times 1.0 \times 1.2 = 576 \text{ W (sensible)}$$

**Step 4 — Equipment Load (computers, $F_R = 1.0$ since all convective):**

$$Q_E = 400 \times 1.0 = 400 \text{ W (sensible)}$$

**Step 5 — Infiltration Load:**

Volumetric flow = $0.5 \times 336 = 168 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.0467 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

$$\dot{m}_a = 0.0467 \times 1.2 = 0.056 \text{ kg/s}$$

Sensible:
$$Q_{is} = 0.056 \times 1.005 \times (32 - 25) = 0.394 \text{ kW} = 394 \text{ W}$$

Latent:
$$Q_{il} = 0.056 \times 2500 \times (0.0165 - 0.0099) = 0.0924 \text{ kW} = 92 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Total Cooling Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TSH} &= 1200 + 576 + 400 + 394 = 2570 \text{ W} = 2.57 \text{ kW} \\
\text{TLH} &= 1100 + 92 = 1192 \text{ W} = 1.19 \text{ kW} \\
\text{GTH} &= 2.57 + 1.19 = 3.76 \text{ kW} = 3.24 \text{ TR}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Recall: 1 TR = 3.517 kW)

**Step 7 — Sensible Heat Factor:**

$$\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{2.57}{3.76} = 0.683$$

**Step 8 — Air Quantity (using $\Delta T = 10$ °C):**

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{2570}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 10} = 0.213 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 766 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 451 \text{ CFM}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** In 14-mark problems, you MUST show all 8 steps with units, justify assumptions, and present results in **TR (Tons of Refrigeration)** — the unit KTU examiners prefer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Air Conditioning Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: AC Design Problem] --> B[Define Indoor Design Conditions]
    B --> B1[DBT: 25 C, RH: 50%]
    B --> B2[Use ASHRAE 55 / ISHRAE data]
    B --> C[Select Outdoor Design Conditions]
    C --> C1[From ISHRAE tables for city]
    C --> C2[0.4 percent basis summer]
    C --> D[Compute Heat Gains]
    D --> D1[Walls, Roof: U x A x CLTD]
    D --> D2[Glass: Conduction + Solar SHGC]
    D --> D3[Occupants, Lights, Equipment]
    D --> D4[Infiltration and Ventilation]
    D --> E[Determine Total Loads]
    E --> E1[TSH, TLH, GTH in kW or TR]
    E --> E2[Compute RSHF]
    E --> F[Select Coil and Determine ADP]
    F --> F1[Use RSHF line on psychrometric chart]
    F --> F2[Assume coil BF approx 0.10 to 0.20]
    F --> G[Calculate Air Quantity]
    G --> G1[From RSH and Delta T of 10 to 12 C]
    G --> H[Select Supply Conditions]
    H --> H1[Supply DBT, WBT, CFM]
    H --> I[Verify Draft and Nozzle Selection]
    I --> I1[ASHRAE 55 velocity check]
    I --> J[Final Design Summary]
    J --> J1[TR, CFM, ESP, duct sizes]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Psychrometric Process Path on a Cooling Coil

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Outdoor Air<br>32 C DBT, 26.5 C WBT] -->|Mix| M[Mixed Air Plenum]
    R1[Return Air<br>25 C DBT, 50 percent RH] -->|Mix| M
    M -->|MADP| C[Cooling Coil<br>BF 0.15, ADP 9.5 C]
    C -->|Supply| S[Supply Air<br>approx 13 C DBT]
    S -->|Room| RO[Room Conditions<br>25 C DBT, 50 percent RH]
    RO -->|Return| R1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Heat Gain Component Hierarchy (Cooling Load Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOT[Total Cooling Load GTH] --> TSH[Total Sensible Heat TSH]
    TOT --> TLH[Total Latent Heat TLH]
    TSH --> S1[Wall and Partition Conduction]
    TSH --> S2[Roof and Ceiling Conduction]
    TSH --> S3[Glass Conduction and Solar]
    TSH --> S4[Infiltration Sensible]
    TSH --> S5[Ventilation Sensible]
    TSH --> S6[People Sensible 60 W per person]
    TSH --> S7[Lights W L x F u x F sa]
    TSH --> S8[Equipment Convective and Radiant]
    TLH --> L1[People Latent 55 to 200 W per person]
    TLH --> L2[Infiltration Latent]
    TLH --> L3[Ventilation Latent]
    TLH --> L4[Process or Gas Equipment]
    S1 -.-> S1a[U x A x CLTD corr]
    S3 -.-> S3a[U g x A g x CLTD g + SHGC x A x I]
    S5 -.-> S5a[m dot x c p x Delta T]
    S7 -.-> S7a[F sa 1.0 to 1.2 for fluorescent]
    S8 -.-> S8a[P input x F u x F R]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Process Diagram — Effective Temperature and CET Construction Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inputs] --> B[DBT, WBT, MRT, Air Velocity V]
    B --> C[Apply Houghton Yaglou Charts 1923]
    C --> D[Effective Temperature ET]
    B --> E[Apply Missenard Correction 1948]
    E --> F[Corrected Effective Temperature CET]
    D --> G[ASHRAE Comfort Chart 1980s onward]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Identify Comfort Zone]
    H --> I[Operative Temperature 22.5 to 26 C]
    I --> J[Air Velocity not greater than 0.25 m per s]
    I --> K[RH 30 to 60 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Interpretation Note (KTU-Adapted):** Mermaid is used here because rendering the original ASHRAE Comfort Chart requires proprietary data. The block flow represents the **logical mapping** of all physical and thermodynamic stages from problem statement to final AC design.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define Effective Temperature. How does it differ from Dry Bulb Temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Effective Temperature (ET)* is an empirical index that combines the effects of **air temperature, humidity, and air velocity** into a single numerical value representing the equivalent sensation of warmth experienced by an average sedentary person.

- **ET = DBT** only when air is *saturated and still* (by definition).
- **ET > DBT** when air is humid and still (e.g., 30 °C DBT at 80% RH has ET ≈ 32 °C).
- **ET < DBT** when air is moving (e.g., 30 °C DBT with 200 fpm velocity has lower ET).

(3 marks — definition + comparison with 2 distinguishing points)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List the six factors affecting human thermal comfort as per ASHRAE Standard 55.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six factors are:
1. Air **temperature** (DBT)
2. **Radiant** temperature (MRT)
3. Air **velocity**
4. **Humidity** (RH / W)
5. **Metabolic** rate (Met)
6. **Clothing** insulation (Clo)

(3 marks — 0.5 marks per factor; 6 factors × 0.5 = 3 marks)

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Module 4)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comfort AC + Load Estimation

**(a) [7 marks]** A 15 m × 10 m × 3.5 m conference room in Kochi (Outdoor: 33 °C DBT, 27 °C WBT) needs comfort AC. Indoor: 25 °C DBT, 50% RH. Occupants: 40 (office work), 30 fluorescent tubes (40 W each, $F_{sa} = 1.2$), 4 ceiling fans (75 W each). Infiltration: 1.5 air changes/h. Compute: (i) RSHF, (ii) ADP (use psychrometric chart), (iii) CFM of supply air for $\Delta T = 10$ °C.

**(b) [7 marks]** Distinguish between **Comfort AC and Industrial AC** with at least four parameters. Provide two real-world examples of Industrial AC with their process tolerance specifications.

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Load on Occupants:**
$q_s = 60$ W, $q_l = 55$ W (office, 1.2 Met)
$Q_{ps} = 40 \times 60 = 2400$ W; $Q_{pl} = 40 \times 55 = 2200$ W

**Step 2 — Load on Lights:**
$Q_L = 30 \times 40 \times 1.0 \times 1.2 = 1440$ W (sensible only)

**Step 3 — Load on Fans (assume all heat goes to room):**
$Q_F = 4 \times 75 = 300$ W (sensible)

**Step 4 — Infiltration Load:**

Volume $= 15 \times 10 \times 3.5 = 525 \text{ m}^3$
$\dot{V} = 1.5 \times 525 = 787.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.2188 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

From psychrometric chart:
- Outdoor (33/27): $W_o = 0.0180$ kg/kg d.a.
- Indoor (25/50%): $W_i = 0.0099$ kg/kg d.a.

Sensible infiltration:
$Q_{is} = 0.2188 \times 1.2 \times 1.005 \times (33 - 25) = 2.111$ kW = **2111 W**

Latent infiltration:
$Q_{il} = 0.2188 \times 1.2 \times 2500 \times (0.0180 - 0.0099) = 5.317$ kW = **5317 W**

**Step 5 — Totals:**

$\text{TSH} = 2400 + 1440 + 300 + 2111 = 6251$ W = 6.251 kW
$\text{TLH} = 2200 + 5317 = 7517$ W = 7.517 kW
$\text{GTH} = 13.768$ kW = **3.91 TR**

**Step 6 — RSHF:**

$$\text{RSHF} = \dfrac{6.251}{13.768} = 0.454$$

**Step 7 — ADP (from psychrometric chart using RSHF line from room point):**
Plot (25, 50% RH) = room point. Draw RSHF line with slope 0.454/(1−0.454) ≈ 0.83 in h–W coordinates. Extend to saturation curve → **ADP ≈ 9.5 °C**.

**Step 8 — CFM:**

$$\text{CFM} = \dfrac{6251 \text{ W} \times 3.412 \text{ BTU/W}}{1.08 \times 10 \text{ °F}} = 1975 \text{ CFM} \approx 1980 \text{ CFM}$$

(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7)

---

**Model Answer for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Comfort AC | Industrial AC |
|-----------|------------|----------------|
| **Primary objective** | Human thermal comfort | Process/Product integrity |
| **Temperature control** | Wide tolerance (±2 °C around setpoint) | Tight tolerance (±0.1 to ±0.5 °C) |
| **Humidity control** | ±5% RH acceptable | ±2% RH or tighter |
| **Cleanliness** | Standard filtration | HEPA / ULPA, ISO Class 5–8 |
| **Air velocity** | Limited to 0.5 m/s to avoid drafts | Often high for process |
| **Load profile** | Predictable (occupancy-driven) | Variable, machine-driven |
| **Examples** | Offices, malls, hospitals | Pharma, semiconductor, food |

**Two Industrial AC Examples:**

1. **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing (Tablet Coating):** Requires $22 \pm 1$ °C, $45 \pm 2%$ RH, ISO 7 cleanroom.
2. **Cold Storage for Vaccines:** Requires $2 - 8$ °C with $\pm 0.5$ °C tolerance, with separate humidity control for paracetamol.

(3 marks for comparison table — 6 points × 0.5 each; 2 marks for each example; 1 mark for tolerance specification)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Industrial AC + Ventilation

**(a) [7 marks]** A pharmaceutical plant producing tablets requires ISO 7 cleanroom conditions. Room size: 20 m × 15 m × 4 m. Indoor: 22 °C DBT, 45% RH. Outdoor: 36 °C DBT, 27 °C WBT. Compute: (i) Sensible heat gain from 8 operators (light work, $q_s = 70$ W, $q_l = 90$ W), (ii) Equipment load: 6 machines of 1.5 kW each ($F_u = 0.8$, $F_R = 0.6$), (iii) Lighting: 60 LED fixtures of 50 W each ($F_{sa} = 1.0$), (iv) Fresh air supply per ASHRAE 62.1 for ISO 7.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **CLTD method** of cooling load estimation. How does it differ from the **TFM (Total Equivalent Temperature Difference / Time Averaging)** method?

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Operators:**
$Q_{ps} = 8 \times 70 = 560$ W
$Q_{pl} = 8 \times 90 = 720$ W

**Step 2 — Equipment:**
$Q_E = 6 \times 1500 \times 0.8 \times 0.6 = 4320$ W (sensible, of which only 60% becomes convective; 40% is radiant, handled via F_sa)

**Step 3 — Lighting (LED):**
$Q_L = 60 \times 50 \times 1.0 = 3000$ W (sensible)

**Step 4 — Fresh Air (ISO 7 = Class 10000):**

ASHRAE 62.1 for cleanroom (high-occupancy manufacturing):
- $R_p = 3.5$ L/s per person, $R_a = 1.0$ L/s per m²

$$V_{bz} = 3.5 \times 8 + 1.0 \times (20 \times 15) = 28 + 300 = 328 \text{ L/s} = 1181 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Ventilation Load:**

From psychrometric chart:
- Outdoor (36/27): $W_o = 0.0165$ kg/kg d.a., $h_o = 79$ kJ/kg
- Indoor (22/45%): $W_i = 0.0073$ kg/kg d.a., $h_i = 41$ kJ/kg

$\dot{V} = 328 \text{ L/s} = 0.328 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
$\dot{m}_a = 0.328 \times 1.2 = 0.3936$ kg/s

Sensible:
$Q_{vs} = 0.3936 \times 1.005 \times (36 - 22) = 5.535$ kW

Latent:
$Q_{vl} = 0.3936 \times 2500 \times (0.0165 - 0.0073) = 9.054$ kW

**Step 6 — Totals (excluding conduction for simplicity here):**

$\text{TSH} = 560 + 4320 + 3000 + 5535 = 13415$ W = 13.42 kW
$\text{TLH} = 720 + 9054 = 9774$ W = 9.77 kW
$\text{GTH} = 23.19$ kW ≈ **6.6 TR**

(Marks: 1+1+1+1+2+1 = 7)

---

**Model Answer for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**CLTD Method:**
- Uses tabulated **Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD)** values from ASHRAE fundamentals.
- Equation: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_{\text{corr}}$.
- **Advantage:** Simple, quick, requires only wall orientation, construction, and time of day.
- **Limitation:** Static, doesn't account for dynamic thermal storage variations accurately.

**TFM (TETD/TA) Method:**
- Uses **Total Equivalent Temperature Difference (TETD)** and **Time-Averaging (TA)** factors.
- Considers **thermal storage** of construction, time lag, and decrement explicitly.
- More accurate for **heavy construction** with high thermal mass.
- Used in ASHRAE **CLTD/SCL/CLF** method of 1970s–2000s.

**Key Differences (3 marks for table, 4 marks for explanation):**

| Aspect | CLTD | TFM |
|--------|------|-----|
| **Inputs** | Wall construction + U + sol-air | TETD, TA, CLF tables |
| **Account for thermal mass** | Indirectly via tabulated CLTD | Explicitly via TA factors |
| **Accuracy** | ±15–20% | ±10–15% |
| **Best suited for** | Light construction | Heavy construction |
| **Current status** | Still used in basic courses | Supplanted by Radiant Time Series (RTS) |

(2 marks for both methods explained with 4 key differences)

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 Cooling Load Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert TR ↔ kW** — KTU expects 1 TR = 3.517 kW. Students often write answers in kW without converting to TR. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Omitting the Solar Allowance Factor ($F_{sa}$)** for lights — when fixtures are installed in the ceiling plenum vs. inside the room, $F_{sa}$ varies from 1.0 (inside) to 0.4 (fully ducted). Default = 1.2 for fluorescent ceiling-return.
> 3. **Mixing RSHF and ESHF** — RSHF uses room loads only; ESHF uses **coil loads** (which include return-air fan heat and duct heat gains, often subtracting 0.68 × RHL from TLH to account for bypass-factor moisture handling).
> 4. **Not using psychrometric chart for WBT/W pairs** — students guess $W$ values; KTU requires reading from the chart, with $+/- 0.0005$ kg/kg tolerance.
> 5. **Misinterpreting BF direction** — BF is the *fraction of air that escapes cooling*, not the *fraction cooled*. Many students invert the ratio in ADP/BF problems.
> 6. **No margin/safety factor** — Professional practice adds 5–10% safety; KTU doesn't explicitly demand it, but stating this shows professional maturity (extra credit consideration).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4:**

**1. Core Definitions**
- Air Conditioning = Control of **T, $\phi$, cleanliness, distribution**
- Comfort AC = **People** centered; Industrial AC = **Process** centered
- ET = Combined index of $T_{db}, T_{wb}, V$; CET adds MRT
- Comfort Zone: $T_o$ = 22.5–26 °C, RH = 30–60%, $V \le 0.25$ m/s

**2. Six Comfort Factors**
- $T_{db}, T_r, V, \phi, \text{Met}, \text{Clo}$ — memorise the order

**3. Cooling Load Components**
- **Sensible:** Walls, Roof, Glass, Infiltration (sens.), Ventilation (sens.), People (sens.), Lights, Equipment
- **Latent:** People, Infiltration (lat.), Ventilation (lat.), Process

**4. Key Formulas (must memorize)**
- $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}$ (conduction)
- $\text{RSHF} = \text{RSH} / (\text{RSH} + \text{RHL})$
- $\text{BF} = (T_2 - T_3)/(T_1 - T_3)$
- $\text{CFM} = \text{SHL} / (1.08 \times \Delta T)$
- $V_{bz} = R_p \cdot P_z + R_a \cdot A_z$

**5. Design Conditions (India)**
- Always use **ISHRAE 0.4% basis** for outdoor
- Indoor comfort: **25 ± 1 °C, 50 ± 5% RH**

**6. Supply Conditions**
- $\Delta T = T_i - T_s \approx 10$–13 °C for comfort
- Avoid drafts: supply velocity < 0.5 m/s at occupant level

**7. Fresh Air (ASHRAE 62.1)**
- Office: 2.5 L/s per person + 0.3 L/s·m²
- Classrooms: 5.0 L/s per person + 0.6 L/s·m²
- Always compute **$V_{bz}$ and $V_{ot}$** separately

**8. Unit Conversions to Memorize**
- 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12000 BTU/h
- 1 m/s = 196.85 fpm
- $c_p$(air) = 1.005 kJ/kg·K; $\rho_a$ = 1.2 kg/m³
- $h_{fg}$(water) = 2500 kJ/kg at ~25 °C

**9. Typical Numerical Answers to Cross-Check**
- Office: 60–80 W/m² sensible, 30–50 W/m² total
- CFM/TR ≈ 400 CFM/TR for comfort (rule of thumb)
- BF of new coil = 0.10–0.20

**10. Common Pitfalls (Re-emphasized)**
- Conduction vs. Convection: U is for steady conduction; CLTD includes radiation.
- People load changes with DBT — use the correct ASHRAE table for indoor DBT.
- Don't forget $F_{sa}$ for lights; don't forget $F_R$ for motors.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is an Air Conditioning System?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (ASHRAE Aligned):**
> An **Air Conditioning System** is an assembly of components (refrigeration cycle hardware, air-handling units, distribution networks, and control devices) designed simultaneously to fulfill **four primary functions** on a conditioned space:
> 1. **Temperature Control** (Heating + Cooling)
> 2. **Humidity Control** (Humidification + Dehumidification)
> 3. **Air Movement & Circulation** (Ventilation + Distribution)
> 4. **Air Cleansing** (Filtration + Purification)

**In simple terms:** Think of an AC system as a *climate chef*. Just like a chef controls five tastes (sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami) to make a perfect dish, an AC system controls temperature, humidity, air purity, and air motion to make the *perfect indoor climate*.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Air Conditioning Systems (KTU Syllabus Overview)

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER TAXONOMY — How KTU classifies AC Systems:**

| Category | Sub-Type | Typical Capacity | Application |
|----------|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Unitary Systems** | Room AC (Window) | 0.75 – 2 TR | Bedrooms, small offices |
| **Unitary Systems** | Split AC | 0.75 – 5 TR | Homes, retail shops |
| **Unitary Systems** | Packaged AC (PTAC/Roof-top) | 3 – 30 TR | Restaurants, server rooms |
| **Central Systems** | All-Air System | 5 – 500+ TR | Theaters, malls, airports |
| **Central Systems** | Air-Water System | 10 – 500+ TR | Hotels, hospitals |
| **Central Systems** | Chilled Water System | 50 – 5000+ TR | Industrial, commercial complexes |

Where **1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW** = Heat removal rate equivalent to melting **1 ton (907.185 kg)** of ice in 24 hours.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine your house is a **fish aquarium** 🐠. The fish need:
- Clean, oxygen-rich water at the right temperature
- Constant circulation (filter = duct fan)
- Periodic water changes (fresh air = ventilation)
- Heater in winter, chiller in summer

An **Air Conditioning system is the aquarium maintenance crew** for your building — constantly monitoring and adjusting the "climate soup" (air) to keep occupants comfortable and processes running smoothly.

---

## 1.4 Central Concept: The Psychrometric Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every AC system — from a 1 TR window AC to a 1000 TR chiller plant — operates on the **Psychrometric Chart**. The four fundamental processes are:
> - **Sensible Heating** (Temperature ↑, Humidity Ratio constant)
> - **Sensible Cooling** (Temperature ↓, Humidity Ratio constant)
> - **Humidification** (Moisture ↑)
> - **Dehumidification** (Moisture ↓)

**Two governing equations every KTU student must memorize:**

$$W = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v} \quad \text{(Humidity Ratio)}$$

$$h = 1.005\,T + W\,(2501 + 1.88\,T) \quad \text{(kJ/kg dry air)}$$

Where:
- $W$ = Humidity ratio (kg water vapor / kg dry air)
- $P_v$ = Partial pressure of water vapor (kPa)
- $P_t$ = Total pressure (typically 101.325 kPa)
- $h$ = Enthalpy of moist air (kJ/kg da)
- $T$ = Dry-bulb temperature (°C)
- **2501 kJ/kg** = Latent heat of vaporization at 0°C
- **1.005 kJ/kg·K** = Specific heat of dry air
- **1.88 kJ/kg·K** = Specific heat of water vapor

---

## 1.5 Winter vs Summer AC — The Seasonal Shift

> [!NOTE]
> **SUMMER AC (Cooling-Dominant):** Outside is hot & humid (e.g., 38°C, 60% RH). The system **removes both sensible AND latent heat** — cooling coil temperature drops below dew point → condensation occurs → water drips outside (that's why AC units drip water!).

> [!NOTE]
> **WINTER AC (Heating-Dominant):** Outside is cold & dry (e.g., 5°C, 30% RH). The system must **ADD heat and ADD moisture**. Heating coil raises temperature; humidifier adds water vapor. The combination prevents dry skin, static shocks, and respiratory irritation.

**Key Insight:** The *same hardware* can be used year-round by simply **reversing the flow** (Heat Pump Mode) and adding a humidifier — this is the basis of **Year-Round AC Systems**.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Room Air Conditioners (Window AC & Mini-Splits)

### 2.1.1 Window Air Conditioner — Detailed Architecture

A **Window AC** is a self-contained unit mounted in a window or wall opening. It integrates **all four major components** in a single chassis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A *Room Air Conditioner* is a factory-assembled, encased unitary system designed to deliver **conditioned air to a single room**, with a **cooling capacity typically ≤ 3 TR** for residential use.

**Components in a Window AC:**

| Component | Function | Typical Location |
|-----------|----------|------------------|
| Hermetic Compressor | Compresses refrigerant vapor | Bottom rear |
| Condenser Coil | Rejects heat to outdoors | Rear (outdoor side) |
| Evaporator Coil | Absorbs heat from room air | Front (indoor side) |
| Capillary Tube | Expansion device | Between condenser & evaporator |
| Blower (Evaporator fan) | Circulates room air over evaporator | Front |
| Propeller Fan (Condenser fan) | Pulls outdoor air through condenser | Rear |
| Filter | Removes dust | Front grille |
| Control Knob / Thermostat | Regulates temperature | Front panel |

**Capacity Range:** 0.75 TR to 2 TR (residential), up to 3 TR (commercial).

### 2.1.2 Split Air Conditioner — Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A *Split System* separates the noisy, heat-generating components (compressor + condenser) into an **Outdoor Unit (ODU)** while keeping the quiet, air-distributing parts (evaporator + blower) in an **Indoor Unit (IDU)** connected by refrigerant piping and electrical wiring.

**Why Split Systems Dominate Modern Residential Markets:**
- ✅ Quieter operation (compressor noise stays outdoors)
- ✅ Aesthetically superior (no bulky window unit)
- ✅ Higher EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio): **2.8 – 4.0**
- ✅ Inverter technology enables variable-speed compressor

**Interconnection between IDU and ODU:**
- **Suction line** (low pressure vapor, larger diameter, insulated)
- **Liquid line** (high pressure liquid, smaller diameter)
- **Control wiring** (electrical + communication)
- **Drain pipe** (condensate removal from IDU)

### 2.1.3 Packaged Air Conditioner (PTAC, Roof-top Units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A *Packaged AC* contains all refrigeration components, air-handling equipment, and controls in **one or two factory-assembled modules**, typically installed on rooftops, in mechanical rooms, or through walls. Capacities range from **3 TR to 30+ TR**.

**Configurations of Packaged AC:**

```
Configuration 1: Air-Cooled Packaged
  → Condenser fans reject heat to outdoor air
  
Configuration 2: Water-Cooled Packaged  
  → Cooling tower + condenser water loop
  
Configuration 3: Split-Packaged
  → Compressor-condenser section outdoors + Air handler indoors
```

**PTAC (Packaged Terminal Air Conditioner):**
- Common in hotel rooms (through-wall units)
- Self-contained, capacity 1–2 TR per unit
- Each guest room has independent control

---

## 2.2 Central Station Air Conditioning Systems

### 2.2.1 All-Air Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** An *All-Air System* uses **air as the sole medium** to transport heating or cooling energy from a central plant (AHU — Air Handling Unit) to conditioned spaces via a duct network. No water is circulated to individual rooms for cooling/heating.

**The Three Pillars of All-Air Systems:**

**(a) Single-Duct System**
- One supply duct serves all zones
- Reheat boxes at each zone for individual control
- Energy-inefficient (heating + cooling simultaneously in reheat)

**(b) Dual-Duct System**
- Two parallel ducts: **Hot Deck** + **Cold Deck**
- Mixing box at each zone blends to required temperature
- Better control but higher installation cost
- Common in **hospitals, laboratories, theaters**

**(c) Multi-Zone System**
- Single AHU with multiple cooling/heating coils
- Dampers modulate per-zone air mix
- Limited to 4–8 zones per AHU
- Common in **office buildings with 4–8 perimeter zones**

**KTU Formula for All-Air Systems:**

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \times c_p \times \Delta T$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{c_p \times \Delta T} = \frac{Q_s}{1.005 \times \Delta T}$$

$$Q_l = \dot{m}_a \times (W_1 - W_2) \times h_{fg}$$

Where:
- $Q_s$ = Sensible heat (kW)
- $Q_l$ = Latent heat (kW)
- $\dot{m}_a$ = Mass flow rate of dry air (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat of air = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (°C or K)
- $W_1, W_2$ = Humidity ratios (kg/kg)
- $h_{fg}$ = Latent heat ≈ **2501 kJ/kg**

**Total Cooling Load:**

$$Q_{total} = Q_s + Q_l = \dot{m}_a \times (h_1 - h_2)$$

Where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are enthalpies of entering and leaving air (kJ/kg dry air).

### 2.2.2 Chilled Water Systems (Air-Water Systems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A *Chilled Water System* uses **chilled water (typically 6°C to 12°C)** produced by a central chiller, circulated through pipes to **Fan Coil Units (FCU)** or **Air Handling Units (AHU)** located in conditioned zones. Air is the secondary medium; water is the primary energy carrier.

**System Architecture:**

```
Chiller → Chilled Water Pump → Supply Pipe (CHWS - 6°C)
   → FCU/AHU → Return Pipe (CHWR - 12°C) → Chiller
```

**Major Components:**
1. **Chiller** (Vapor compression or absorption): 50 TR – 5000+ TR
2. **Chilled Water Pumps** (primary + secondary loops)
3. **Expansion Tank** (accommodates volume changes)
4. **Three-way / Two-way Valves** (flow control at FCUs)
5. **FCU / AHU** (heat exchangers in conditioned spaces)
6. **Chemical Treatment** (corrosion inhibitors)

**Chilled Water Supply Temperature Standard:** **6°C** (with 12°C return → **ΔT = 6°C**)
- Modern high-efficiency systems use **8°C to 14°C** for chiller energy savings

### 2.2.3 Comparison Table (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Feature | All-Air System | Chilled Water (Air-Water) |
|---------|---------------|---------------------------|
| **Distribution Medium** | Air only | Chilled water + air |
| **Duct Size** | Large (carries cooling energy) | Smaller (water carries energy) |
| **Pipe Work** | None | Required |
| **Floor Space** | Large mechanical room + ducts | Smaller ducts, requires pipe shafts |
| **Best Suited For** | Large open spaces, theaters, auditoriums | Multi-floor buildings, hotels |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Lower (high fan power) | Higher (water has higher heat capacity) |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Higher initial, lower operating |

---

## 2.3 Winter Air Conditioning & Heating Systems

### 2.3.1 Winter AC — The Challenges

> [!NOTE]
> In winter, the outdoor air is **cold AND dry**. To maintain **inside conditions at ~22°C and 50% RH**, the system must:
> - **SENSIBLY HEAT** the cold incoming air (raise T from 5°C → 22°C)
> - **HUMIDIFY** the dry air (add moisture from low RH to ~50% RH)

**Mass flow rate of moisture required (humidification load):**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{m}_a \times (W_{inside} - W_{outside})$$

### 2.3.2 Heating Systems — Equipment Options

| Heating System | Energy Source | Typical Application |
|----------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Furnace (gas/oil)** | Combustion | Residential, small commercial |
| **Heat Pump (Air-Source)** | Reverse cycle refrigeration | Residential, mild climates |
| **Heat Pump (Water-Source)** | Ground/water loop | Commercial, cold climates |
| **Boiler + Hot Water Coil** | Gas/oil combustion → hot water | Large commercial, industrial |
| **Electric Resistance Heater** | Electrical energy | Supplementary, small spaces |
| **Heat Recovery (HRV)** | Waste heat from exhaust | Energy-efficient buildings |

**Heat Pump COP (Coefficient of Performance):**

$$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{heating}}{W_{input}} = \frac{\text{Desired heat output}}{\text{Electrical energy input}}$$

- For **COP = 3.0**, every 1 kW of electricity delivers 3 kW of heat
- Modern inverter heat pumps achieve **COP 4.0–5.0**

---

## 2.4 Humidifiers — Detailed Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A *Humidifier* is a device that **adds moisture** to air, increasing its humidity ratio ($W$) towards the desired setpoint.

| Type | Working Principle | Capacity Range |
|------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Wetted Media** | Air passes through water-saturated pad | Small residential |
| **Atomizing (Spray)** | High-pressure spray nozzle breaks water into fine mist | Commercial |
| **Steam Pan** | Heated water bath generates steam | Industrial |
| **Steam Injection (Dry Steam)** | External boiler injects steam into duct | Large commercial |
| **Ultrasonic** | High-frequency vibration creates fog | Specialty (clean rooms) |

**Steam Humidifier Steam Consumption Formula:**

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \frac{\dot{m}_a \times (W_{desired} - W_{supply})}{1000} \quad \text{(kg/hr)}$$

---

## 2.5 Year-Round Air Conditioning System (YACS) & Controls

### 2.5.1 Year-Round AC System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *Year-Round AC System* provides **conditioned air in all seasons** — winter (heating + humidification) and summer (cooling + dehumidification). It typically integrates:
> - Heat pump (refrigeration cycle reversible)
> - Humidifier (winter)
> - Dehumidification via reheat coil (summer)
> - Mixed air damper (economizer cycle)

**Schematic Block Flow (Mermaid will be in Section 4):**
```
Outdoor Air + Recirculated Air → Mixing Dampers
  → Filter → Preheat Coil → Cooling/Heating Coil
  → Humidifier (winter) → Supply Fan → Ductwork → Rooms
  → Return Air → Return Fan → Mixing Dampers (Loop)
```

### 2.5.2 Controls — Thermostat & Humidistat

**Thermostat:**
- Senses **dry-bulb temperature**
- Compares to setpoint (e.g., 22°C)
- Modulates cooling/heating capacity to maintain setpoint
- Modern versions: **Digital, programmable, Wi-Fi enabled**

**Humidistat:**
- Senses **relative humidity** (%RH) or **dew-point temperature**
- Activates humidifier (when RH too low in winter)
- Deactivates humidifier (when RH at setpoint)
- Typical winter setpoint: **35–45% RH** in residential; **40–55% RH** in commercial

**PID Control Loop:**

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where $e(t) = T_{set} - T_{actual}$ (error signal).

---

## 2.6 Air Distribution Systems & Duct Design

### 2.6.1 Duct Materials & Shapes

| Material | Shape | Use Case |
|----------|-------|----------|
| Galvanized Iron (GI) sheet | Rectangular | Main ducts (low pressure) |
| GI sheet | Round/spiral | Low-medium pressure |
| Aluminum | Round | Lightweight applications |
| Fiberglass-lined GI | Rectangular | Acoustical, clean rooms |
| Flexible (PVC + spring steel) | Round | Branch ducts, last 2–3 m to diffuser |
| FRP / Pre-insulated panels | Rectangular | Clean rooms, hospitals |

### 2.6.2 Duct Sizing — Equal Friction Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *Equal Friction Method* assumes **constant pressure drop per unit length** (typically **0.8 Pa/m to 1.2 Pa/m**) throughout the duct system. This is the **most common method used in practice** for low-velocity (≤ 10 m/s) duct design.

**Velocity Recommendations (ASHRAE):**

| Space Type | Main Duct (m/s) | Branch Duct (m/s) |
|------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Residences** | 3 – 5 | 2.5 – 4 |
| **Offices** | 5 – 8 | 4 – 6 |
| **Theaters** | 4 – 6 | 3 – 5 |
| **Industrial** | 7 – 10 | 5 – 8 |

**Duct Sizing Formulas:**

$$Q = A \times V$$

$$A = \frac{Q}{V}$$

For rectangular ducts: $A = W \times H$ (width × height)

$$D_{eq} = 1.30 \times \frac{(WH)^{0.625}}{(W+H)^{0.25}} \quad \text{(Equivalent diameter of rectangular duct)}$$

**Friction Loss (Darcy-Weisbach Equation):**

$$\Delta P_f = f \times \frac{L}{D} \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$

Where:
- $\Delta P_f$ = Friction pressure loss (Pa)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor (function of Reynolds number & roughness)
- $L$ = Duct length (m)
- $D$ = Hydraulic diameter (m)
- $\rho$ = Air density = **1.2 kg/m³** (at 20°C)
- $V$ = Air velocity (m/s)

**Hagen-Poiseuille (for laminar flow):**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{D^2}$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD — Memorize this table completely.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |

| 1 | $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ | Ton of refrigeration | kW |

| 5 | $\phi = \frac{h_a - h_{fwb}}{h_{sg} - h_{fwb}} \times 100$ | % Saturation from psychrometric reading | — |
| 6 | $Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat | kW |
| 7 | $Q_l = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta W \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat | kW |

**Critical Constants (Bold Memorize):**
- $c_{p,air}$ = **1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- $c_{p,vapor}$ = **1.88 kJ/kg·K**
- $h_{fg}$ at 0°C = **2501 kJ/kg**
- $\rho_{air}$ at 20°C = **1.2 kg/m³**
- Molecular weight ratio = **0.622**

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Coil Bypass Factor (BPF) — Foundation of AC Coil Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bypass Factor (BPF)** is one of the most critical concepts in KTU AC design. It quantifies how *inefficiently* a cooling coil contacts the passing air — some air bypasses the coil without being cooled to the coil surface temperature.

### Step 1: Define the Concept

When air passes through a cooling coil:
- Air in **direct contact** with coil surface is cooled to the coil's **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** temperature.
- Air that **bypasses** the coil stays at the inlet temperature.

### Step 2: Define Bypass Factor Mathematically

Let:
- $T_1$ = Dry-bulb temperature of air entering coil (°C)
- $T_{ADP}$ = Apparatus Dew Point (°C) — equal to average coil surface temperature
- $T_2$ = Dry-bulb temperature of air leaving coil (°C)

The temperature drop that **actually occurred:**

$$\Delta T_{actual} = T_1 - T_2$$

The temperature drop that **would have occurred if 100% contact** (ideal):

$$\Delta T_{ideal} = T_1 - T_{ADP}$$

The **Bypass Factor** is the fraction of air that did NOT contact the coil:

$$BPF = \frac{\Delta T_{actual}}{\Delta T_{ideal}} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$

### Step 3: Physical Interpretation

- **BPF = 0** → Perfect contact, $T_2 = T_{ADP}$ (theoretically ideal)
- **BPF = 1** → No contact, $T_2 = T_1$ (useless coil)
- **Typical BPF** → **0.05 to 0.40** (5% to 40% bypass)

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Given:** Air enters cooling coil at $T_1 = 30°C$, ADP = 12°C. Air leaves at $T_2 = 18°C$.
>
> **Find:** Bypass Factor.

**Solution:**

$$BPF = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} = \frac{30 - 18}{30 - 12} = \frac{12}{18} = 0.667$$

This means **66.7% of air bypassed** the coil — a poorly designed or fouled coil! A well-designed coil should achieve **BPF ≤ 0.20**.

### Step 5: Engineering Utility

Knowing BPF allows engineers to:
- ✅ Determine **sensible heat factor** from psychrometric chart
- ✅ Design cooling coils with proper face velocity (**2.5–3.5 m/s** for BPF ≈ 0.1)
- ✅ Calculate **Contact Factor (CF)** = $1 - BPF$
- ✅ Predict leaving air conditions from inlet conditions

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) and Room Heat Ratio (RHR)

### Step 1: Definition of SHF

The **Sensible Heat Factor** is the ratio of sensible to total heat load in a conditioned space:

$$SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t}$$

### Step 2: Definition of RHR (Room Heat Ratio)

The **Room Heat Ratio** accounts for the **return air** heat gain vs outdoor air heat gain. When outdoor air is added to the system (ventilation), the **grand total heat** is the sum of room load + outdoor air load.

$$RHR = \frac{Room \, Q_s}{Room \, Q_s + Outdoor \, Air \, Q_s} \quad \text{(Sensible portion)}$$

$$RHR_{total} = \frac{Room \, Q_t}{Room \, Q_t + Outdoor \, Air \, Q_t} \quad \text{(Total heat basis)}$$

### Step 3: Combined Coil Load & SHF

$$SHF_{coil} = \frac{Q_{s,room} + Q_{s,OA}}{Q_{s,room} + Q_{s,OA} + Q_{l,room} + Q_{l,OA}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

> **Given:** A room has sensible heat = 24 kW, latent heat = 6 kW. Outdoor air adds 4 kW sensible and 2 kW latent.
>
> **Find:** SHF of room, SHF of coil.

**Solution:**

Room:
$$SHF_{room} = \frac{24}{24 + 6} = \frac{24}{30} = 0.80$$

Coil:
$$SHF_{coil} = \frac{24 + 4}{24 + 4 + 6 + 2} = \frac{28}{36} = 0.778$$

**Interpretation:** The coil must handle 28 kW sensible + 8 kW latent = 36 kW total.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ADP (Apparatus Dew Point) from SHF

### Step 1: Graphical Construction

On the psychrometric chart:
1. Plot the **room air state point** ($T_{room}$, $W_{room}$)
2. Draw a line through this point with slope $= -\frac{c_p}{\Delta W / \Delta T} = -\frac{h_{fg}}{SHF/(1-SHF)} = $ based on SHF
3. The line ends at the **saturation curve** — this intersection is the **ADP**.

### Step 2: Mathematical Statement

$$SHF = \frac{h_{1} - h_2}{h_{1} - h_{ADP}} \times \frac{c_p \Delta T}{h_{1} - h_{ADP}} = \frac{c_p (T_1 - T_2)}{h_1 - h_{ADP}}$$

For an ADP that lies on the saturation curve, you can find:

$$ADP = T_1 - \frac{SHF}{1 - BPF} \times (T_1 - T_{ADP})$$

More commonly used:
$$SHF = \frac{c_p (T_1 - T_2)}{h_1 - h_2}$$

### Step 3: Use in Design

Knowing SHF and BPF, the designer can specify:
- **Number of coil rows** (4-row, 6-row, 8-row)
- **Face velocity** (m/s)
- **Coil entering/leaving water temperature** (chilled water side)

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Worked Problem: Cooling Load Calculation for a Restaurant

> **PROBLEM (KTU Exam Style):**
> 
> A restaurant in Trivandrum has the following data:
> - Dimensions: 20 m × 15 m × 3.5 m (height)
> - Seating capacity: 80 persons
> - Indoor design: 24°C, 55% RH
> - Outdoor design: 34°C, 70% RH
> - Glass window area: 30 m² (east-facing, double glass)
> - Lighting: 5 kW total
> - Equipment (kitchen exhaust hood): 8 kW total heat gain
> - Ventilation: 15 L/s per person (ASHRAE 62.1)
> - Air leakage: 1.5 air changes per hour (ACH)
> - Walls: R-value = 1.5 m²K/W, area = (2×20 + 2×15) × 3.5 - 30 = 175 - 30 = 145 m²
> - Roof: R-value = 2.5 m²K/W, area = 300 m²
> 
> **Determine:** (i) Total cooling load (kW), (ii) SHF, (iii) Air flow rate required (m³/s)

### SOLUTION (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):

#### Step 1: Sensible Heat from Walls

$$Q_{s,wall} = \frac{A \cdot \Delta T}{R} = \frac{145 \times (34 - 24)}{1.5} = \frac{145 \times 10}{1.5} = 966.67 \, W$$

#### Step 2: Sensible Heat from Roof

$$Q_{s,roof} = \frac{300 \times (34 - 24)}{2.5} = \frac{300 \times 10}{2.5} = 1200 \, W$$

#### Step 3: Sensible Heat from Glass (with solar radiation)

For east-facing glass, assume solar heat gain factor (SHGC) ≈ **0.4**, peak solar ≈ **400 W/m²** at noon:

$$Q_{s,glass,solar} = A \cdot SHGC \cdot I_{solar} = 30 \times 0.4 \times 400 = 4800 \, W$$

Conductive through glass (U-value ≈ 2.7 W/m²K):

$$Q_{s,glass,cond} = 30 \times 2.7 \times 10 = 810 \, W$$

Total glass:
$$Q_{s,glass} = 4800 + 810 = 5610 \, W$$

#### Step 4: Sensible Heat from Lighting

$$Q_{s,light} = 5000 \, W \text{ (assume all converted to heat)}$$

#### Step 5: Sensible Heat from People (Restaurants — moderate activity)

For **moderate activity in restaurant** (per ASHRAE): sensible heat per person ≈ **65 W** (total ~120 W per person with 55 W latent).

$$Q_{s,people} = 80 \times 65 = 5200 \, W$$

#### Step 6: Sensible Heat from Equipment

$$Q_{s,equip} = 8000 \, W$$

#### Step 7: Sensible Heat from Infiltration (Air Leakage)

Volume of restaurant:
$$V = 20 \times 15 \times 3.5 = 1050 \, m^3$$

Infiltration air flow rate:
$$Q_{inf} = 1.5 \times 1050 = 1575 \, m^3/hr = 0.4375 \, m^3/s$$

Mass flow:
$$\dot{m}_{inf} = 0.4375 \times 1.2 = 0.525 \, kg/s$$

$$Q_{s,inf} = \dot{m}_{inf} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.525 \times 1.005 \times 10 = 5.276 \, W$$

#### Step 8: Latent Heat from People

$$Q_{l,people} = 80 \times 55 = 4400 \, W$$

#### Step 9: Latent Heat from Infiltration (Outdoor 70% RH at 34°C → Indoor 55% RH at 24°C)

From psychrometric table:
- $W_{out}$ at 34°C, 70% RH ≈ **0.0229 kg/kg**
- $W_{in}$ at 24°C, 55% RH ≈ **0.0104 kg/kg**

$$\Delta W = 0.0229 - 0.0104 = 0.0125 \, kg/kg$$

$$Q_{l,inf} = \dot{m}_{inf} \cdot \Delta W \cdot h_{fg} = 0.525 \times 0.0125 \times 2501 = 16.41 \, W$$

#### Step 10: Total Cooling Load

Sensible Total:

$$Q_s = 966.67 + 1200 + 5610 + 5000 + 5200 + 8000 + 5.28 = 25,981.95 \, W \approx 25.98 \, kW$$

Latent Total:

$$Q_l = 4400 + 16.41 = 4416.41 \, W \approx 4.42 \, kW$$

Total Cooling Load:

$$Q_t = Q_s + Q_l = 25.98 + 4.42 = 30.40 \, kW$$

#### Step 11: Convert to Tons of Refrigeration

$$\text{Capacity in TR} = \frac{30.40}{3.517} = 8.64 \, TR$$

#### Step 12: Sensible Heat Factor

$$SHF = \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = \frac{25.98}{30.40} = 0.8546 \approx 0.85$$

#### Step 13: Air Flow Rate Required

For a typical supply air ΔT = 10°C (from 24°C → 14°C):

$$\dot{V} = \frac{Q_s}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{25,982}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 10} = 2.154 \, m^3/s$$

Or using supply air ΔT = 8°C:

$$\dot{V} = \frac{25,982}{1.2 \times 1005 \times 8} = 2.693 \, m^3/s$$

#### Step 14: Air Changes per Hour (Verification)

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{\dot{V} \times 3600}{V} = \frac{2.154 \times 3600}{1050} = 7.39 \, \text{ACH}$$

This is within **ASHRAE recommended 6–15 ACH for restaurants** ✅.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Selection Guide (KTU Viva)

| Building Type | Best AC System | Reason |
|---------------|---------------|--------|
| Single room (bedroom) | Window/Split AC | Simple, low cost, individual control |
| 10-room small office | Ducted split system | Centralized yet affordable |
| Hotel (200 rooms) | Chilled water + FCU | Each room independent, central plant |
| Theater (500 seats) | All-air, dual-duct | Centralized air, low noise |
| Hospital OT | Dedicated AHU + HEPA | Sterile, positive pressure |
| Server room | CRAC units (precision AC) | 24/7 operation, redundancy |
| Shopping mall | All-air + VAV | High heat density, varying loads |
| Factory (clean room) | Chilled water + FFU | Strict temp/RH control |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Heating Coil Capacity for Winter AC

> **PROBLEM:** Building requires 4 m³/s of outdoor air at outdoor conditions 5°C, 30% RH. Indoor setpoint: 22°C, 45% RH. Determine heating coil capacity.

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of dry air:

$$\dot{m}_a = \dot{V} \cdot \rho = 4 \times 1.2 = 4.8 \, kg/s$$

**Step 2:** Sensible heat from heating coil:

$$Q_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 4.8 \times 1.005 \times (22 - 5) = 82.01 \, kW$$

**Step 3:** Latent heat — none, since heating alone does not add moisture.

**Step 4:** Humidification requirement:

From psychrometric tables:
- $W_{out}$ at 5°C, 30% RH ≈ **0.0020 kg/kg**
- $W_{in}$ at 22°C, 45% RH ≈ **0.0073 kg/kg**

$$\Delta W = 0.0073 - 0.0020 = 0.0053 \, kg/kg$$

$$\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta W = 4.8 \times 0.0053 = 0.02544 \, kg/s = 91.6 \, kg/hr$$

**Conclusion:** Heating coil = **82 kW** + humidifier delivering **91.6 kg/hr steam**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP
